canonical.pattern: 1D linear DP (Fibonacci recurrence)
canonical.key_idea: Every arrival at step n comes from n-1 (one-step) or n-2 (two-step), and those two sets of paths are disjoint, so ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). It is Fibonacci with shifted base cases.
canonical.invariant: dp[i] = number of distinct step-sequences (order matters) that land exactly on step i using moves of 1 or 2.

progression.idea: Plain recursion f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-2). Correct, but the call tree recomputes the same subproblems exponentially many times (f(n-2) is computed inside both branches).
progression.complexity: O(2^n) time (tighter: O(phi^n)), O(n) stack space

progression.idea: Memoize the recursion or fill a table bottom-up: each subproblem solved exactly once. Insufficient only in that it stores all n states when the recurrence never looks back more than 2.
Keep just two rolling variables (prev, prev2) since dp[i] depends only on the last two states; update in a single pass.

traps: Base cases: dp[0] = 1 (the empty way to stand at ground) and dp[1] = 1; setting dp[0] = 0 shifts every answer down by one Fibonacci term.
This counts compositions, not partitions: 1+2 and 2+1 are different ways; saying 'combinations' in the walkthrough is a red flag.
Overflow: fib grows fast; 32-bit dies around n = 46, 64-bit around n = 92 - state which type you need or take a modulus.
When rolling variables, update order matters: compute cur before overwriting prev2, or you sum the wrong pair.
followups: Steps of size 1..k allowed? - dp[i] = sum of previous k states; maintain a sliding window sum for O(n) total instead of O(nk).
n up to 1e18 with answer mod 1e9+7? - Matrix exponentiation or fast doubling on the Fibonacci matrix, O(log n).
Some steps are broken and cannot be landed on? - Force dp[i] = 0 at broken steps, same recurrence elsewhere.
Each step has a cost and you want the cheapest way up? - Swap sum for min: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]) (Min Cost Climbing Stairs).
Why is the answer not C(n, #two-steps) summed directly? - It is: sum over j of C(n-j, j) equals Fib(n+1); the DP is just the cheaper way to evaluate it.
canonical.mutation: You may now take 1, 2, or 3 steps, but never two 3-steps in a row. Re-derive: what extra state do you need, and what is the new recurrence?

canonical.pattern: 1D linear DP with adjacency constraint (take/skip)
aliases: max independent set on a path
canonical.key_idea: At house i either skip it (best stays dp[i-1]) or rob it, which forces skipping i-1, giving a[i] + dp[i-2]; take the max. This is exactly max-weight independent set on a path graph, solvable greedily-left-to-right because the constraint only looks back one position.
canonical.invariant: dp[i] = maximum loot obtainable from the prefix houses[0..i] with no two adjacent houses robbed.

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n subsets of houses, discard any with two adjacent picks, track the best sum. Correct but exponential - the adjacency structure is never exploited.
Memoized recursion rob(i) = max(rob(i+1), a[i] + rob(i+2)): each index solved once. Only shortfall is O(n) memo plus recursion stack when two scalars suffice.
Bottom-up with two rolling variables: cur = max(prev, prev2 + a[i]); shift. Single pass, branchless inner loop.

traps: Base cases for n = 1 and n = 2 (dp[1] = max(a[0], a[1]), not a[1]).
Greedy 'rob every other house' is wrong: [2,1,1,2] gives 4 by robbing both ends, either alternating pattern gives 3.
Rolling-variable update order: overwrite prev2 with the old prev only after computing cur, or the recurrence silently reads the wrong state.
dp[i] is a prefix optimum, not 'best ending with a robbery at i' - conflating the two breaks reconstruction and variants.
followups: Houses arranged in a circle? - Case split: run the linear solve on [0..n-2] and on [1..n-1], answer is the max (House Robber II).
Houses on a binary tree? - Post-order DP returning a pair (best if robbed, best if not) per node (House Robber III).
Return which houses to rob? - Record the argmax choice at each i and backtrack from the end.
Constraint becomes 'no two robbed within distance k'? - dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], a[i] + maxPrefix(dp[0..i-k-1])); a running prefix max keeps it O(n).
Values can be negative? - Robbing nothing must remain legal; max with dp[i-1] already encodes that, but verify the empty-set answer 0 is allowed by the problem.
canonical.mutation: Robbing house i now trips a silent alarm that locks down houses i+1 AND i+2. Re-derive the recurrence - which lookback does the take-branch need now, and do the base cases change?

canonical.pattern: circular-constraint DP via case split into two linear passes
aliases: max independent set on a cycle
canonical.key_idea: The only thing the circle adds is that house 0 and house n-1 are now adjacent, so a valid plan omits at least one of them. Solve linear House Robber twice - on [0..n-2] and on [1..n-1] - and take the max; the case split makes the cycle disappear.
canonical.invariant: Within each pass, dp[i] = max loot from that segment's prefix ending at i with no two adjacent robbed; the wrap-around constraint is enforced entirely by which endpoint the pass excludes.

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n subsets with the circular adjacency check (including the 0/n-1 pair). Exponential; ignores that only one edge differs from the solved linear problem.

Carry an explicit boolean state 'was house 0 robbed' through a single DP around the circle and forbid robbing n-1 when it is set. Same asymptotics as optimal but doubles the state and is easy to get subtly wrong at the seam.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) space (2 states per index)

progression.idea: Two linear take/skip passes with rolling variables, one excluding the last house, one excluding the first; max of the two. Any valid circular selection omits house 0 or house n-1, so the two passes jointly cover the whole feasible set.

traps: n = 1 must short-circuit to nums[0]: both slices would otherwise drop the only house (or double-handle it, depending on slicing).
Slice off-by-one: exclude exactly one endpoint per pass ([0, n-2] and [1, n-1]), not both, not neither.
Do not add a third 'rob neither endpoint' case - both passes already contain it, and special-casing it invites bugs.
Reusing a linear helper that internally reads the full array instead of the passed bounds silently reintroduces the cycle bug.
followups: Prove the case split is exhaustive and sound. - Every valid set omits house 0 or house n-1 (they cannot coexist), and each pass returns the optimum over exactly one of those families; max covers the union.
Circle with 'no two robbed within distance k'? - Fix the take/skip decisions of the first k houses (constant number of cases for fixed k) and run the linear windowed DP for each case.
General graph version? - Max-weight independent set is NP-hard; polynomial only on special structure (paths, cycles, trees, bounded treewidth).
Recover the chosen houses? - Backtrack inside whichever pass won, then map slice indices back to original positions.
Cost of the split versus the stateful single pass? - Same O(n), but two clean passes have half the states and no seam logic - fewer bugs under interview pressure.
canonical.mutation: You must rob EXACTLY k houses on the circle (still no two adjacent), maximizing loot. Re-derive: what dimension gets added to the state, and does the two-pass split still work?

canonical.pattern: unbounded knapsack (minimum-count exact cover)
canonical.key_idea: dp[a] = fewest coins summing exactly to a = 1 + min over coins c of dp[a-c]. Equivalently it is BFS/shortest-path from amount 0 to the target on a DAG where each edge adds one coin - unweighted, so plain DP or BFS both work.
canonical.invariant: dp[a] = minimum number of coins whose values sum to exactly a, or INF if a is unreachable, finalized for all a' < a before a is computed.

progression.idea: Recurse: for the current remaining amount try every coin and take the min of 1 + solve(rem - c). Exponential because the same remaining amounts are re-solved along every path.

Top-down memoization over remaining amount: each of the amount+1 states solved once, trying k coins each. Works, but recursion depth can reach the amount itself - stack-overflow risk in C++ for amount around 1e4+.
progression.complexity: O(amount * k) time, O(amount) space plus O(amount) recursion stack

progression.idea: Bottom-up table over amounts 0..amount with an inner loop over coins (order of the two loops is irrelevant for MIN, unlike counting). Iterative, cache-friendly, no stack.

traps: Greedy biggest-coin-first is wrong: coins [1,3,4], amount 6 - greedy takes 4+1+1 = 3 coins, optimal is 3+3 = 2.
Initialize with amount+1 or a safe INF, never INT_MAX: INT_MAX + 1 in the transition is signed overflow (UB in C++).
Base case dp[0] = 0, and skip coins with c > a inside the loop; return -1 only after checking dp[amount] is still INF.
For MIN, loop order does not matter (reusing a coin is fine - it is unbounded); do not cargo-cult the descending loop from 0/1 knapsack here.
followups: Count the number of ways instead of the minimum? - Sum instead of min, and the coin loop must move OUTSIDE to count combinations once each (Coin Change II).
Each coin usable at most once? - 0/1 knapsack: per coin, iterate amounts descending so a coin cannot feed its own update.
Reconstruct the actual coins used? - Store the argmin coin per amount and walk back from the target subtracting choices.

Why does greedy work for [1,5,10,25] but not arbitrary systems? - Canonical coin systems satisfy the greedy-exchange property; arbitrary denominations do not, and [1,3,4] with 6 is the standard counterexample.

Amount up to 1e9 with few small coins? - The O(amount) table is infeasible; use shortest paths over residues modulo the smallest coin (Dijkstra on minCoin nodes) to characterize reachability and minimum counts.
canonical.mutation: Every denomination c_i now has a finite stock s_i (bounded knapsack). Re-derive: why does the plain unbounded transition break, and how do you keep the complexity near O(amount * k) (binary splitting or monotone deque) instead of O(amount * sum(s_i))?

canonical.pattern: unbounded knapsack combination counting (coin-outer loop order)
canonical.key_idea: Process coins one type at a time: for each coin c, dp[a] += dp[a-c] with amounts ascending. Fixing the order in which coin TYPES are introduced gives every multiset exactly one construction path, so you count combinations rather than ordered sequences.
canonical.invariant: After processing the first j coin types, dp[a] = number of distinct multisets of those j types summing to exactly a.

progression.idea: Recursively choose how many of each coin type to use (0, 1, 2, ... while it fits), multiplying out the choice tree. Exponential in the number of feasible multisets; no sharing of partial sums.
progression.complexity: O(prod over i of (amount/c_i + 1)) time, O(k) stack space

progression.idea: 2D table dp[j][a] = combinations forming a using the first j coin types, dp[j][a] = dp[j-1][a] + dp[j][a-c_j]. Correct and makes the combination-counting argument explicit, but the j dimension only ever reads the previous row.
progression.complexity: O(k * amount) time, O(k * amount) space

progression.idea: Roll the coin dimension away: single 1D array, coins in the OUTER loop, amounts ascending in the inner loop (ascending is what allows reusing the same coin type unboundedly).

traps: Swapping the loops (amount outer, coins inner) counts ordered sequences - permutations - which is a different problem (Combination Sum IV).
dp[0] = 1, the empty combination; setting it to 0 zeroes the entire table.
Counts overflow int fast; use unsigned/64-bit or apply the modulus if one is given - say this out loud in the interview.
Iterating amounts DESCENDING inside a coin would turn it into 0/1 (each type used at most once) - the direction of the inner loop is the unbounded/0-1 switch.
followups: Count ordered sequences instead of combinations? - Invert the nesting: for each amount, sum dp[a-c] over all coins (Combination Sum IV).
Why does coin-outer count each multiset exactly once? - It imposes a canonical order: all uses of coin 1 are committed before coin 2 exists, so each multiset has a unique build history.
Generating-function view? - The answer is the coefficient of x^amount in the product of 1/(1-x^c) over coins; the DP is that polynomial product evaluated incrementally.
At most m coins in total? - Add a used-count dimension dp[a][m], O(k * amount * m) time; or truncate the generating function by degree in a second variable.
Answers mod 1e9+7 with amount 1e5 and k 500? - Same 1D DP with a mod after each add; about 5e7 additions, comfortably fast in C++.
canonical.mutation: Each coin type may now be used at most TWICE. Re-derive: why does the ascending 1D update overcount, and what layered transition (dp_new[a] = dp_old[a] + dp_old[a-c] + dp_old[a-2c]) or loop restructuring fixes it?

canonical.pattern: LIS via patience sorting (tails array + binary search)
canonical.key_idea: Maintain tails[l] = the smallest value that can end an increasing subsequence of length l+1. Each new element replaces the first tail >= it (lower_bound) or extends the array; tails stays sorted, and its final length is the LIS length.
canonical.invariant: tails is strictly increasing, and tails[l] = the minimum possible tail value over all increasing subsequences of length l+1 in the prefix processed so far.

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n subsequences and keep the longest strictly increasing one. Hopeless past n around 25; ignores that only the last chosen value matters for extendability.
dp[i] = 1 + max dp[j] over j < i with a[j] < a[i]; answer is max over i. Each state summarizes 'best chain ending here', but scanning all j is quadratic - too slow at n = 1e5.

Patience/tails: binary-search each element into the tails array (lower_bound for strict increase). Replacing a tail with a smaller value never invalidates recorded lengths, it only improves future extendability. Equivalent alternative: Fenwick/segment tree keyed by value storing max chain length.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

traps: lower_bound vs upper_bound: strictly increasing needs lower_bound; switching to upper_bound silently allows duplicates (non-decreasing LIS).
tails is NOT an actual LIS - values get overwritten; reconstruction needs per-element (assigned length, predecessor) bookkeeping on the side.
In the O(n^2) version, dp[i] is 'LIS ending at i', so the answer is max over the array, not dp[n-1].
Duplicate values are the classic edge case: [2,2,2] has LIS 1 strictly, 3 non-decreasing - confirm which one the interviewer wants before coding.
followups: Reconstruct one LIS? - Record each element's assigned length and the index it chained from; walk back from any element holding the final length.
Longest non-decreasing subsequence? - Change lower_bound to upper_bound; nothing else moves.
Count the number of distinct LIS? - Fenwick tree over coordinate-compressed values storing (maxLen, ways) pairs; combine on prefix query, O(n log n).
Russian Doll Envelopes (2D LIS)? - Sort by width ascending, height DESCENDING among equal widths, then run 1D LIS on heights - the descending tiebreak prevents equal-width nesting.

Why is replacing a tail safe? - It only lowers tails[l], which preserves sortedness and can only make longer chains easier later; already-recorded lengths came from real subsequences and stay achieved.
canonical.mutation: Consecutive chosen elements must now be within index distance k of each other. Re-derive: why does the tails array collapse, and what structure (sliding-window max over dp values, via monotonic deque or segment tree over positions filtered by value) replaces it?

canonical.pattern: 2D grid DP over two sequence prefixes (match/drop)
canonical.key_idea: dp[i][j] = LCS length of the first i chars of s and first j chars of t: on a character match extend the diagonal by 1; otherwise drop the last char of one string and take the better of the two. Prefix pairs form a DAG with only three incoming edges per cell.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = length of the longest common subsequence of s[0..i) and t[0..j) (row 0 and column 0 are all zeros for the empty prefix).

progression.idea: Enumerate every subsequence of s (2^n of them) and check each against t with a linear scan. Ignores that only prefix pairs matter, not which characters were kept.
Full (n+1) x (m+1) table filled row by row. Optimal time for the standard approach; the only slack is storing all rows when the transition reads just the previous one.
Keep two rolling rows (or one row + a diagonal temp), orienting so the shorter string indexes the row. Same time, minimal memory; note it sacrifices trivial reconstruction.

traps: Index shift: dp[i][j] compares s[i-1] with t[j-1]; forgetting the -1 is the number-one LCS bug.

On a match take dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 only - also maxing with the left/up neighbors is redundant-but-correct for LCS, yet copying that habit into variants (e.g. weighted alignment) produces wrong answers, so learn the tight transition.
Rolling rows: cur must be fully overwritten (or cleared) each row, and reconstruction is impossible without the full table or Hirschberg.
Answer lives at dp[n][m], not the max over the table (it happens to be the max here, but relying on that breaks in variants like longest common substring).
followups: Print the LCS, not just its length? - Backtrack from dp[n][m]: on match emit the char and go diagonal, else step toward the larger of up/left.
Reconstruction with O(min(n,m)) space? - Hirschberg: split s in half, find t's optimal split point using one forward and one backward LCS row pass, recurse; still O(nm) time.
Longest common SUBSTRING instead? - Transition resets to 0 on mismatch and the answer is the max cell; or suffix automaton / suffix array in O(n+m).
Minimum deletions+insertions to transform s into t? - (n - LCS) deletions plus (m - LCS) insertions; edit distance without replace reduces to LCS.
Can you beat O(nm)? - Bit-parallel rows give O(nm/64); truly subquadratic in general would contradict SETH-based lower bounds, so no in practice.
canonical.mutation: Three input strings instead of two - find their common subsequence of maximum length. Re-derive the state, the transition (careful: matching only two of three does NOT let you take a diagonal), and the time/space, then say how you'd roll a dimension.

canonical.pattern: 2D grid DP over two prefixes (Levenshtein: insert/delete/replace)
canonical.key_idea: dp[i][j] = min operations converting the first i chars of a into the first j chars of b: on a match copy dp[i-1][j-1] free; otherwise 1 + min(delete = dp[i-1][j], insert = dp[i][j-1], replace = dp[i-1][j-1]). It is a shortest path on the prefix-pair grid DAG.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = minimum number of insert/delete/replace operations transforming a[0..i) into b[0..j).

progression.idea: Recurse on (i, j) trying all three operations at every mismatch with no memoization; the same prefix pairs are re-solved exponentially often.
Full (n+1) x (m+1) table, first row/column = j and i (build from / erase to empty). Optimal time; keeps all rows although only the previous row and the current diagonal are ever read.
Two rolling rows (shorter string across the row), remembering the diagonal cell before overwriting it. If you only need 'is distance <= k', band the DP to a 2k+1-wide diagonal strip instead.
progression.complexity: O(n*m) time, O(min(n,m)) space (banded check: O(k*min(n,m)) time)

traps: Base cases dp[i][0] = i and dp[0][j] = j - forgetting them (or zeroing them) makes every answer 0-ish and is the most common bug.
On a match, add NOTHING: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; adding 1 on match overcounts every aligned character.
Know which neighbor is which op (up = delete from a, left = insert into a, diagonal = replace); the min hides naming confusion until an interviewer asks you to recover the edit script.
Rolling rows: save prev[j-1] (the diagonal) before overwriting, and reset cur[0] = i at the start of every row.
followups: Only insert and delete allowed? - Distance = n + m - 2*LCS(a, b); replace is what separates Levenshtein from the LCS reduction.
Operations have different costs? - Same DP with weighted transitions; correctness is unchanged because it is still a shortest path on the same DAG.
Test edit distance <= k in less than O(nm)? - Ukkonen banding: only cells with |i-j| <= k can be <= k, so fill a 2k+1 diagonal band in O(k*min(n,m)).
Output the actual edit script? - Backtrack from dp[n][m] choosing any neighbor that reproduces the stored value; emit the op per step.
Distance exactly 1 (One Edit Distance)? - No DP: single linear scan, branch at the first mismatch on the length difference.
canonical.mutation: Add a fourth operation: swapping two ADJACENT characters costs 1 (transposition). Re-derive the transition - what extra lookback cell does it need, what guard condition on the characters, and why does the restricted version differ from true Damerau distance?

canonical.pattern: 1D boolean DP over prefix segmentability (dictionary segmentation)
canonical.key_idea: dp[i] = true iff the prefix s[0..i) splits entirely into dictionary words: dp[i] = OR over j of (dp[j] AND s[j..i) in dict). Every segmentation ends with some last word, and the DP tries all candidates for it.
canonical.invariant: dp[i] = true iff s[0..i) can be fully segmented into dictionary words (dp[0] = true: the empty prefix is segmented by the empty concatenation).

progression.idea: Recursively try every dictionary word (or every split point) at the current position with no memo. On adversarial inputs like s = 'aaaa...b' with dict {a, aa, aaa} the same suffixes are re-explored exponentially.
progression.complexity: O(2^n) time worst case, O(n) stack space

progression.idea: Memoize by start index (or fill dp bottom-up) checking all O(n^2) (j, i) pairs; each substring build/hash costs up to O(n). Correct, but ignores that no candidate word can be longer than the longest dictionary word.
progression.complexity: O(n^2 * n) = O(n^3) time with naive substrings (O(n^2) with precomputed rolling hashes), O(n) space plus the dictionary

progression.idea: Bottom-up dp with the inner split point restricted to the last L positions (L = max word length), dictionary in a hash set (or walk a reversed trie from i). Bounding by L is what makes long inputs cheap.
progression.complexity: O(n * L) substring checks = O(n * L^2) time with a string hash set, O(n * L) with a trie; O(n) space plus dictionary storage

traps: dp[0] = true is the seed; without it nothing ever turns true.
substr off-by-one: the candidate word is s.substr(j, i - j) - mixing the (start, length) and (start, end) conventions is the classic slip.
Skipping the max-word-length bound turns a 10^5-char input into an O(n^2)+ scan for no reason - state the bound explicitly.
C++ substr allocates: on hot paths use string_view or hash comparisons, or the hidden O(L) copies dominate.
followups: Return all valid sentences (Word Break II)? - Memoized DFS returning the list of segmentations per start index; note the output itself can be exponential, so complexity is output-sensitive.
Count the segmentations? - Replace OR with addition over the same transitions (mod if the count is large).
Minimum number of words? - Replace OR with min: dp[i] = 1 + min dp[j] over valid last words.
Huge text, fixed dictionary, many queries? - Build Aho-Corasick once; dp[i] becomes true if any dict word ends at i with dp[i - len] true, O(n + total dict length) per text.
Dictionary huge but s short? - Flip the loop: for each position iterate dictionary words that could end there via the trie, and compare which side (L vs dict size) is cheaper.
canonical.mutation: Each dictionary word may be used AT MOST ONCE across the whole segmentation. Re-derive: why does plain prefix DP break (state no longer just the index), and what state works for a small dictionary (index + used-word bitmask) versus why the general case is hard?

canonical.pattern: 0/1 knapsack subset-sum (boolean reachability to half the total)
canonical.key_idea: An equal partition exists iff some subset reaches total/2 (the complement then reaches it too). That is 0/1 subset-sum reachability: process each item once, updating reachable sums with the amount loop descending so an item cannot be reused.
canonical.invariant: After processing the first j items, dp[a] = true iff some subset of those j items sums to exactly a (dp[0] = true always).

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n subsets tracking sums. Correct, exponential, and blind to the fact that only which sums are reachable matters, not which subsets realize them.
progression.complexity: O(2^n) time (with incremental sums), O(n) space

progression.idea: 2D boolean table dp[j][a] = 'some subset of the first j items sums to a', dp[j][a] = dp[j-1][a] OR dp[j-1][a - v_j]. Pseudo-polynomial and correct, but stores n rows when each row depends only on the previous.
progression.complexity: O(n * S) time, O(n * S) space, where S = total/2

progression.idea: 1D boolean array with the inner amount loop DESCENDING per item (descending is what enforces use-at-most-once); in C++ compress to std::bitset and do reachable |= reachable << v_i for a 64x constant-factor win.
progression.complexity: O(n * S) time (O(n * S / 64) with bitset), O(S) space (O(S / 64) with bitset)

traps: Odd total sum: answer is immediately false - check before allocating anything.
Ascending inner loop with the 1D array lets an item feed its own update, silently converting 0/1 into unbounded knapsack - THE bug of this family.
dp[0] = true is the seed (empty subset); items larger than the target must be skipped, not force-subtracted.
Early-exit once dp[target] flips true; and remember the answer is reachability of exactly total/2, not 'closest to it'.
followups: Why is this called pseudo-polynomial? - O(n*S) is polynomial in the numeric VALUE S but exponential in its bit-length; subset-sum is NP-complete, so no truly polynomial algorithm is expected.
Minimize the difference between the two sides? - Same reachability table over 0..total/2; answer is min of total - 2a over reachable a (Last Stone Weight II).
Partition into k equal-sum subsets? - The 1D knapsack no longer suffices; bitmask-over-items DP in O(2^n * n) or pruned backtracking.
n <= 40 but values up to 1e15? - S is astronomically large, so meet-in-the-middle: enumerate both halves' subset sums (2^20 each), sort one, binary-search complements: O(2^(n/2) * n).
Count subsets hitting the target? - Same descending loop with 64-bit (or modular) counts and += instead of OR.
canonical.mutation: The two sides must ALSO contain the same NUMBER of elements (n even, exactly n/2 each). Re-derive: what dimension is added to the state, in which directions must the two inner loops run, and what are the new time/space bounds?

canonical.pattern: 0/1 knapsack subset-sum (count partitions)
canonical.key_idea: Splitting nums into a '+' set P and a '-' set N gives sum(P)-sum(N)=target and sum(P)+sum(N)=total, so sum(P)=(total+target)/2 - the problem collapses to counting subsets hitting one fixed sum.
canonical.invariant: After processing the first i numbers, dp[s] = number of subsets of those i numbers whose sum is exactly s (dp[0]=1 before any item).

progression.idea: DFS trying both signs at every index; correct but the same (index, running sum) states recur exponentially often with no reuse.
Memoize on (index, running sum + total offset) since the running sum lives in [-total, total]; works but keeps the sign framing and a 2*total-wide table per index.
progression.complexity: O(n * total) time, O(n * total) space

progression.idea: Transform to counting subsets summing to S=(total+target)/2 and run a 1D counting knapsack: for each num, for s from S down to num, dp[s] += dp[s-num].
progression.complexity: O(n * S) time, O(S) space, S=(total+target)/2

traps: If (total + target) is odd or total < |target|, the answer is 0 - check before dividing by 2
target can be negative; take |target| (P and N are symmetric) before the transform
The 1D loop must iterate s downward, or each number is reused like an unbounded knapsack
Zeros legitimately double the count (either sign works); the subset formulation handles this only because dp counts subsets containing or excluding each zero - do not filter zeros out
followups: What if nums could contain negatives - the transform's nonnegative bound dies; offset the sum axis or memoize with a hash map on (index, sum)
n <= 40 but values up to 1e9 - meet in the middle: enumerate 2^(n/2) signed sums per half, sort one half, count complements
Return one valid sign assignment instead of the count - walk the dp table backward from (n, S), taking whichever branch has a nonzero count
Why initialize dp[0]=1 and add rather than OR - this is a counting DP; boolean subset-sum answers reachability, not multiplicity
canonical.mutation: Each number may also be dropped entirely (three choices: +, -, skip). The subset transform breaks because sum(P)+sum(N) no longer equals total - re-derive as a DP over (index, reachable signed sum) with an offset, or as two coupled subset counts.

canonical.pattern: grid path-counting DP (lattice paths / closed-form binomial)
canonical.key_idea: Every path into (i,j) arrives from above or the left, so counts add: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Equivalently a path is an interleaving of (m-1) downs and (n-1) rights, giving C(m+n-2, m-1) directly.
canonical.invariant: dp[j] = number of monotone (right/down only) paths from (0,0) to column j of the row currently being processed.

progression.idea: Plain recursion paths(i,j) = paths(i-1,j) + paths(i,j-1); overlapping subproblems make it exponential without caching.
Tabulate row by row; each cell needs only the value above (still in dp[j]) and the value to the left (dp[j-1]), so one array suffices: dp[j] += dp[j-1].
Closed form C(m+n-2, m-1), computed incrementally as res = res * (m+n-2-k) / (k+1) in 64-bit - each partial result stays integral.

traps: The binomial overflows 32-bit almost immediately - use 64-bit and multiply-then-divide in the order that keeps intermediates integral
The entire first row and first column are 1, not 0 - zero-initializing them zeroes the whole table
In the rolling update, dp[j] must still hold the previous row's value when you add dp[j-1] - do not reset the array between rows
m = 1 or n = 1 must return 1 (a single forced path), a common base-case slip in the recursive form
followups: Add obstacles - same recurrence with blocked cells pinned to 0 (Unique Paths II); the closed form no longer applies
Allow a diagonal move too - dp gains a dp[i-1][j-1] term (Delannoy numbers); again no simple binomial
Huge m, n with answers mod 1e9+7 - precompute factorials and modular inverses, answer each query in O(1)
Why can't we just DFS-enumerate paths - the count itself is exponential in m+n; only counting, never enumeration, scales
canonical.mutation: Some cells are blocked. Re-derive: the additive recurrence survives with dp = 0 at obstacles, and if the grid is huge but obstacles are few, count paths by inclusion-exclusion over sorted obstacles using binomials (dp over obstacles in O(k^2)).

canonical.pattern: grid DP (min-cost monotone path on a DAG)
canonical.key_idea: Right/down moves make the grid an acyclic graph, so the cheapest way into (i,j) is grid[i][j] plus the cheaper of its two predecessors - no revisiting is possible, so plain DP replaces any shortest-path machinery.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = minimum total cost over all right/down paths from (0,0) to (i,j), including both endpoint cells.

progression.idea: Recursively try both moves from every cell and take the min over all complete paths; the path count C(m+n-2, m-1) is exponential.
Full m x n table dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]); keeps everything so the path can be reconstructed, but stores the whole grid again.
Row-rolling 1D array: dp[j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[j] from the row above, dp[j-1] from the left).

traps: First row and first column have exactly one predecessor - seed them as running prefix sums, never min() against a missing neighbor
dp[0][0] is grid[0][0], not 0 - dropping the start cell's cost is the most common wrong-answer here
A zero-initialized table makes absent neighbors look free through min(); use the correct bases or sentinel infinity
Solving in place mutates the caller's grid - acceptable in an interview only if you call it out
followups: Reconstruct the actual path - keep the 2D table and walk backward choosing the predecessor equal to dp[i][j] - grid[i][j]
Allow all four directions - the DAG dies; with nonnegative weights this is Dijkstra on the implicit grid, O(mn log(mn))
Negative cell values - still fine for right/down since every path has exactly m+n-1 cells; four-directional would need Bellman-Ford and may be unbounded
Maximize instead of minimize - swap min for max; the same DAG argument keeps it correct
canonical.mutation: You may now move in all four directions. Explain exactly why the recurrence breaks (cyclic dependencies: dp[i][j] can depend on dp[i+1][j] which depends back) and what replaces it - Dijkstra over cells for nonnegative weights, or 0-1 BFS when weights are binary.

canonical.pattern: linear prefix DP (gated Fibonacci / take-1-or-2 characters)
canonical.key_idea: The count for the first i characters depends only on whether the last one and last two characters form valid codes: dp[i] = [s[i-1] valid] * dp[i-1] + [s[i-2..i-1] in 10..26] * dp[i-2] - climbing stairs with validity gates.
canonical.invariant: dp[i] = number of complete decodings of the prefix of length i, with dp[0] = 1 for the empty prefix.

progression.idea: Branch on consuming one or two characters at each position; without caching, identical suffixes are re-decoded exponentially often.
Bottom-up table with dp[0] = 1: add dp[i-1] when the current digit is not '0', add dp[i-2] when the two-digit window is between 10 and 26.
Only the previous two values are ever read - carry two scalars and the last character.

traps: '0' decodes nothing alone - it must pair as '10' or '20'; a stranded '0' (e.g. '30', leading '0') zeroes the whole answer
dp[0] = 1, not 0 - otherwise '12' returns 1 instead of 2 because the two-char branch loses its base
The two-char window is 10..26 inclusive - '06' is invalid even though '6' alone is fine
Counts overflow 32-bit for long strings in variants with mod arithmetic - know when the interviewer expects mod 1e9+7
followups: Add '*' matching any digit 1-9 (Decode Ways II) - same two-term recurrence with per-case multiplicities (9, 15, 6, 2...) under mod 1e9+7

Return one actual decoding - committing to an arbitrary valid branch can dead-end (on '10', taking '1' alone strands the '0'; on '110', taking '11' strands it too), so either backtrack with DFS pruned by the dp counts (only enter suffixes with a nonzero count), or use one character of lookahead: whenever the next character is '0', the two-digit code is forced
Generalize to codes 1..k - the lookback window becomes the digit-length of k; the recurrence gains one term per extra digit
Digits arrive as a stream - the O(1)-space form is already online: keep two scalars plus the previous character
canonical.mutation: Codes now run 1..99 (any two-digit code without a leading zero is valid). Re-derive the gates: the 10..26 range check widens to 10..99, so the structure degenerates toward pure Fibonacci and the only remaining constraint is '0' placement - state exactly which inputs still return 0.

canonical.pattern: greedy farthest-reach sweep (monotone reachability)
canonical.key_idea: Reachability from index 0 is monotone: if i is reachable then so is every index below it. One left-to-right sweep maintaining the farthest reachable index therefore decides the whole problem - the DP is correct but strictly dominated.
canonical.invariant: After processing index i, far = the maximum index reachable using only jumps that launch from positions in [0, i].

progression.idea: DFS over every jump choice from every reachable index; branches multiply and states are revisited without memoization.
dp[i] = true if some j < i has dp[j] and j + nums[j] >= i; correct but re-scans all predecessors for every index.
Sweep i from 0: if i > far, return false; far = max(far, i + nums[i]); return true once far >= n-1. Monotone reachability makes the single scalar sufficient.

traps: Test i > far before extending far, or you extend the frontier from an index you never actually reached
n = 1 is trivially true even when nums[0] = 0 - you are already standing on the last index
Zeros are only fatal when no earlier position can leap strictly past them - special-casing zeros directly is a smell
Early-exit on far >= n-1; scanning past it is harmless but reading nums beyond reachable territory in variants can be a bug
followups: Minimum number of jumps - layered farthest-reach greedy in O(n) (the sequel problem)
From i you may jump exactly arr[i] left or right (Jump Game III) - monotonicity is gone; BFS/DFS with a visited set
Prove the greedy - invariant argument: far never decreases, and far >= i certifies reachability of every index up to far
Attach a cost to each jump and minimize total cost - reachability greedy no longer suffices; DP or Dijkstra over indices
canonical.mutation: nums[i] is now the exact jump length rather than a maximum. Monotone reachability collapses (you can overshoot gaps), so re-derive as reachability on an implicit graph - BFS from index 0 with a visited array, O(n) states.

canonical.pattern: greedy layered expansion (implicit BFS levels over jump ranges)
canonical.key_idea: All indices reachable in exactly k jumps form BFS level k. One sweep tracks the current level's right edge and the farthest the next level can reach; crossing the edge increments the jump count - BFS without a queue.
canonical.invariant: curEnd is the largest index reachable within 'jumps' jumps; farthest is the largest index reachable within jumps+1 - the window (curEnd, farthest] is exactly the next BFS frontier.

progression.idea: DFS enumerating every jump sequence and taking the minimum length; exponential and wildly redundant.
dp[i] = 1 + min(dp[j]) over all j with j + nums[j] >= i; correct but each index scans all its potential predecessors.
Single pass: farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]); when i == curEnd, do jumps++ and curEnd = farthest. Each index is visited once and levels are counted implicitly.

traps: Loop i < n-1, not i < n - standing on the last index must not trigger a spurious final jumps++
Update farthest before the i == curEnd boundary check, or the level cut uses a stale frontier
LeetCode guarantees reachability; in the general version detect a stall (i reaches curEnd with farthest == curEnd) and return -1
jumps counts level transitions from level 0 - initializing curEnd or jumps to 1 double-counts the first hop
followups: Return the jump sequence itself - record which index achieved each level's farthest and backtrack through the levels
Why is this BFS - each window (curEnd, farthest] is precisely the frontier of the next level in the implicit graph where i points to i+1..i+nums[i]
Weighted landings (cost per index), minimize total cost - level count no longer equals cost; Dijkstra or a monotone-deque DP
Jumps shrink or grow over time (nums[i] changes per jump number) - state must include the jump index; DP over (position, jumps) or BFS on the product graph
canonical.mutation: You may jump up to nums[i] in either direction. The one-directional frontier argument collapses - re-derive as BFS with a visited set, and to dodge O(n^2) edge expansion, expand each frontier over still-unvisited index intervals (ordered-set / union-find skip trick) for O(n log n).

canonical.pattern: 2D DP, min-of-three-neighbors (largest square anchored at bottom-right corner)
canonical.key_idea: A side-k square ending at (i,j) requires side-(k-1) squares ending at the top, left, and top-left neighbors, so for a '1' cell dp[i][j] = min(top, left, diag) + 1 - the weakest supporting square caps the growth.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = side length of the largest all-ones square whose bottom-right corner sits exactly at (i,j); the answer is the max over all cells, squared.

progression.idea: From every cell, grow candidate squares outward and validate each new ring (or validate every square via 2D prefix sums); revalidation repeats work across overlapping candidates.
progression.complexity: O(m*n*min(m,n)^2) time, O(1) extra space (O(m*n) with prefix sums)

progression.idea: Full DP table over the grid with the min-of-three recurrence; every cell is computed once from three finished neighbors.
Roll a single row, stashing the old top-left value in a scalar before overwriting - dp[j] (above), dp[j-1] (left), and the saved prev (diagonal) are all that is needed.

traps: Return side*side - forgetting to square the tracked maximum side is the classic finishing slip
Cells are chars '1'/'0' in the LeetCode signature - arithmetic directly on chars silently corrupts the table
First row/column cells have no three neighbors - their dp equals the cell itself; padding a zero row and column removes the branch
In the rolled version, save dp[j-1]'s pre-update value (the diagonal) before overwriting, or the recurrence reads the current row's value as last row's
followups: Maximal rectangle of 1s - the corner recurrence has no rectangle analogue; per-row histograms plus a monotonic stack give O(mn)
Largest square containing at most k zeros - 2D prefix sums of zeros plus binary search on the side length, O(mn log min(m,n))
Why min of the three - a square of side k needs all three side-(k-1) squares; whichever neighbor is smallest is the binding constraint, and equality is achievable
Rows arrive as a stream too large to store - the rolling-row form is already an online algorithm in O(n) memory
canonical.mutation: Find the largest all-ones rectangle instead of a square. A rectangle is not determined by one side value, so the min-of-three state carries too little information - re-derive: build a heights histogram per row and run largest-rectangle-in-histogram with a monotonic stack, O(mn) total.

canonical.pattern: expand-around-center (palindrome symmetry over 2n-1 centers)
canonical.key_idea: Every palindrome has a unique center - n character centers plus n-1 gap centers - and palindromes are closed under peeling both ends, so expanding outward from each of the 2n-1 centers finds the longest with no table at all.
canonical.invariant: For a fixed center the expansion loop maintains that s[l..r] is a palindrome and extends only while s[l-1] == s[r+1]; every palindromic substring is examined at exactly one of the 2n-1 centers.

progression.idea: Check every substring for palindromicity from scratch; each check is linear and nothing is shared between overlapping substrings.
Interval DP: isPal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and isPal[i+1][j-1], filled by increasing length - shares the inner-window answer but pays quadratic memory for it.
Expand around each of the 2n-1 centers with two pointers; same quadratic worst case (e.g. 'aaaa...') but constant memory and much better typical behavior.
Manacher: inside the current rightmost-reaching palindrome, initialize each new center's radius from its mirror, then extend - the right boundary only ever advances, amortizing to linear.

traps: Even-length palindromes live at gap centers - probing only the n character centers misses 'abba' entirely
The expansion loop exits one step past validity - converting the final (l, r) to start/length has a guaranteed off-by-one if taken verbatim
The DP table must be filled by increasing substring length (or i descending, j ascending) so isPal[i+1][j-1] exists before it is read
Substring, not subsequence - clarify before coding; the two problems have entirely different solutions
followups: Count all palindromic substrings - identical center machinery; sum the number of successful expansions instead of tracking the max
Longest palindromic subsequence instead - different problem entirely: interval DP on dp[i][j], O(n^2)
Shortest palindrome formed by prepending characters - longest palindromic prefix via the KMP failure function of s + '#' + reverse(s)
n up to 1e6 - Manacher's O(n); a rolling-hash + binary-search-on-length approach is a serviceable O(n log n) fallback
canonical.mutation: Allow up to k mismatched pairs inside the palindrome. Naive budgeted expansion is still Theta(n^2) worst case (on 'aaa...a' every center expands to full radius with zero mismatches, so the cost is the sum of radii regardless of k) - re-derive via the kangaroo method: preprocess suffix-array/hashing LCE in O(1) per query, then each center makes at most k+1 constant-time jumps (extend to the next mismatch, spend budget, repeat), O(nk) total.

canonical.pattern: expand-around-center counting (charge each palindrome to its unique center)
canonical.key_idea: Each palindromic occurrence has exactly one center among the 2n-1 candidates, so summing the number of successful expansions per center counts every palindromic substring exactly once - the longest-palindrome machinery with sum instead of max.
canonical.invariant: Every palindromic substring is counted exactly once, at its unique center; after processing a center, the running total equals the number of palindromes centered at or before it.

progression.idea: Test every substring for palindromicity independently; nothing shared across the O(n^2) substrings.
Interval DP table isPal[i][j] filled by increasing length; the answer is the number of true cells. Quadratic memory buys nothing over expansion here.
Expand around all 2n-1 centers, incrementing the count once per successful widening step.
Manacher: a center with radius r hosts exactly r palindromes (or its parity-appropriate share), so the answer is the sum of all odd radii plus all even radii.

traps: Occurrences count, not distinct contents - 'aaa' answers 6, not 3; confirm which one the interviewer wants before coding
Both center parities are required - dropping the n-1 gap centers silently loses every even-length palindrome
In the Manacher formulation the count is the sum of radii, not the number of centers with nonzero radius - each unit of radius is one more palindrome
The DP-table variant must count the length-1 diagonal - forgetting it undercounts by exactly n
followups: Count distinct palindromic substrings by content - Eertree (palindromic tree): one node per distinct palindrome, built online in O(n); a string has at most n distinct palindromes
Longest instead of count - same centers, track the max expansion instead of summing
Count palindromic subsequences - a different interval DP with end-character casework, not a center argument
Range queries: count palindromic substrings inside s[l..r] - needs heavy machinery (offline Eertree tricks); worth saying it is not an easy extension
canonical.mutation: Count distinct palindromic substrings, deduplicated by content. Center-summing overcounts repeats - re-derive with a palindromic tree where each node is one distinct palindrome and the answer is just the node count, or hash every palindrome seen at every expansion step (not just each center's maximal one - in 'xabay' the substring 'b' is maximal at no center, its center expands on to 'aba') into a set at O(n^2) as the pragmatic fallback.

canonical.pattern: interval DP on s[i..j] (match-ends-or-drop-one; LCS-with-reverse reduction)
canonical.key_idea: On window s[i..j]: if the ends match they contribute 2 plus the best strictly inside; otherwise drop one end and take the better window. Equivalently LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)).
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = length of the longest palindromic subsequence lying entirely within s[i..j]; dp[i][i] = 1 and empty windows read as 0.

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n subsequences and keep the longest palindrome - hopeless past n around 20.
Memoized recursion f(i,j) = s[i]==s[j] ? 2 + f(i+1,j-1) : max(f(i+1,j), f(i,j-1)); every (i,j) window solved once.
Bottom-up by increasing window length keeps only the previous row alive (dp over j for fixed i descending, plus one saved diagonal scalar).

traps: The i+1 > j-1 inner window (adjacent equal characters) must evaluate to 0 so 'aa' scores 2 - an unguarded table read returns garbage there
Fill order is i descending, j ascending (or by window length) - the natural double-ascending loop reads unfilled cells
Subsequence, not substring - 'bbbab' answers 4 via 'bbbb', which no substring achieves; misreading this sends you to the wrong algorithm family
The LCS(s, reverse(s)) shortcut gives the correct length, but an arbitrary LCS string need not itself be a palindrome - reconstruct from the interval DP, not from the LCS
followups: Minimum insertions to make s a palindrome - exactly n - LPS(s); each unmatched character needs one insertion
Minimum deletions to make s a palindrome - also n - LPS(s); insertions and deletions are symmetric here
Print one optimal palindrome - backtrack from dp[0][n-1], emitting matched end pairs outside-in and one center character if the length is odd
Count distinct palindromic subsequences - much harder interval DP with duplicate-end casework (LeetCode 730), worth naming not solving
n up to 1e5 - no general subquadratic LPS is known; expect special structure (small alphabet, bitset-accelerated LCS) or changed constraints
canonical.mutation: Each character now has a weight, possibly negative, and you maximize the total weight of a palindromic subsequence. Matched ends are no longer automatically taken - re-derive the transition so matching s[i]==s[j] adds w[i]+w[j] only when beneficial, and handle the single-center character (take it only if its weight helps).

items.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown

canonical.pattern: state-machine DP (hold / sold / rest states)
canonical.key_idea: Model each day as one of three financial states (holding a share, sold today, idle with cash) and take max-profit transitions between them; the cooldown is encoded by forbidding rest->buy on the day right after a sell.
canonical.invariant: hold[i] = max profit ending day i while holding a share; sold[i] = max profit having sold exactly on day i; rest[i] = max profit on day i holding nothing and not having sold today.

progression.idea: Recurse over (day, holding?) trying buy/sell/skip, jumping to day+2 after a sell to enforce cooldown. Correct but recomputes identical (day, holding) subproblems exponentially often.

Memoize that same recursion on the (day, holding) pair — only 2n states, each O(1) work. Insufficient only in the sense that it still carries O(n) memo + recursion overhead when a constant-space sweep exists.
Iterative sweep with three rolling variables: hold = max(hold, rest - price), sold = hold + price, rest = max(rest, prevSold). One pass, transitions read only yesterday's values.

traps: Cooldown means a buy on day i must come from rest[i-1], never sold[i-1] — equivalently the top-down version jumps i+2 after a sell; using i+1 silently drops the cooldown.
Initialization: hold = -prices[0] on day 0, sold = 0 (or -inf if you treat 'sold on day 0' as impossible), rest = 0; getting sold's seed wrong corrupts day 1.
In the rolling-variable version, update order matters: capture yesterday's sold before overwriting it, or hold will read today's sold.
Answer is max(sold, rest) at the end — returning hold (still owning stock) is a classic slip.
followups: Add a transaction fee — subtract the fee in the sell transition (sold = hold + price - fee); states unchanged.
Cooldown of k days instead of 1 — buy must read rest as of i-1 where rest only absorbs sold values that are ≥ k days old; keep a sliding window / delayed pointer into sold, still O(n).
No cooldown at all — states collapse and the answer is the greedy sum of all positive consecutive deltas.
At most K transactions with cooldown — add a transaction-count dimension: O(nK) time, states become hold[j]/sold[j]/rest[j].
Why not greedy here — cooldown makes local sell decisions non-exchangeable; a locally best sell can block a better buy tomorrow, so you need the state DP.
canonical.mutation: The exchange now imposes a cooldown after BUYING (you must wait one day before you are allowed to sell), and none after selling. Re-derive the states and transitions — which edge moves, and does the state count change?

items.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV

canonical.pattern: DP with transaction-count dimension (state-machine over k buy/sell pairs)
aliases: at most k disjoint intervals max profit
canonical.key_idea: Track best profit per (transactions used, holding?) and observe that the O(n) inner scan for 'best earlier buy' can be carried as a running maximum of dp[j-1][m] - prices[m], collapsing O(k n^2) to O(k n). When k >= n/2 the limit is vacuous and the problem degenerates to the unlimited-transaction greedy.
canonical.invariant: buy[j] = max profit while currently holding, having opened at most j transactions; sell[j] = max profit not holding with at most j completed transactions — both as of the current day, transactions counted at buy time.

progression.idea: Enumerate every choice of up to k disjoint [buy, sell] intervals and sum their deltas. Combinatorial explosion — the number of interval sets grows roughly as C(n, 2k).
dp[j][i] = max(dp[j][i-1], max over m<i of (dp[j-1][m] - prices[m]) + prices[i]). Correct, but re-scanning all m for every (j, i) is a full extra factor of n and TLEs at n = 1e3–1e5.

For each j sweep i once while maintaining bestBuy_j = max(bestBuy_j, dp[j-1][i] - prices[i]); then dp[j][i] = max(dp[j][i-1], bestBuy_j + prices[i]). First check k >= n/2 and if so return the greedy sum of positive deltas instead of allocating k rows.
progression.complexity: O(n·k) time (O(n) when k >= n/2), O(k) space with rolling rows

traps: Skipping the k >= n/2 shortcut: a profitable transaction needs 2 days, so larger k is meaningless — without the shortcut you allocate O(nk) for k up to 1e9 and blow memory/time.
Seed buy[j] with -infinity (or -prices[0] on day 0), not 0 — a zero seed lets phantom free shares leak profit.
Decide once whether a transaction is counted at buy or at sell and keep every transition consistent; mixing conventions makes dp[j-1] reference the wrong layer.
When rolling to 1D over j, iterate j from high to low within a day (or keep buy/sell as separate arrays updated in the right order) to avoid reading this day's already-updated lower layer.
followups: k = 2 — Stock III: four rolling scalars (buy1, sell1, buy2, sell2) in one pass; ask the candidate to write the transitions from memory.
Exactly k transactions instead of at most k — initialize unreachable states to -infinity and the answer may legitimately be negative or impossible.
Add a per-transaction fee — subtract fee in the sell (or buy) transition; the k >= n/2 shortcut becomes the fee-aware greedy.
Can you beat O(nk) — yes: a priority-queue / interval-merging technique (or WQS binary search on a penalty per transaction, exploiting concavity of profit in k) gives O(n log n).
Memory to O(k) — keep only current and previous transaction layers, or the buy[]/sell[] vectors of length k.
canonical.mutation: Profit in k is concave. Suppose k is up to 1e9 but n is up to 1e6 with the answer needed for EVERY k from 1..n/2 — re-derive: which structure (Lagrangian/WQS penalty on transaction count, or greedy delta-merging) lets you avoid n separate O(nk) runs?

canonical.pattern: running max/min pair DP (Kadane variant for products)
canonical.key_idea: A single running maximum is not enough because multiplying by a negative turns the smallest product into the largest; carry BOTH the max and min product of subarrays ending at the current index and update them jointly.
canonical.invariant: After processing index i, curMax and curMin are exactly the maximum and minimum products over all subarrays that END at i (never empty).

progression.idea: For every start index extend the product rightward, tracking the best seen. Simple and O(1) extra space, but the quadratic pair count is too slow once n reaches ~1e5.

One pass keeping curMax/curMin ending at i: candidates are a[i], curMax*a[i], curMin*a[i]; take max and min of the three (or swap curMax/curMin first when a[i] < 0). Answer is the global max of curMax.

Alternative: two sweeps (left-to-right and right-to-left) of running prefix products, resetting to 1 after a zero — works because the max-product subarray touches either the array's start-side or end-side of some zero-free block's negative split.

traps: Zeros: the max-with-a[i] candidate silently restarts the chain at 0 — forgetting a[i] as a standalone candidate breaks arrays like [0, 2].
Compute new curMax and new curMin from the OLD pair; overwriting curMax before computing curMin uses a half-updated state.
Initialize the answer to a[0], not 0 — all-negative arrays like [-3] must return -3.
Overflow: with wider constraints products exceed 64 bits fast; know that the standard trick is log-of-absolute-values plus sign parity, and say so before the interviewer asks.
followups: Return the subarray itself, not just the value — track start indices alongside curMax/curMin and record on improvement.
Max sum instead of product — plain Kadane; articulate exactly which property (negatives flipping extrema) forced the pair here.

Values are real numbers in (0, 1] mixed with values > 1 — negatives are gone but the min still matters? No: with all positives only the max chain matters, and fractions just shrink it, so it reduces to Kadane on logs.
Circular array version — either double the array with length cap or combine best-linear with total-product/complement reasoning; discuss why the zero case breaks naive wraparound.
Longest subarray with positive product — a different DP: track lengths of chains with positive/negative running sign.
canonical.mutation: Elements can be up to 1e18 in absolute value and n up to 1e5, so products overflow any integer type — re-derive a comparison scheme (log-magnitude + sign parity + zero segmentation, or count-of-negatives two-pointer per zero-free block) that never materializes the product.

canonical.pattern: tree DP (max-weight independent set on a tree, rob/skip pair per node)
canonical.key_idea: Post-order compute, for every node, a pair (best loot if this node is robbed, best if it is not); a robbed node forces both children into their not-robbed value, while a skipped node lets each child independently take its better option.
canonical.invariant: For every node v, the returned pair is (max loot within v's subtree given v IS robbed, max loot given v is NOT robbed) — children's choices already optimal under that constraint.

progression.idea: rob(node) = max(node.val + rob(four grandchildren), rob(two children)). Each subtree is re-solved through both its parent and grandparent paths, so identical subproblems multiply — exponential blowup on deep trees.
progression.complexity: O(2^h) time worst case, O(h) stack space

progression.idea: Same recursion memoized in a hashmap keyed by node pointer. Linear, but pays hashmap overhead and still expresses the grandchild recursion instead of the cleaner pair formulation.
Single post-order traversal returning the (robbed, skipped) pair: robbed = val + L.skipped + R.skipped; skipped = max(L.robbed, L.skipped) + max(R.robbed, R.skipped). No map, one visit per node.

traps: skipped is max of EACH child's two options summed — forcing children to be robbed when the parent is skipped (a common misread of 'alternate levels') is wrong: the optimum is not level-alternating.
Null child must return (0, 0); returning a sentinel like -inf poisons the sums.
Skewed trees make h = n — recursion can overflow the stack; know the explicit-stack post-order rewrite.
Memoize on node identity (pointer), never on node value — duplicate values collide.
followups: General n-ary tree — identical pair DP, robbed adds every child's skipped, skipped adds every child's max.
Same question on a general graph — that is maximum-weight independent set, NP-hard; the tree structure is exactly what makes this polynomial.
Return the actual set of robbed houses — keep the pair plus choice flags and backtrack from the root's argmax.
Iterative version — explicit stack with a visited/expanded marker or reverse-BFS order by depth.

Weights can be negative — robbed should be allowed to skip... no: robbing a node with negative value is never forced, but the pair DP already handles it since skipped is always an option at the parent; confirm max at root.
canonical.mutation: The alarm now links any two houses within tree-distance 2 (parent, child, AND sibling/grandparent conflicts). Re-derive the per-node state — two booleans no longer suffice; what does the state have to record about the child layer?

canonical.pattern: interval DP (last-operation framing)
canonical.key_idea: Fix the LAST balloon burst in an open interval (l, r): at that moment its neighbors are exactly the intact boundaries l and r, so the score is a[l]*a[k]*a[r] and the two sides decouple into independent subintervals. First-burst framing fails because bursting changes adjacency across the split.
canonical.invariant: dp[l][r] = maximum coins from bursting every balloon strictly inside the open interval (l, r), under the promise that balloons l and r are still intact throughout.

progression.idea: Try every burst order recursively on the remaining sequence. Orders are permutations, and the remaining-sequence state is not shared across branches.

Memoize on the bitmask of remaining balloons: neighbors are recoverable from the mask, so states collapse from n! to 2^n. Still hopeless at n = 300 — the mask space alone is astronomically over budget.

Pad both ends with 1. dp[l][r] over OPEN intervals: dp[l][r] = max over k in (l, r) of dp[l][k] + dp[k][r] + a[l]*a[k]*a[r], filled by increasing interval length. The last-burst choice is what makes dp[l][k] and dp[k][r] independent.

traps: Choosing the FIRST balloon to burst instead of the last — subproblems then share a mutating boundary and the recurrence is simply wrong; this is the whole insight, state it explicitly.
Forgetting the 1-padding, or letting k range over closed endpoints — a[l] and a[r] must be multipliers, never burst inside (l, r).
Fill order: iterate by interval LENGTH (or l descending, r ascending); a plain double loop over l, r reads unfilled cells.
Off-by-one between open-interval dp[l][r] and closed-interval dp[i][j] conventions — pick one and keep base cases consistent (adjacent indices give 0).
followups: Minimize instead of maximize — identical recurrence with min; nothing about the framing was max-specific.
Relation to Matrix Chain Multiplication — same last-operation skeleton; be able to map balloons to matrix split points.
Recover the optimal burst order — store the argmax k per (l, r) and emit it by post-order expansion (k is burst LAST within its interval).
Why doesn't greedy (burst smallest value first) work — construct a 3-element counterexample live.
Can O(n^3) be beaten — Knuth-style optimizations do not apply (no quadrangle inequality here); n <= 500 is the intended regime.
canonical.mutation: The reward for bursting k between intact neighbors i, j changes from a[i]*a[k]*a[j] to a[i] + a[k] + a[j]. Walk the derivation again: does last-burst independence still hold, does padding still work, and does the answer structure (which k wins) change qualitatively?

canonical.pattern: 2D boolean grid DP over two prefix pointers (third index implied by i+j)
canonical.key_idea: Whether s1's first i chars and s2's first j chars can weave into s3's first i+j chars depends only on (i, j) — the s3 pointer is forced to i+j. That collapses the search to reachability on an (m+1) x (n+1) grid where a right/down step consumes a matching char from s2/s1.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] is true iff s1[0..i) and s2[0..j) interleave to form exactly s3[0..i+j).

progression.idea: Recurse: at each step try taking the next char from s1 or from s2 whenever it equals the next char of s3. Both branches often match (duplicate chars), so the tree doubles repeatedly.
Memoize on (i, j) — only (m+1)(n+1) states because the s3 index is i+j, not free. Full table is fine but stores a row you never need again.
Tabulate with a single rolling row over the shorter string: dp[j] = (dp[j] and s1[i-1]==s3[i+j-1]) or (dp[j-1] and s2[j-1]==s3[i+j-1]). Same transitions, one row.

traps: Check |s1| + |s2| == |s3| up front — without it the dp indexes past s3 or quietly returns a wrong true.
The s3 char being matched at (i, j) is s3[i+j-1]; writing s3[k] with a separately-tracked k desynchronizes under memoization.
Base row/column must PROPAGATE: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] AND s1[i-1]==s3[i-1] — setting the whole edge true whenever prefixes 'mostly' match is a classic silent bug.

Greedy two-pointer (always consume from whichever string matches) fails when both match — e.g. s1="aa", s2="ab", s3="aaba": prefer-s1 greedy eats both s1 a's, then needs 'b' while s2 is stuck at 'a', yet a(s2) a(s1) b(s2) a(s1) works; have this counterexample ready.
followups: Count the number of distinct interleavings — same grid, booleans become integer counts (sum instead of OR), watch overflow.
Return one witness interleaving — backtrack from dp[m][n] preferring either edge; O(m+n) reconstruction.
Three source strings — 3D dp over (i, j, k) with the s4 index forced to i+j+k: O(n^3); for an unbounded number of strings the problem becomes NP-hard, so the dimension trick does not scale.

Streaming s3 — can you decide online? No: feasibility is not prefix-monotone per branch; you need the frontier (the reachable anti-diagonal set), which is exactly BFS over the grid diagonal by diagonal.
Reduce space below min(m, n)? Only the current anti-diagonal is live in the BFS view, so O(min(m, n)) is tight for exact feasibility.
canonical.mutation: Add the rule that no more than L consecutive characters may be taken from the same string. Re-derive the state: (i, j) alone no longer suffices — what must be appended, and what happens to time/space as a function of L?

canonical.pattern: 2D counting DP over prefix alignment (subsequence-embedding count)
canonical.key_idea: Count the ways s's first i chars contain t's first j chars as a subsequence: always inherit the count that skips s[i-1], and when s[i-1] == t[j-1] additionally add the count that uses it. Counting (sum) replaces the boolean OR of the matching version.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = number of distinct index-sets in s[0..i) that spell out t[0..j) exactly (dp[i][0] = 1: the empty target embeds one way).

progression.idea: Enumerate all subsequences of s and compare each against t. There are 2^|s| subsequences — dead on arrival past |s| ~ 25.
Memoized recursion on (i, j): skip s[i] always, use it when chars match. Linear in table size, but holds the full m x n memo.
Tabulate with one row indexed by j, sweeping j DOWNWARD per character of s so dp[j-1] still holds the previous-row value: dp[j] += dp[j-1] when s[i-1] == t[j-1].
progression.complexity: O(m·n) time, O(n) space where n = |t|

traps: In the 1D version, iterating j ascending double-counts — you would add the CURRENT row's dp[j-1]; descend, or keep two rows.
dp[*][0] = 1, not 0 — the empty pattern has exactly one embedding; zeroing it kills every count downstream.
Counts grow combinatorially: intermediate values overflow int32 even when the final answer fits — accumulate in unsigned/64-bit (or the required modulus).
Early exit j > i: a longer pattern than prefix can never embed; also lets you clamp the j loop to min(i, n) for a real constant-factor win.
followups: Count distinct subsequences OF s itself (no pattern) — different recurrence: double the count per char and subtract the overcount from that char's previous occurrence.
Answer modulo 1e9+7 — trivial to add, but discuss why subtraction-based variants need (+mod) guards.
Return the k-th embedding in lexicographic index order — walk the table greedily: commit s[i] if the use-branch count >= k, else subtract and skip.
t is tiny (|t| <= 10) but s is huge and streamed — keep only the length-(n+1) vector and update per incoming char in O(n): the 1D form IS the streaming algorithm.
Allow at most one mismatched character in the embedding — add an errors-used dimension e in {0, 1}: dp[i][j][e], twice the work.
canonical.mutation: t may now contain a wildcard '?' matching any single character, and you must count embeddings of t in s. Re-derive: which branch of the recurrence changes, and why does the answer now depend on multiplicities in a way that can explode the count's magnitude?

canonical.pattern: 2D boolean DP over (text prefix, pattern prefix) with star-closure transitions
canonical.key_idea: dp[i][j] answers 'does s[0..i) match p[0..j)'. A '*' never stands alone — treat (char, '*') as one unit that either vanishes (dp[i][j-2]) or, if its char matches s[i-1], consumes one more text character (dp[i-1][j]); plain chars and '.' just step diagonally.
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] is true iff the first i characters of s are matched COMPLETELY by the first j characters of p (whole-prefix match, not search).

progression.idea: Recursive backtracking: on x* try consuming 0, 1, 2, ... chars of s. Patterns like (a*)^k versus a^n a-mismatch drive it exponential — the same (i, j) pairs recur down every branch.
progression.complexity: O(2^(m+n)) time worst case, O(m+n) stack

progression.idea: Memoize (i, j) — the branching collapses onto (m+1)(n+1) states with O(1) transitions each. Fully sufficient; tabulation below is the same cost organized bottom-up.

Bottom-up table (or two rolling rows over j). Equivalent view: compile p to a Thompson NFA and simulate — the reachable-state set per text char is exactly one dp row; useful when the interviewer pushes toward 'real' regex engines.
progression.complexity: O(m·n) time, O(n) space with rolling rows

traps: Base row: dp[0][0] = true, and dp[0][j] = (p[j-1] == '*' and dp[0][j-2]) — patterns like a*b* must match empty; forgetting this fails immediately on s="", p="a*".
'*' binds to the PREVIOUS pattern char: index j-2 in the vanish branch; writing j-1 corrupts every star transition.
The consume branch of x* requires x to match s[i-1] (x == s[i-1] or x == '.') — gating only on the star is a silent over-accept.
'.' matches exactly one char, never empty; and this is full-match semantics — anchoring is implicit, don't add search-style slack.
followups: Wildcard Matching (LC44: '*' = any sequence, '?' = one char) — simpler recurrence, and a greedy two-pointer with backtrack marks achieves O(mn) worst / near-linear typical; contrast why THIS problem has no such greedy.
Add '+' (one or more) — x+ behaves like x followed by x*: the vanish branch becomes 'must consume at least once', dp[i][j] = matches(x, s[i-1]) and (dp[i-1][j] or dp[i-1][j-2]).
Why do production engines (RE2) use NFA/DFA instead of backtracking — backtracking is exponential (ReDoS); the dp/NFA guarantees O(mn).
Support alternation and groups (a|b), (ab)* — the flat prefix table dies; you need the NFA construction with epsilon transitions, still O(mn) in pattern states.
Return WHERE the match ends if p only needs to match a prefix of s — read off the last column: all i with dp[i][n] true.
canonical.mutation: Add character classes [abc] (match one char from the set) and '+' — re-derive the unit decomposition of the pattern: what is a 'unit' now, how do unit lengths vary, and how do the two star/plus branches index into j?

items.name: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets

canonical.pattern: bitmask DP over element subsets (bucket-filling with implicit bucket boundaries)
canonical.key_idea: With target = sum/k, process elements in any bucket-by-bucket order: dp[mask] stores (sum of mask) mod target for reachable masks — because every completed bucket contributes exactly target, the running remainder is the current bucket's fill, and bucket boundaries are implicit at remainder 0.
canonical.invariant: dp[mask] = (sum over elements of mask) mod target, defined only for masks reachable without ever overshooting target within a bucket — every complete bucket in a reachable mask sums to exactly target.

progression.idea: Assign each of n numbers to one of k buckets and check sums. k^n assignments with massive symmetry (bucket labels don't matter) — no shared state.

Backtracking that fills one bucket at a time: sort descending, place the largest unplaced number first, skip equal-valued siblings, and abort the whole branch if a number fails in an EMPTY bucket. Excellent in practice but still exponential worst case and hard to bound.
progression.complexity: exponential time worst case (no tight bound without a mask memo — the same used-set is re-explored once per bucket history), O(n) space

progression.idea: Bitmask DP: for each reachable mask with remainder r, extend by any unused element v with r + v <= target (masks that would overflow a bucket are simply never created). Feasible iff the full mask is reached; n <= 16 makes 2^n states affordable.

traps: Preconditions first: sum % k != 0 → false, and any single element > target → false; skipping these wastes the whole search or corrupts the dp.
The extension guard is r + nums[i] <= target — allowing equality is essential (that's how buckets close) but allowing overshoot makes remainders meaningless.

Backtracking dedup is two-fold: never try more than one currently-empty bucket for a given number, and skip sibling numbers of equal value after a failure — omit either and n = 16 adversarial inputs TLE.
The dp's correctness argument (order-independence of bucket filling) deserves one spoken sentence in an interview — many candidates code it right but can't defend why remainder-only state suffices.
followups: k = 2 — plain subset-sum knapsack: pseudo-polynomial O(n · sum) bitset, no bitmask needed; know when to downgrade.
Why can't this be polynomial in general — 3-PARTITION is strongly NP-hard, so even pseudo-polynomial is off the table when k scales with n.
Minimize the MAXIMUM bucket sum instead of forcing equality — binary search the cap + feasibility check, or dp over (mask) storing min-max; connects to job scheduling / LC 1723.
n up to 32 — 2^n dies; meet-in-the-middle helps subset-SUM but bucket structure breaks it, so you're back to pruned search with dominance and memo on normalized bucket multisets.
Elements arrive with multiplicities in the millions — switch state from element masks to counts per distinct value (polynomial in value-space, exponential no longer in n).
canonical.mutation: Each of the k subsets must ALSO contain exactly n/k elements. Re-derive: does dp[mask] = remainder still carry enough information, or must the state or the extension rule change to enforce the cardinality boundary at the same moment the sum boundary closes?

items.name: Minimum Insertion Steps to Make a String Palindrome

canonical.pattern: interval DP on (i, j) / reduction to n − LPS via LCS with the reverse
canonical.key_idea: Every character you keep inside a longest palindromic subsequence needs no partner; every other character needs exactly one inserted mirror. So the answer is n − LPS(s), and LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)) — or derive it directly as an interval DP on s[i..j].
canonical.invariant: dp[i][j] = minimum insertions (anywhere in the substring) needed to make s[i..j] a palindrome; base dp[i][j] = 0 whenever i >= j.

progression.idea: Recurse on (i, j): if s[i] == s[j] shrink both ends free, else pay 1 insertion and try dropping either end. Correct recurrence, but uncached it re-expands overlapping (i, j) pairs exponentially.
Memoize/tabulate that recurrence over all O(n^2) intervals: dp(i, j) = dp(i+1, j-1) if s[i]==s[j], else 1 + min(dp(i+1, j), dp(i, j-1)). Full table needed only if you also want reconstruction.

Compute LCS(s, reverse(s)) with two rolling rows and return n − LCS. Same asymptotic time, linear memory; the interval and LCS views are two derivations of the same quantity — be able to present both.

traps: Fill order for the interval table: by increasing length (or i descending, j ascending) — a naive row-major loop reads dp[i+1][j-1] before it exists.

The equal-chars case takes dp[i+1][j-1] with NO cost — adding the other branches under a min (1+dp(i+1,j), 1+dp(i,j-1)) is provably HARMLESS, just redundant: each candidate is a feasible strategy's cost (>= OPT) and dp[i+1][j-1] achieves OPT. The exchange argument shows matching ends always dominate, which is why the clean recurrence drops the min; know that argument.

LCS(s, rev(s)) = LPS(s) is a real theorem, not folklore — the naive 'common subsequence of s and its reverse is a palindrome' claim is FALSE for the subsequence itself in general; it's the LENGTH that's right. Be ready to state this precisely.
Dimension off-by-ones: mixing 0-indexed s with 1-indexed (n+1)-sized LCS tables is the top source of silent wrong answers here.
followups: Minimum DELETIONS to make a palindrome — identical answer n − LPS; explain why insertions and deletions are dual (each unmatched char is fixed by one op of either kind).
Mixed insertions and deletions, each cost 1 — still n − LPS; with unequal costs ci, cd it becomes min(ci, cd) per unmatched char, argue via the pairing.
Also allow REPLACE — the else-branch gains a 1 + dp(i+1, j-1) option and the LPS shortcut breaks; you must return to the interval DP.
Output an actual minimal palindrome — reconstruct by walking the interval table from (0, n-1), emitting mirrored chars on inserts.
n up to 1e5 — O(n^2) dies; discuss that no subquadratic exact algorithm is known (LCS-hardness under SETH) and what approximations/bit-parallel (Hunt–Szymanski, bitset LCS) buy you.
canonical.mutation: Insertions now have per-character costs c[ch] (inserting 'z' may be 50x inserting 'a'). Re-derive: the n − LPS shortcut collapses — why exactly — and what does the else-branch of the interval recurrence become when the two ends differ?

canonical.pattern: grid connected components (flood fill)
canonical.key_idea: Count connected components of land cells: scan the grid, and every time you hit an unvisited '1', increment the answer and sink its entire component with DFS/BFS so it is never counted again.
canonical.invariant: Every land cell is visited exactly once; the counter increments iff the outer scan reaches a land cell not yet claimed by any earlier traversal.

progression.idea: Iterative label propagation: give every land cell a unique label, repeatedly sweep the grid replacing each label with the min of its 4-neighbors until nothing changes, then count distinct labels. Correct but each sweep only moves labels one step, so a snake-shaped island needs O(mn) sweeps.

Union-find over cells: union each land cell with its right/down land neighbors in one pass, answer = number of land roots. Works and is the right tool for dynamic variants, but for a static grid it adds DSU machinery and the inverse-Ackermann factor for no benefit over plain traversal.

Single pass with DFS/BFS: on discovering an unvisited '1' from the outer double loop, bump the count and traverse the whole component, marking each cell visited (flip to '0' or use a visited array). Every cell is touched a constant number of times.
progression.complexity: O(mn) time, O(mn) space (visited + worst-case stack/queue)

traps: Recursive DFS on a 1000x1000 all-land grid recurses ~10^6 deep and smashes the stack in C++ (default ~1 MB); use BFS or an explicit stack.
