In BFS, mark visited at enqueue time, not at dequeue time - otherwise the same cell enters the queue many times and memory blows up.
Only 4-directional adjacency counts; adding diagonals silently merges islands and is a classic mis-read.
If you sink by mutating the input grid, confirm the interviewer allows destroying input; otherwise budget the O(mn) visited array.
followups: Grid too large for RAM, streamed row by row - keep union-find state for just the previous and current row, merging components and adjusting the count as rows arrive.
Land cells are added one at a time and you must report the count after each (Islands II) - online union-find: +1 for the new cell, -1 for each union with a distinct neighboring root.
Count DISTINCT island shapes - canonicalize each component (path signature of the traversal, or coordinates normalized to the component's top-left) and hash the signatures.
Return the size of the largest island instead - same traversal, track cells per component and take the max.
How would you parallelize it - partition the grid into blocks, solve each block locally, then merge components across block boundaries with union-find.
canonical.mutation: Queries addLand(r,c) arrive online on an initially all-water n x m grid and after each you must report the island count - your scan-and-sink approach recomputes everything; re-derive the incremental solution.

canonical.pattern: connected components on an adjacency matrix
canonical.key_idea: Cities and direct connections form an undirected graph given as an n x n adjacency matrix; a province is a connected component, so count how many traversals (or DSU roots) it takes to cover all nodes.
canonical.invariant: visited[i] == true iff city i's province has already been counted by some earlier traversal start.

progression.idea: Compute the full transitive closure with Floyd-Warshall-style boolean relaxation (reach[i][j] |= reach[i][k] && reach[k][j]), then group rows: two cities share a province iff their closure rows connect. Massive overkill - you only need components, not all-pairs reachability.

Union-find: for every pair (i,j) with isConnected[i][j]==1, union them; answer = number of distinct roots. Same asymptotics as DFS here (you must read the whole matrix anyway) but carries the alpha factor and extra code; it earns its keep only when edges arrive dynamically.

Plain DFS/BFS component count: for each unvisited city start a traversal that scans its matrix row for neighbors, marking visited; increment the count per start. Reading the matrix is Omega(n^2), so this is optimal.
progression.complexity: O(n^2) time, O(n) space beyond the input

traps: Neighbor discovery costs O(n) per node because the input is a matrix, so total is O(n^2) - quoting O(V+E) here is wrong and interviewers notice.
The diagonal is 1 (self-connection); a naive edge loop that unions i with i is harmless but treating it as a real edge in other logic can bite.
The matrix is symmetric - scanning only j > i halves work, but if you assume symmetry, say so; a directed variant breaks it.
Component count, not edge count: two cities can be in one province with isConnected[i][j]==0 via a chain.
followups: Connections arrive as a stream of (u,v) merges with interleaved count queries - union-find, maintaining the count as components - successful unions.
Minimum new connections to make one province - (number of provinces - 1), one edge per merge.
The matrix is too large for memory - store each row as a bitset on disk / process row-blocks with DSU; DSU state is only O(n).
What if connections are directed - provinces become strongly connected components; run Tarjan or Kosaraju in O(n^2) on the matrix.
Return the largest province size - track component sizes in DSU or count nodes per DFS.
canonical.mutation: isConnected becomes asymmetric (i trusts j does not imply j trusts i) and a province is now a maximal set where every city reaches every other - re-derive with strongly connected components instead of plain traversal.

canonical.pattern: multi-source BFS (level = elapsed time)
canonical.key_idea: Seed the queue with ALL initially rotten oranges at t=0 and BFS outward; each BFS level is one minute, so the last level at which a fresh orange rots is the answer, and any fresh orange never reached means -1.
canonical.invariant: When a fresh orange is infected (enqueued), its recorded minute equals the minimum over all initial rotten sources of the shortest 4-directional path length - BFS from multiple sources computes min-distance-to-nearest-source.

progression.idea: Simulate minute by minute: each minute, scan the whole grid, collect fresh oranges adjacent to rotten ones, flip them, repeat until a full scan changes nothing. A long corridor takes O(mn) minutes, each costing a full O(mn) scan.

Multi-source BFS: push every rotten cell with distance 0, count fresh oranges, then standard BFS where infecting a neighbor records dist+1 and decrements the fresh count. Answer is the max distance assigned if fresh count hits 0, else -1. Each cell enters the queue at most once.

traps: Zero fresh oranges at the start must return 0, not -1 and not the number of rotten levels - check before/after BFS.
Off-by-one on the answer: the initial rotten layer is minute 0; count levels only when at least one fresh orange actually rots.
Mark oranges rotten at enqueue time; marking at dequeue lets a cell be enqueued from two sides and can distort the fresh counter.
Track a fresh counter instead of rescanning the grid at the end - rescanning is correct but wasteful and easy to fumble under pressure.
followups: Why BFS and not DFS - DFS explores one path deep and cannot give simultaneous, minimum-time spread; BFS levels are exactly minutes.
Each source i starts rotting at its own delay t_i - push sources with initial distance t_i and use a priority queue (or bucket queue) since the frontier is no longer uniform.
Infection takes variable time per cell - edge weights appear, so multi-source Dijkstra (or 0-1 BFS if weights are 0/1).
New fresh oranges spawn at known times - process events and BFS frontier in time order with a priority queue merging both event types.
Same trick elsewhere - Walls and Gates / 01 Matrix are the identical multi-source pattern with distance instead of time.
canonical.mutation: Each rotten orange starts at its own time t_i and passing infection into cell c costs w(c) >= 1 minutes - uniform BFS levels no longer equal time; re-derive the algorithm.

canonical.pattern: single-source grid flood fill (region recolor)
canonical.key_idea: From the start pixel, traverse all 4-connected pixels that share the start pixel's ORIGINAL color and repaint them; record the original color before mutating anything.
canonical.invariant: A pixel ends up with the new color iff it is connected to the seed by a 4-directional path consisting entirely of pixels that originally had the seed's color.

progression.idea: Fixpoint sweeping: repaint the seed, then repeatedly sweep the whole image repainting any original-color pixel adjacent to a repainted one, until a sweep changes nothing. Each sweep grows the region by only one ring, so a long thin region forces O(mn) sweeps.
DFS or BFS from the seed: visit a neighbor iff it still holds the original color, repaint on visit (repainting doubles as the visited mark). Each pixel is processed once.
progression.complexity: O(mn) time, O(mn) worst-case stack/queue space

traps: newColor == originalColor causes infinite recursion because repainting no longer changes state - early-return on that case before traversing.
Read and store the original color BEFORE the first repaint; reading it after corrupts the traversal predicate.
Recursive DFS depth is O(mn) on a solid image - fine at 50x50 LeetCode scale, stack overflow at real image sizes; know the iterative rewrite.
Only pixels matching the original color are eligible - not everything adjacent, and not other regions of the same color elsewhere in the image.
followups: How does a real paint bucket implement this efficiently - scanline fill: fill a horizontal run at once, then scan the rows above/below for new runs; far fewer stack operations and cache-friendly.
Add tolerance (recolor colors within delta of the seed color) - identical traversal with the equality predicate replaced by |color - original| <= delta.
Support undo - record the list of (pixel, oldColor) pairs changed by each fill and replay in reverse.
Boundary fill vs flood fill - boundary fill spreads until hitting a border color rather than spreading over a target color; only the predicate changes.
8-directional connectivity - swap the 4-neighbor delta array for 8; note it changes which regions merge.
canonical.mutation: The image is 10^9 x 10^9 but only 10^5 pixels are non-background, given as a coordinate list - a grid traversal is impossible; re-derive using a hash-based adjacency structure.

canonical.pattern: multi-source BFS from all zeros (alt: two-sweep DP)
canonical.key_idea: Nearest-0 distance for every cell = one BFS seeded simultaneously with ALL zeros (invert the question: zeros expand outward). A two-sweep DP (top-left pass then bottom-right pass) computes the same in O(1) extra space.
canonical.invariant: BFS: a cell's distance is final the moment it is first enqueued. DP: after both sweeps, dist[i][j] = min over all four neighbor directions of neighbor+1 (each direction covered by one of the two passes).

progression.idea: For each cell containing 1, run its own BFS until a 0 is found. Correct, but a grid of mostly 1s makes each BFS O(mn), repeated mn times.
Multi-source BFS: enqueue every 0 with distance 0, set 1-cells to unvisited, expand; first arrival at a cell is its nearest-0 distance. Optimal time, but carries an O(mn) queue plus a distance grid.

Two-sweep DP in place: dist[i][j] = 0 for zeros; sweep top-left to bottom-right taking min(up, left)+1, then sweep bottom-right to top-left taking min(current, down+1, right+1). Why two passes suffice: grid steps commute and there are no obstacles, so any shortest path from a zero can be reordered into a prefix of down/right steps followed by a suffix of up/left steps; the forward pass computes the prefix distances and the backward pass, reading those results, completes the suffix - the passes compose rather than each covering whole paths alone.
progression.complexity: O(mn) time, O(1) extra space (answer stored in place)

traps: One DP sweep is NOT enough - the forward pass only sees up/left predecessors; forgetting the reverse pass is the classic wrong answer.
Initialize unknown cells to a large sentinel but small enough that sentinel+1 does not overflow int - use m*n or INT_MAX-1, never INT_MAX.
In BFS, seed only the zeros; enqueueing 1-cells too gives distance 0 everywhere.
Do not use the input values as the visited marker after you start writing distances into the same grid - 0/1 semantics get destroyed mid-BFS.
followups: Why does multi-source BFS give distance to the NEAREST zero - conceptually add a super-source wired to all zeros with weight-0 edges; BFS from it computes min over sources.
Diagonal moves allowed - distance becomes Chebyshev; same BFS with 8 neighbor deltas, DP needs extra sweep directions.
Only k arbitrary cells are queried - precompute is still O(mn); per-query BFS wins only when k * avg-search-area << mn.
Maximize distance to nearest land (LC 1162) - identical multi-source BFS, answer is the max distance assigned.
Cells have weights (cost to step) - BFS breaks; multi-source Dijkstra in O(mn log(mn)).
canonical.mutation: Steps right/down cost 1 but steps up/left cost 2 - BFS levels and the two-sweep DP both assume uniform cost; re-derive the algorithm and its complexity.

canonical.pattern: boundary-seeded flood fill (solve the complement)
canonical.key_idea: Instead of testing whether each region is surrounded, mark the complement: flood fill from every border 'O' to find cells that ESCAPE, then flip every unmarked 'O' to 'X' in one pass.
canonical.invariant: After the marking phase, a cell holds '#' iff it can reach the grid border through a 4-connected path of 'O' cells; exactly the non-'#' O-cells are surrounded.

progression.idea: For every 'O' cell, BFS its region and check whether the search ever touches the border; if not, flip the region. Without memoizing region membership across queries you re-traverse the same region from every one of its cells.

Label each 'O' region exactly once with a region id, recording for each id whether any of its cells lies on the border, then flip regions whose flag is false. Optimal asymptotically but needs id bookkeeping and two conceptual structures.

Seed DFS/BFS only from the O(m+n) border 'O' cells, marking reached cells with a sentinel '#'. Then one linear pass: '#' -> 'O' (escaped), remaining 'O' -> 'X' (captured). Same complexity as labeling but a single traversal and zero bookkeeping.
progression.complexity: O(mn) time, O(mn) worst-case stack/queue space

traps: Never flip cells during the discovery traversal - you need the sentinel/rewrite split, otherwise a region flipped early corrupts adjacency for regions still being explored.
Seed all four borders including the corners; iterating rows 0 and m-1 plus columns 0 and n-1 double-visits corners harmlessly, but skipping a border loses answers.
Recursive DFS depth can hit m*n on a big all-'O' board - iterative for production-size inputs.
Forgetting the final '#' -> 'O' restore leaves sentinels in the output - trivially wrong and embarrassingly common.
followups: Why attack the complement - 'surrounded' is a global property (no escape anywhere) while 'escapes' has O(m+n) natural sources; complement turns a per-region check into one multi-source traversal.
Count captured regions instead of flipping - run the same marking, then count connected components of remaining 'O'.
Board streamed row by row, too big for memory - union-find over two rows with one virtual 'border' node; regions still connected to the virtual node at the end escaped.
3D version (voxels, 6-adjacency) - identical algorithm seeded from all six faces.
Do it with union-find in one pass - union every 'O' with O-neighbors and border O-cells with a virtual root; flip cells not connected to the root.
canonical.mutation: A region also survives if it contains at least one shielded cell 'S', even when fully enclosed - re-derive the seeding so both escape routes are handled in one traversal.

canonical.pattern: reverse multi-source traversal from targets, intersect reachability
canonical.key_idea: Simulating water from every cell is quadratic; reverse the flow - climb FROM each ocean's border cells to neighbors with height >= current, producing two reachability masks, and output cells set in both.
canonical.invariant: A cell is marked in an ocean's set iff there exists a non-increasing height path from the cell to that ocean's border - equivalently a non-decreasing path from the ocean inward, which is what the reverse traversal explores.

progression.idea: From each cell, DFS downhill (neighbor height <= current) with memo-free exploration to test if it reaches the Pacific edge and the Atlantic edge. Each cell's search can cover the whole grid, and naive per-cell memoization is unsound because reachability is not a local property along one path.

Two multi-source traversals: seed the Pacific search with the top row and left column, the Atlantic with the bottom row and right column, and expand to any neighbor with height >= current cell (reverse of downhill flow). Each traversal visits each cell once; intersect the two visited sets.

traps: The comparison flips in reverse: expand to neighbors with height >= current. Writing <= (copying the forward rule) is the number-one bug here.
Equal heights DO flow - using strict > silently drops plateau paths.
Pacific touches TWO edges (top row and left column) and Atlantic the other two; seeding only one edge per ocean passes small tests and fails the rest.
Keep two independent visited arrays; sharing one poisons the second traversal.
followups: Why can't you memoize the brute-force per-cell DFS - a cell's reachability depends on paths through cells currently on the recursion stack, so cached partial answers can be wrong; the reverse traversal sidesteps cycles entirely.
Return one actual path for a qualifying cell - store a parent pointer during either traversal and walk it back.
k oceans / k target regions - k visited bitmasks, one traversal each; answer cells have all k bits set.
Count cells reaching Pacific OR Atlantic - union of the two masks instead of intersection; no new traversal.
Heights update at one cell - localized recompute is hard (reachability is global); discuss recomputing affected region vs full O(mn) redo.
canonical.mutation: Water can now also hop uphill by at most 1 unit (flows to neighbor if neighbor <= h+1) - re-derive the reversed edge condition and confirm the two-traversal structure still works.

canonical.pattern: directed cycle detection (topological sort feasibility)
canonical.key_idea: Courses and prerequisites form a directed graph; all courses can be finished iff the graph is a DAG. Detect a cycle with Kahn's algorithm (fewer than n nodes ever reach indegree 0) or DFS with three colors (a back edge to an in-progress node).
canonical.invariant: Kahn: a node enters the queue only when every prerequisite has already been popped; total popped == V iff the graph is acyclic. DFS: gray marks exactly the nodes on the current recursion path.

progression.idea: For each node, run a DFS/BFS asking whether the node can reach itself; any yes means a cycle. Correct but repeats a full graph search per node.

DFS with three states - white (unseen), gray (on the current recursion stack), black (finished). An edge into a gray node is a back edge, hence a cycle. Optimal time, but recursion depth is O(V): at 10^5+ nodes a linear chain can overflow the C++ stack, and the iterative rewrite with explicit state is fiddly under pressure.

Kahn's algorithm: compute indegrees, queue all indegree-0 nodes, repeatedly pop one, 'delete' it by decrementing successors' indegrees, enqueueing any that hit 0. Every node of a DAG is eventually popped; nodes trapped in or behind a cycle never reach indegree 0, so answer = (popped count == n). Fully iterative.

traps: Edge direction: pair [a,b] means b must come before a, so the edge is b -> a. Reversing it still detects cycles (a graph and its reverse share cycles) but breaks every ordering follow-up, so get it right now.
Two-state DFS (visited only) is wrong - a cross edge to an already-finished node is not a cycle; you need the gray/on-stack state.
Self-loop [x,x] is an instant cycle; make sure indegree/DFS handles it rather than filtering it away.
Duplicate prerequisite pairs are fine for Kahn only if you count each duplicate edge in indegree AND decrement once per stored edge - build both from the same edge list.
followups: Return an actual valid order - record nodes as Kahn pops them (that IS Course Schedule II).
Minimum number of semesters with unlimited courses per semester - BFS level count in Kahn's algorithm = longest path length + 1.
Which courses are involved in cycles - condense with Tarjan SCC; nodes in SCCs of size > 1 (or with a self-loop).
Prerequisites are added online, reject any addition creating a cycle - incremental cycle detection; simple answer is a reachability check from the edge head before inserting, O(V+E) per insert.
Kahn vs DFS in production - Kahn: iterative, no stack risk, gives levels for parallelism; DFS: gives reverse postorder and finds an explicit cycle path easily.
canonical.mutation: Each course i now has duration d_i, unlimited parallelism, and you need the minimum total time to complete all courses - re-derive using the same graph but a different quantity propagated through it.

canonical.pattern: topological ordering construction (Kahn / DFS postorder)
canonical.key_idea: Produce any linear order of courses where every prerequisite precedes its dependent, or empty if a cycle exists; Kahn's algorithm emits nodes as their indegree reaches zero, which is exactly such an order.
canonical.invariant: At the moment a course is appended to the output, every one of its prerequisites is already in the output (its indegree in the remaining graph was zero).

progression.idea: Repeated selection: scan all courses for one whose prerequisites are all satisfied, take it, mark done, repeat n times; if a full scan finds nothing before n courses are placed, there is a cycle. Each selection rescans everything.

DFS: run three-color DFS from every unvisited node, push each node when it FINISHES, reverse the push order at the end; abort on a back edge. Optimal time, but recursion depth risk at scale and the postorder-then-reverse subtlety is an easy fumble.

Kahn's algorithm: indegree array, queue of indegree-0 nodes, append each popped node directly to the answer while decrementing successors. The output IS the topological order in emission order, iteratively, and cycle detection falls out for free (result size < n).

traps: DFS variant: push on POSTORDER and reverse at the end - pushing on discovery (preorder) produces garbage that passes some tests.
Check result.size() == n before returning; a cycle silently truncates Kahn's output and returning the partial order is wrong.
Edge direction is prerequisite -> course (pair [a,b] gives b -> a); flipping it yields a reverse topological order that looks plausible and fails.
Many valid answers exist - do not waste time sorting or canonicalizing unless asked; judges accept any valid order.
followups: Lexicographically smallest valid order - replace Kahn's queue with a min-heap; O(E + V log V).
Minimum semesters with at most k courses per semester - level-by-level Kahn with capacity; for tight optimality with small n it becomes bitmask DP (LC 1494).
Count distinct valid orders - #P-hard in general; for n <= ~20, DP over subsets in O(2^n * n).
How do build systems (make, cargo) use this - dependency DAG topological order, with levels giving safe parallel batches; cycles reported as errors.
Detect and OUTPUT one cycle when infeasible - DFS with a parent/stack trace from the back edge, or peel indegree-0 nodes and DFS the remainder.
canonical.mutation: Each course's prerequisite list becomes OR-groups - finishing ANY one course in a group unlocks it - re-derive feasibility and order construction (plain indegree counting no longer models it).

canonical.pattern: graph 2-coloring (odd-cycle detection)
canonical.key_idea: A graph is bipartite iff it can be 2-colored with adjacent nodes differing, iff it has no odd cycle; greedily color each component from any start (BFS/DFS), and any edge joining two same-colored nodes disproves bipartiteness.
canonical.invariant: color[v] = parity of BFS distance from v's component start; the graph is bipartite iff every edge connects nodes of opposite parity - a violating edge closes an odd cycle.

progression.idea: Try all 2^V colorings and check every edge for each. Exists only to be dismissed - exponential and ignores that the coloring of a connected component is forced (up to swap) once one node is fixed.

Recursive DFS coloring: color node c, recurse into neighbors with 1-c, fail on a same-color edge. Optimal time and the same O(V) extra space as BFS - the real cost is stack-overflow risk: recursion depth O(V) means a 10^5-node path overflows the default C++ stack.

Iterative BFS coloring per component: color the start 0, neighbors get the opposite color, and any edge whose endpoints already share a color ends it. Equivalently, DSU with parity (union with relative parity 1, conflict if find shows equal parity) - which also handles online edge insertion.
progression.complexity: O(V+E) time, O(V) space (plus adjacency input)

traps: The graph may be disconnected - loop over all vertices and start a coloring in every uncolored component; checking only node 0 is the top failure.
Use a 3-value color array (-1 uncolored, 0, 1); with a 2-value array you cannot distinguish 'uncolored' from 'color 0'.
A self-loop is immediately non-bipartite (node adjacent to itself); handle before/inside the loop rather than assuming clean input.
Conflict check must fire when the neighbor is ALREADY colored the same - do not overwrite an existing color and lose the contradiction.
followups: Prove the odd-cycle equivalence - an odd cycle forces some adjacent pair to share parity; conversely BFS coloring fails only when an edge joins equal-depth-parity nodes, closing an odd cycle through their BFS-tree paths.
Edges arrive online with bipartiteness queries after each - DSU with parity: maintain parity to root, union with relative parity 1, conflict on equal parity within one set.
Graph is bipartite, now find maximum matching - Hopcroft-Karp in O(E * sqrt(V)).
Count valid 2-colorings - 2^(number of connected components) if bipartite, else 0.
Minimum edges to delete to make it bipartite - odd cycle transversal / max-cut flavored, NP-hard in general; planar and small cases have special algorithms.
canonical.mutation: Edges arrive one at a time and after each insertion you must answer whether the graph is still bipartite - restarting BFS per edge is O(E*(V+E)); re-derive an incremental structure.

canonical.pattern: graph deep copy via traversal + original->clone hash map
canonical.key_idea: Traverse once (DFS or BFS); keep a map original->clone and create the clone the moment a node is first discovered, BEFORE recursing/enqueuing, so cycles terminate. Neighbor wiring always goes through the map.
canonical.invariant: map contains u exactly when u has been discovered, and clone(u)'s neighbor list is completed exactly once, at u's own processing step.

progression.idea: Naive recursion clone(u) = new node whose neighbors are clone(v) for each v, with no memo. Any cycle (even u<->v, which this undirected graph guarantees for every edge) recurses forever. Even on a DAG-shaped view it is still wrong: a node reachable by multiple paths gets re-cloned once per path, so the output is the tree unfolding of the graph rather than a deep copy, and stacked diamonds make the node count (and runtime) exponential. Correct and linear only on trees.
progression.complexity: non-terminating on cycles; exponential time/output on DAGs with shared substructure; correct O(V+E) only on trees

progression.idea: Two passes: pass 1 BFS to discover all nodes and allocate a bare clone per node into a map; pass 2 re-walk edges and wire clone neighbor lists via the map. Works, but the second traversal buys nothing the map did not already enable.
progression.complexity: O(V+E) time, O(V) map + O(V) queue space, two full traversals

progression.idea: One-pass DFS/BFS: on first visit insert map[u]=new clone, then for each neighbor v create-if-absent and push clone(u)->neighbors.push_back(map[v]). Insertion-before-recursion is what breaks the cycle.

traps: Inserting into the map AFTER the recursive calls -> infinite recursion on any cycle; the insert must happen before neighbors are explored.
Null input graph: return nullptr, not an empty node.
Keying the map by node->val works only because vals are guaranteed unique; key by Node* to be safe under interview mutations.
Recursive DFS can blow the stack on a long path graph (n large); have the iterative BFS version ready.
followups: Iterative version without recursion? - BFS with the same map; clone at enqueue time.
How would you clone a linked list with random pointers? - identical map trick, or O(1)-space interleaving (insert clones between originals).
What if the graph is too big for one machine's memory? - serialize with stable ids (adjacency stream), rebuild by id; the map becomes an id->clone table shardable by hash.
How do you verify a deep copy in a test? - same structure via parallel BFS, plus assert no pointer in the clone equals any original pointer.
Directed graph, possibly disconnected, given a list of all nodes? - run the same traversal from every unvisited node; map is shared.
canonical.mutation: Nodes carry no unique labels and equality is pointer identity only, and edges are directed with some nodes unreachable from the entry node - you receive the entry pointer plus a count n. What can you clone, and what would you need to clone everything?

canonical.pattern: shortest path on an implicit graph via BFS (bidirectional BFS to optimize)
canonical.key_idea: Words are vertices; an edge joins words differing in exactly one position. Unit edge weights make BFS give the shortest ladder. Never build the graph explicitly - generate neighbors by trying 26 letters at each of L positions and testing membership in a hash set.
canonical.invariant: Every word is enqueued at most once, and when dequeued its recorded level equals the true minimum ladder length from beginWord (BFS layer = distance).

progression.idea: Materialize the graph: compare all word pairs for one-letter difference (O(L) per pair), then BFS. The pairwise build is quadratic in N and dominates; at N=5000, L=10 that is ~2.5e8 char ops plus O(N^2) adjacency memory.
progression.complexity: O(N^2 * L) time, O(N^2) space for edges

progression.idea: BFS with on-the-fly neighbor generation: dequeue w, for each of L positions substitute each of 26 letters, O(L) to build/hash each candidate, keep it if in the dictionary set and unseen. Erasing visited words from the set doubles as the visited check.
progression.complexity: O(N * 26 * L^2) time, O(N * L) space

progression.idea: Bidirectional BFS: expand alternately from beginWord and endWord, always growing the smaller frontier; stop when frontiers touch. Same worst-case bound but the b^d frontier growth becomes ~2*b^(d/2), typically 10-100x fewer expansions.
progression.complexity: O(N * 26 * L^2) worst case time, O(N * L) space, exponent halved in practice

traps: Mark visited at ENQUEUE time, not dequeue - marking late lets the same word enter the queue from many parents and blows the frontier up.
endWord absent from wordList -> return 0 immediately; beginWord is allowed to be absent.
The answer counts WORDS on the ladder, not edges - start the level at 1, not 0.
In bidirectional BFS, check frontier intersection when GENERATING a neighbor that lives in the other side's visited set, and always swap to expand the smaller frontier.
followups: Return all shortest ladders (Word Ladder II)? - BFS once recording per-word parent lists at the first level reached, then backtrack from endWord.
Edits get costs (vowel swap cheaper)? - weights break BFS; switch to Dijkstra over the same implicit graph.
Words of different lengths with insert/delete/substitute allowed? - neighbor generation changes; deletion-pattern buckets (w with one char removed) link words across lengths.
Why not DFS? - DFS finds a path, not the shortest; no layer structure.
Preprocessing many queries on one dictionary? - build wildcard buckets h*t -> [hot,hat,...] once, O(N*L) buckets, then each BFS skips candidate generation.
canonical.mutation: You may also swap any two adjacent letters as one move (in addition to single-letter substitution). What breaks in the neighbor generator, and does BFS remain correct?

canonical.pattern: single-source BFS on a grid with 8-directional moves
canonical.key_idea: Unit-cost moves on a grid mean plain BFS from (0,0) yields the shortest clear path; the only twists are 8 directions and counting cells rather than moves.
canonical.invariant: The queue holds cells of at most two consecutive distance values, so every cell is enqueued once with its true shortest distance from (0,0).

progression.idea: DFS/backtracking over all simple paths keeping the best length. Exponential blowup - the grid has exponentially many monotone-ish paths and DFS has no layer structure to prune with.
progression.complexity: exponential time (up to ~8^(n^2) branching), O(n^2) recursion space

progression.idea: Dijkstra with all edge weights 1. Correct, but the priority queue's log factor is pure overhead when a FIFO queue already dequeues in nondecreasing distance order.
progression.complexity: O(n^2 log n) time, O(n^2) space

progression.idea: BFS: seed (0,0) with distance 1, expand 8 neighbors, mark visited at enqueue. First time (n-1,n-1) is dequeued (or enqueued) its distance is the answer.

traps: Forgetting to check grid[0][0]==1 or grid[n-1][n-1]==1 -> must return -1 before any traversal.
1x1 grid with a 0 -> answer is 1 (path length counts cells, not steps).
Using 4 directions out of habit - this problem is 8-directional; diagonals are legal.
Marking visited at dequeue instead of enqueue -> duplicate queue entries, up to 8x memory and time waste.
followups: Speed it up for one s-t query? - A* with Chebyshev distance max(|dr|,|dc|) as the heuristic; admissible for 8-directional unit moves.
Cells get traversal costs? - Dijkstra; or 0-1 BFS with a deque if costs are only 0/1.
Many queries on a static grid? - one BFS gives all distances from a fixed source; for arbitrary pairs precompute nothing better than per-query A* in general grids.
Can you avoid the visited array? - overwrite grid cells with their distance (in-place), if mutation is allowed.
Nearest exit / multiple targets? - multi-source BFS: seed the queue with every target at distance 0 and read off the source.
canonical.mutation: You may convert at most one blocking 1 into a 0 along your way. Re-derive: what is the state space now, and what does visited mean?

canonical.pattern: single-source shortest paths with non-negative weights - Dijkstra (lazy-deletion heap)
canonical.key_idea: Answer = max over all nodes of dist[v] after SSSP from k (the last node to hear the signal), or -1 if any dist stays infinite. Non-negative weights license Dijkstra's greedy finalization.
canonical.invariant: When (d,u) is popped with d == dist[u], dist[u] is final - no future path can beat it because every alternative goes through a node with distance >= d and edges are non-negative.

progression.idea: Enumerate paths by DFS, tracking the best arrival per node. Exponentially many paths; revisits with worse distances are hard to prune correctly without becoming Dijkstra anyway.

Bellman-Ford: V-1 rounds relaxing every edge. Simple and handles negatives, but does Theta(V*E) work even when distances settle early; with V=100, E=6000 it is fine here yet loses badly on large sparse graphs.
Dijkstra with a binary min-heap and lazy deletion: pop (d,u), skip if d > dist[u] (stale), else relax out-edges and push improved (dist[v], v). Each edge relaxes once past finalization.
progression.complexity: O(E log E) time (log E ~ log V), O(V + E) space

traps: Skipping the staleness check (d > dist[u] -> continue) turns the heap loop quadratic-ish and can re-relax finalized nodes.
Nodes are 1-indexed - size arrays n+1 and iterate 1..n when taking the max.
Any unreachable node means return -1; do not return the max of reachable ones.
Relaxing with dist[u] == INF sentinel then adding w overflows int; guard before adding.
followups: Negative edge weights? - Dijkstra's finalization invariant breaks (a later negative edge can undercut a popped node); use Bellman-Ford O(V*E).
All-pairs delays for this n? - Floyd-Warshall O(V^3) = 1e6 ops at V=100; simpler than V Dijkstras.
Dense graph, E ~ V^2? - array-based Dijkstra picking the min unvisited node is O(V^2), beating the heap's O(V^2 log V).
Also return the actual route to the slowest node? - keep parent[v] updated on every successful relaxation, walk back from argmax.
decrease-key vs lazy deletion? - decrease-key (indexed heap) gives O(E log V) with no stale entries but is fiddly; lazy deletion is the interview-safe default.
canonical.mutation: Each node v additionally imposes a processing delay p[v] before forwarding the signal onward (source excluded). Where does p enter the relaxation, and does Dijkstra's correctness argument survive?

canonical.pattern: hop-constrained shortest path - Bellman-Ford limited to K+1 relaxation rounds (layered DP over edge count)
canonical.key_idea: Define cost[i][v] = cheapest way to reach v using at most i edges; K stops means at most K+1 edges, so run exactly K+1 edge-relaxation rounds, each reading from the previous round's snapshot. Plain Dijkstra with a visited set is WRONG here: a pricier-but-shorter-in-hops path may be the only one that can still reach the target.
canonical.invariant: After round i, cur[v] equals the minimum cost of any src->v path using at most i edges.

progression.idea: DFS from src tracking (cost, stops), pruning when stops exceed K or cost exceeds best. Exponential path count; pruning by cost alone cannot tame it because cheap prefixes with many hops multiply.
progression.complexity: O(V^(K+1)) time worst case, O(K) recursion space

progression.idea: Dijkstra on the expanded state (node, hopsUsed): pop cheapest state, expand only if hops <= K. Correct because states, not nodes, get finalized - but the state space is V*(K+1) and the heap carries the log.

Bellman-Ford, K+1 rounds: next[] starts as copy of cur[]; for each edge (u,v,w) relax next[v] = min(next[v], cur[u]+w); swap. Reading cur (last round's snapshot) is what caps paths at i edges after round i.

traps: Relaxing in-place (one array) lets a path absorb multiple new edges per round, silently answering 'at most K+? edges' - the snapshot copy is mandatory.
Off-by-one: K stops = K+1 edges = K+1 rounds; direct flight src->dst must survive K=0.
cur[u] == INF sentinel plus w overflows int; guard cur[u] != INF before relaxing.
Classic Dijkstra with a visited/finalized set fails - dominance here is 2-dimensional (cost AND hops), so a 'worse cost, fewer hops' state must stay alive.
followups: Drop the K constraint? - plain Dijkstra O(E log V); the whole layered machinery exists only because of the hop budget.
Why exactly does visited-set Dijkstra fail? - it finalizes a node at its cheapest cost, discarding a costlier path with fewer hops that is the only one able to continue within budget.
Return the itinerary too? - keep parent[i][v] = (u) per round, backtrack from (bestRound, dst); O(V*K) memory.
Negative prices (rebates)? - bounded Bellman-Ford still works: with at most K+1 edges, negative cycles cannot spin, so the DP stays well-defined.
Which wins, state-Dijkstra or bounded BF? - BF is O(K*E) with tiny constants and no heap; state-Dijkstra wins only when it can pop dst long before exploring the full layer structure.
canonical.mutation: Minimize stops FIRST, and among all minimum-stop routes minimize cost. Which algorithm and state ordering does the lexicographic objective force?

canonical.pattern: minimax (bottleneck) path - Dijkstra with max-edge relaxation on a grid
canonical.key_idea: The objective is min over paths of the MAX edge weight (edge = |height difference| between adjacent cells), not the sum. Dijkstra still applies verbatim if relaxation becomes cand = max(dist[u], w(u,v)) - the greedy finalization argument only needs the path objective to be monotone non-decreasing as edges are appended.
canonical.invariant: When a cell u is popped with d == dist[u], d is the minimum over all start->u paths of the maximum edge weight on the path - final by the same exchange argument as sum-Dijkstra, since appending an edge never decreases a path's max.

progression.idea: DFS/backtracking over all paths tracking the running max. Exponential path count with nothing to prune against early.
Binary search the answer t on [0, 1e6]: keep only edges with weight <= t and BFS from (0,0); feasibility is monotone in t, so binary search converges. Each probe is a full grid sweep.

Dijkstra where dist[v] = minimal achievable bottleneck to v; pop the global-min cell, relax neighbors with max(dist[u], |h[v]-h[u]|). Equivalent alternative: sort all edges, Kruskal-union until start and end connect.
progression.complexity: O(M*N log(M*N)) time, O(M*N) space (both variants)

traps: Writing dist[u] + w out of muscle memory instead of max(dist[u], w) - compiles, runs, wrong answers only on some inputs.
Single-cell grid: effort is 0 (empty path), not h[0][0].
Lazy-deletion staleness check still required; stale pops with the max-relaxation silently do extra work or corrupt dist if the check is missing.
Binary-search variant: probe with edges <= mid (inclusive), and search on edge-weight VALUES 0..1e6, not on cell heights.
followups: Why is Dijkstra still correct with max instead of +? - both are monotone, associative path compositions where extending a path cannot decrease its cost; the cut/exchange proof never uses additivity.

The union-find view? - sort edges ascending, union endpoints one by one; the first edge weight at which start and end share a root is the answer (bottleneck = max edge on the minimax path, which lies on any MST).
Maximize the minimum instead (widest path)? - flip to max-heap with min(dist[u], w) relaxation, dist init 0/infinity swapped.
Which of the three approaches for streaming/huge grids? - binary search + BFS uses O(1) extra per cell and no heap; best constant-memory profile.
Diagonal moves allowed? - only the neighbor generator changes; the pattern is untouched.
canonical.mutation: Minimize the SUM of |height differences| instead of the maximum. What single line changes, and does any of the three approaches (Dijkstra / binary search / union-find) stop working entirely?

canonical.pattern: minimax bottleneck on node values - Dijkstra with max(cell) relaxation / union-find over height-sorted cells
canonical.key_idea: At time t you can stand on any cell with value <= t, so the answer is the minimum over paths from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1) of the maximum CELL value on the path - a node-weighted bottleneck problem. Dijkstra with dist[v] = max(dist[u], grid[v]) or union-find adding cells in increasing height order both read it off directly.
canonical.invariant: When a cell is popped (Dijkstra) or when both corners first share a DSU root (union-find), the current threshold equals the minimal max-height over all paths reaching it - monotone max-composition finalizes greedily.

progression.idea: For t = 0,1,2,... run a fresh BFS over cells <= t until the corner is reachable. Correct because feasibility is monotone in t, but re-floods the grid up to n^2 times.
Binary search t over the n^2 distinct heights, BFS reachability per probe. Monotone feasibility makes the search sound; each probe is still a full sweep.
progression.complexity: O(n^2 log n) time, O(n^2) space

progression.idea: Dijkstra: dist[0][0] = grid[0][0]; relax dist[v] = max(dist[u], grid[v]); answer is dist at the far corner. Or exploit that heights are a permutation of 0..n^2-1: activate cells in height order, union with active neighbors, stop when corners connect.
progression.complexity: O(n^2 log n) time, O(n^2) space (Dijkstra heap or DSU + counting-sorted cells)

traps: Initializing dist[0][0] = 0 instead of grid[0][0] - you must wait for the start cell itself to submerge.
Relaxing with max(dist[u], |grid difference|) - this problem's weight is the destination CELL VALUE, not an edge difference (contrast with Path With Minimum Effort).
n = 1: answer is grid[0][0] with no moves.
Union-find variant: cells must be processed strictly in increasing height (heights are distinct, a permutation of 0..n^2-1 - use a position-of-height array, no comparison sort needed).
followups: Difference from Path With Minimum Effort? - weights live on nodes (absolute heights) vs edges (height deltas); relaxation swaps grid[v] for |h[v]-h[u]|, everything else is identical.
Why is binary search sound? - reachability under threshold t is monotone: raising t only adds cells, never removes them.
Return the actual route? - parent pointers during Dijkstra, or after DSU find the path with a BFS restricted to cells <= answer.
Can you beat O(n^2 log n)? - the DSU variant with counting-sorted heights is O(n^2 alpha(n^2)) since the permutation kills the sort.
k swimmers starting at k cells, all must reach the corner? - one union-find sweep answers all: record the threshold at which each start joins the corner's component.
canonical.mutation: Each move now also costs 1 second and you may NOT wait in place: you arrive at cell c at time = number of moves so far, and need grid[c] <= that time. Why does pure bottleneck reasoning collapse, and what state must the search carry?

items.name: Min Cost to Connect All Points

canonical.pattern: minimum spanning tree on a dense (complete) graph - array-based Prim
canonical.key_idea: Every pair of points is an edge weighted by Manhattan distance, so the graph is complete: E = V^2. On complete graphs the array-based Prim (no heap) is the right tool - O(V^2) time and O(V) memory, with no edge list ever materialized.
canonical.invariant: Cut property: at every step minDist[v] is the cheapest edge crossing the cut (tree, non-tree) that ends at v, so the global minimum over non-tree vertices is a safe edge belonging to some MST.

progression.idea: Generate all V(V-1)/2 edges, sort, run Kruskal with union-find. Correct, but at V=1000 that is ~500k edges to build, store, and sort - the sort dominates and the edge list is pure memory waste.
progression.complexity: O(V^2 log V) time, O(V^2) space

progression.idea: Heap-based Prim pushing all edges out of each newly added vertex. On a complete graph the heap swells to O(V^2) entries, so the log factor survives and memory is no better than Kruskal.
progression.complexity: O(V^2 log V) time, O(V^2) space

progression.idea: Dense Prim: keep minDist[v] = cheapest edge from the current tree to v. V times: scan for the unadded vertex with smallest minDist, add it, then relax minDist[w] against dist(v,w) for all unadded w. Two nested O(V) loops, distances computed on the fly.

traps: Manhattan |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|, not Euclidean - and no sqrt anywhere.

Total cost overflow: coordinates up to 1e6 in absolute value make a single edge up to 4e6; ~1000 edges can reach ~4e9 > INT_MAX. Accumulate in long long (LeetCode's constraints often sneak under 2^31, but do not gamble).

The constraints guarantee all points are distinct, so no zero-weight edges exist - but TIED distances are common under the Manhattan metric; never lean on 'all edge weights distinct' in a correctness argument.
Choosing heap-Prim or Kruskal 'by default' on a complete graph - the interviewer is testing whether you know density flips the tool choice.
followups: Kruskal vs Prim - when does each win? - Kruskal O(E log E) for sparse E ~ V; dense Prim O(V^2) for E ~ V^2; heap-Prim O(E log V) in between.
Can you beat O(V^2) for Manhattan MST specifically? - yes: L1 geometry admits an O(V log V) candidate-edge reduction (8-octant sweep with a BIT) feeding Kruskal; a classic 'know it exists' answer.
Prove the cut property in one line? - swap argument: any spanning tree not using the min cut edge gains it and drops another cut edge from the created cycle, never increasing cost.
Points arrive online, maintain MST? - hard in general; incremental insert is O(V) per point (new vertex's best edge + cycle fix), full dynamic MST needs link-cut machinery.

Is the MST unique here? - all pairwise distances distinct is SUFFICIENT for uniqueness but not necessary; Manhattan ties are common and MAY admit multiple MSTs (all of equal total cost), yet ties alone do not force non-uniqueness - e.g. collinear points (0,0),(1,0),(5,0),(6,0) have tied distances but a unique MST.
canonical.mutation: You get ONE free cable: you may pick any single pair of points and connect them at cost 0 before building the rest. Which pair do you pick, and what is the new optimum in terms of the original MST?

canonical.pattern: incremental union-find cycle detection (DSU with union by size + path compression)
canonical.key_idea: A tree plus exactly one extra edge has exactly one cycle. Process edges in input order through a DSU; the first edge whose endpoints already share a root closes that unique cycle - and because all other cycle edges preceded it, it is automatically the cycle edge that occurs LAST in the input, which is exactly the required tie-break.
canonical.invariant: After processing a prefix of edges (skipping none, since only the final answer fails), the DSU components are exactly the connected components of that prefix; find(u) == find(v) iff u,v already connected, iff adding (u,v) creates a cycle.

progression.idea: For each edge from the last to the first: delete it, DFS the remaining n-1 edges, and check connected + acyclic. Rebuilds and rescans the whole graph per candidate.
Single pass with DSU: for each (u,v), if find(u) == find(v) return it, else union. Union by size plus path compression makes each op effectively constant.

traps: Nodes are 1-indexed with values 1..n; size the parent array n+1.
Believing you must collect ALL cycle edges and pick the last - the first union failure IS the last cycle edge in input order; no second pass needed.
Path compression alone (or rank alone) gives O(log N) amortized, still fine, but state the alpha(N) bound only if you have both.
Do not pattern-match this onto Redundant Connection II - the directed version splits into in-degree-2 vs cycle cases and needs a genuinely different algorithm.
followups: Directed version? - Redundant Connection II: find a node with in-degree 2 (candidate edges) and/or a cycle; test removing the later candidate; DSU verifies. Materially harder - say so.
Could DFS replace DSU? - yes: before adding (u,v), DFS-check whether v is reachable from u; O(N) per edge, O(N^2) total - DSU is the one-pass win.
Edges also get DELETED over time? - DSU cannot un-union; go offline (process deletions in reverse as insertions) or use link-cut trees online.
Report the full cycle, not just one edge? - on the failing edge (u,v), walk the u->v tree path (parent pointers from a DFS of accepted edges); cycle = path + (u,v).
Roughly why alpha(N)? - union by size keeps trees O(log N) deep and compression flattens them; the interleaved analysis (Tarjan) yields inverse-Ackermann, effectively <= 4 for any feasible N.
canonical.mutation: Edges now carry weights, and you must delete a cycle edge that minimizes the remaining graph's total weight (still leaving a tree). Why does 'first union failure' stop being the answer, and what do you compute instead?

canonical.pattern: union-find over shared identifiers (connected components of emails)
canonical.key_idea: Two accounts belong to the same person iff they are linked by a chain of shared emails - a connectivity relation, so union-find. Map each email to the first account index that mentioned it; when a later account repeats an email, union the two account indices. Then bucket every email under find(owner) and sort each bucket.
canonical.invariant: Two account indices share a DSU root iff their email sets are connected by a chain of pairwise-overlapping accounts - exactly the problem's definition of 'same person'.

progression.idea: Repeatedly scan all account pairs, merging any two whose email sets intersect, until a full pass makes no merge. Each pass is quadratic in accounts with set-intersection cost, and chains of merges force multiple passes.
progression.complexity: O(A^2 * K * P) time (A accounts, K emails each, P passes), O(A*K) space

progression.idea: Explicit graph: nodes are emails, add an edge from each account's first email to every other email in that account, DFS/BFS to extract components. Correct and linear in edges, but allocates adjacency lists of strings just to encode connectivity.
progression.complexity: O(N*L) build + traversal, then O(N*L log N) output sort; O(N*L) space (N total emails, L max length)

progression.idea: DSU over account indices with an email->firstAccountIndex hash map: one pass to union, one pass to bucket emails by find(root), sort each bucket, prepend the root account's name. Sorting the output dominates asymptotically.
progression.complexity: O(N*L*alpha) union phase + O(N*L log N) sorting; O(N*L) space

traps: Same NAME never implies same person - two 'John's stay separate unless an email links them; the name is only recovered from any account in the final component.
Emails inside one merged group must be output sorted (and deduplicated - a set or sort+unique both work); forgetting the sort passes small tests and fails judging.
Bucket by find(root) at output time, not by the possibly-stale parent stored during the union phase.
Unioning account indices (ints) via the email map is cheaper than unioning email strings directly - string-keyed DSU parent maps cost a hash per hop.
followups: Accounts arrive as a stream? - DSU is naturally incremental: union on arrival, answer component queries any time; only the sorted output is deferred.
Detect conflicting names inside one component (data-quality flag)? - keep a name per root during union; on merge, if names differ record a conflict for that root.

Scale to billions of emails across machines? - distributed connected components: hash-partition emails, iterate label propagation / pointer-jumping rounds (a la map-reduce CC), alpha-style DSU does not shard directly.
What actually dominates the runtime? - the lexicographic sort of each component's emails, O(N*L log N) with L-length comparisons; the DSU phase is near-linear.
DFS-on-email-graph vs DSU? - same asymptotics; DSU avoids building adjacency lists and is shorter to write; DFS wins if you also need the linking structure.
canonical.mutation: Each (account, email) pair now has a timestamp, and two accounts may only be linked by an email if their uses of it are within 30 days of each other. Why does plain DSU over all-time data overmerge, and how do you restructure the computation?

items.name: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph

canonical.pattern: union-find connectivity counting (DSU)
canonical.key_idea: Start with n singleton components; every union of two vertices with different roots merges components and decrements the count. Equivalently, count DFS restarts over unvisited nodes.
canonical.invariant: At any point, find(x) == find(y) iff x and y are connected by the edges processed so far; count == number of distinct DSU roots.

progression.idea: For every pair (u,v), run BFS to test reachability, then group nodes into equivalence classes. Works but redoes the same traversal O(n) times over the same edges.
progression.complexity: O(n^2 * (n + e)) time, O(n + e) space

progression.idea: Build an adjacency list and flood-fill: iterate nodes 0..n-1, each time you hit an unvisited node increment the answer and DFS/BFS-mark its whole component. Insufficient only when edges arrive as a stream or you can't afford to materialize adjacency.
progression.complexity: O(n + e) time, O(n + e) space

progression.idea: DSU with path compression + union by size. Initialize count = n; for each edge, if find(u) != find(v), union and count--. No adjacency list needed, works online edge-by-edge.
progression.complexity: O(n + e * alpha(n)) time, O(n) space

traps: Isolated nodes never appear in the edge list — you must initialize count = n, not derive n from the edges.
Decrement count only when the two roots differ; blindly decrementing per edge undercounts on redundant edges.
Recursive find with path compression can blow the stack on a 10^5-node chain in C++/Python — write iterative find or rely on union-by-size keeping trees shallow.
If you go the DFS route, building adjacency from a directed reading of the edge pairs (forgetting to insert both directions) silently splits components.
followups: Report the component count after every edge insertion - DSU is naturally incremental, print count after each union.
Support edge deletions too - offline: process queries in reverse turning deletions into insertions; online needs link-cut trees or ETT.
Return the size of the largest component - store size at each root, update on union, track running max.
Minimum edges to add to make the graph connected - components - 1, provided you have that many spare edges (LC 1319 twist).
Graph given as an n x n adjacency matrix instead - traversal is Theta(n^2) regardless, so plain BFS is fine; DSU gains nothing.
canonical.mutation: Edges now arrive as an infinite stream interleaved with 'how many components?' queries. Your DFS solution recomputes from scratch per query — re-derive why DSU answers each query in O(1) after O(alpha) per edge, and what breaks if the stream can also delete edges.

canonical.pattern: tree characterization via union-find cycle detection
canonical.key_idea: An undirected graph on n nodes is a tree iff it has exactly n-1 edges and is acyclic (connectivity then comes free). Check the edge count, then union edges — any union whose endpoints already share a root proves a cycle.
canonical.invariant: With exactly n-1 edges processed and zero redundant unions, every union reduced the component count by 1, so components = n - (n-1) = 1 and no cycle exists.

progression.idea: DFS from node 0 tracking the parent to detect cycles (a visited neighbor that isn't the parent = cycle), then verify all n nodes were visited for connectivity. Correct, but the parent-skip trick is fragile: a duplicated edge like [0,1],[1,0] looks like the parent and hides a real multi-edge cycle.
progression.complexity: O(n + e) time, O(n + e) space

progression.idea: Early-exit if edges.size() != n-1. Then run DSU over the edges: if any edge joins two nodes with the same root, return false. If all n-1 unions succeed, the graph is acyclic with n-1 edges, hence connected, hence a tree. Handles multi-edges for free (second copy hits same-root).
progression.complexity: O(n + e * alpha(n)) time, O(n) space

traps: Checking only connectivity or only acyclicity — you need both unless you've pinned the edge count to n-1 first (either two of the three properties imply the third).
n == 1 with zero edges is a valid tree; an unguarded edges[0] access or count check can reject it.
Parent-skip DFS breaks on duplicate/parallel edges; skip the parent edge by index, not by node id, if duplicates are possible.
Fewer than n-1 edges means disconnected even if acyclic — don't return true just because no cycle was found.
followups: Directed version: is it a valid rooted tree - need exactly one node with in-degree 0, every other node in-degree exactly 1, and all n reachable from that root.
Do it without DSU - one DFS from node 0, return visitedCount == n && edges.size() == n-1.
Which edge should be removed to make it a tree - that's Redundant Connection (LC 684): the first edge whose union hits same-root.
Minimum edges to add/remove to turn an arbitrary graph into a tree - remove e - (n - c) redundant edges, add c - 1 connectors (c = components).
Edges stream in and you must say 'still possibly a tree?' after each - DSU answers incrementally; a same-root union is a permanent no.
canonical.mutation: The edges become directed and you're asked whether the graph is a valid rooted tree with an unknown root. The n-1 edge count still holds, but same-root DSU no longer suffices — re-derive using in-degrees plus reachability from the unique in-degree-0 candidate.

canonical.pattern: topological sort from pairwise ordering constraints (Kahn's BFS)
canonical.key_idea: Each adjacent word pair contributes at most one constraint: the first position where they differ gives edge earlierChar -> laterChar. Topologically sort the constraint graph; a cycle or an invalid prefix pair means no valid alphabet exists.
canonical.invariant: A character is dequeued only when its in-degree hits 0, i.e. every character constrained to precede it has already been emitted — so the output prefix is always consistent with all processed constraints.

progression.idea: Enumerate all permutations of the k distinct characters and test each against every adjacent word pair with a custom comparator. Correct but k! explodes past k ~ 10 and ignores that constraints are local and sparse.
progression.complexity: O(k! * C) time (C = total chars across words), O(k) space

progression.idea: Build the edge set from adjacent pairs, then DFS with 3-coloring (white/gray/black); a gray hit means cycle, otherwise reverse postorder is a valid alphabet. Works in linear time but the recursion + color bookkeeping is easier to fumble under pressure than Kahn's, and it doesn't extend cleanly to 'lexicographically smallest order'.
progression.complexity: O(C + k + E) time, O(k + E) space (E <= number of word pairs)

progression.idea: Kahn's BFS: compute in-degrees, seed the queue with in-degree-0 characters, repeatedly pop, append to output, and decrement neighbors' in-degrees, enqueueing those that reach 0. If output length < k, a cycle exists — return "".
progression.complexity: O(C + k + E) time, O(k + E) space

traps: Prefix violation: ["abc","ab"] yields no edge and must return "" immediately — the longer word sorting before its own prefix is unconditionally invalid.
Only the FIRST differing position between adjacent words carries information; deriving edges from later positions is wrong.
Characters that appear in words but in no constraint must still be in the output — collect all chars up front and count them into k.
Duplicate edges from multiple word pairs inflate in-degree; dedupe with a set or the node never reaches 0 and you falsely report a cycle.
followups: Return the lexicographically smallest valid alphabet - swap the queue for a min-heap in Kahn's; O(E + k log k).
Is the alphabet unique - yes iff the Kahn queue never holds more than one element at any pop.
Why compare only adjacent word pairs - sortedness is transitive; word[i] vs word[j] for j > i+1 adds no constraint not implied by the chain.
DFS vs Kahn's here - both linear; Kahn's detects cycles by output length and adapts to tie-breaking, DFS needs explicit gray-state detection.
Scale: alphabet is unicode, 10^5 symbols - nothing changes asymptotically; E stays <= number of adjacent pairs, one edge per pair.
canonical.mutation: Words now arrive online, one at a time, and after each arrival you must report whether at least one valid alphabet still exists. Recomputing Kahn's each time is O(n * (k + E)) — re-derive using incremental cycle detection (each new word adds at most one edge against its predecessor).

canonical.pattern: Eulerian path via Hierholzer's algorithm (lexicographic post-order)
canonical.key_idea: Using every ticket exactly once is an Eulerian path from JFK. Hierholzer's: from each node keep consuming the lexicographically smallest unused out-edge; append a node to the route only when it has no unused out-edges left, then reverse the appended list.
canonical.invariant: A node is emitted only when all its remaining out-edges have been consumed, so in the reversed output every node's onward suffix is a fully edge-exhausted walk — exactly the Eulerian path property.

progression.idea: Backtracking DFS: try destinations in sorted order, mark the ticket used, recurse, unmark on failure, succeed when path length == tickets + 1. Correct because the first found path is lexicographically smallest, but dead-end thrashing makes it exponential on adversarial inputs (repeatedly walking into a sink and unwinding).
progression.complexity: O(d^E) worst-case time (d = max out-degree), O(E) space

progression.idea: Hierholzer's with a min-heap (or sorted multiset) of destinations per airport: iteratively walk greedily; when stuck at a node with no remaining out-edges, emit it (post-order) and backtrack one frame. Emitted sequence reversed is the itinerary — dead ends get spliced into the route instead of being retried.
progression.complexity: O(E log E) time (heap pops), O(E) space

traps: Pure greedy without post-order fails: from JFK, tickets JFK->KUL, JFK->NRT, NRT->JFK — taking lex-smaller KUL first strands the NRT tickets; Hierholzer fixes this structurally, not by luck.
Duplicate tickets exist — store destinations in a multiset/heap/counted map, never a plain set.
You must start at "JFK" even when the degree conditions would put the natural Eulerian start elsewhere; the problem guarantees feasibility from JFK.
Forgetting the final reverse, or recursing 300+ deep in a language with a tight stack — the iterative explicit-stack version avoids both.
followups: What guarantees an Eulerian path exists in a directed graph - at most one node with out-in = +1 (start), one with in-out = +1 (end), all others balanced, and all edges in one connected piece.
The input might be infeasible - verify those degree conditions plus connectivity first and return empty instead of crashing mid-walk.
Why the heap per node - each edge is consumed exactly once and popping the smallest is O(log d), giving the lexicographic-smallest itinerary for free.
Convert your recursion to iteration - explicit stack; emit (push to route) when the top node's heap is empty, else push its popped smallest neighbor.
Difference vs Eulerian circuit - circuit needs all nodes balanced and you'd end where you started; the JFK constraint would then be just a rotation.
canonical.mutation: Tickets are now optional: find the lexicographically smallest route that visits every AIRPORT (node) at least once, using any subset of tickets. Edge-exhaustion (Eulerian) reasoning collapses — this is a node-cover walk, NP-hard in general — so re-derive what small constraints (n <= 15?) would let bitmask-DP over visited airports work.

canonical.pattern: bridge finding via Tarjan low-link (single DFS)
canonical.key_idea: One DFS assigns discovery times disc[] and low[v] = smallest disc reachable from v's subtree using tree edges plus at most one back edge. Tree edge (u,v) is a bridge iff low[v] > disc[u]: nothing in v's subtree climbs above u without that edge.
canonical.invariant: After finishing v's subtree, low[v] = min disc[] over all vertices reachable from the subtree of v via any number of tree edges plus at most one back edge; bridge condition is strict: low[child] > disc[u].

progression.idea: For each edge, delete it and run BFS/DFS to test whether the graph stays connected; if not, it's a bridge. Correct but repeats a full traversal per edge — 10^5 edges times 10^5 traversal is 10^10, hopeless.
progression.complexity: O(E * (V + E)) time, O(V + E) space

progression.idea: Tarjan bridge DFS: on entering v set disc[v] = low[v] = timer++; for each neighbor, recurse on unvisited (then low[v] = min(low[v], low[child]), record bridge if low[child] > disc[v]) or, for a visited non-parent neighbor, low[v] = min(low[v], disc[neighbor]).
progression.complexity: O(V + E) time, O(V + E) space

traps: Strict vs non-strict: low[child] >= disc[u] is the articulation-point condition, not the bridge condition — using >= reports extra edges.

Back-edge relaxation canonically uses disc[neighbor]; on a simple graph (which LC 1192 guarantees) low[neighbor] happens to yield the identical bridge set, but the habit silently breaks the articulation-point variant — there a leaked low value can slip under the low[child] >= disc[u] test and hide a cut vertex — so learn the disc form.
Skipping the parent by node id breaks on parallel edges (the second copy is a legitimate back edge that kills the bridge); skip by edge index when multi-edges are possible.
Recursion depth can hit 10^5 on a path graph — iterative DFS or a bigger stack in C++, sys.setrecursionlimit alone is not enough in Python.
followups: Find articulation points instead - condition becomes low[child] >= disc[u], with the root special-cased: it's a cut vertex iff it has 2+ DFS children.
Compress into 2-edge-connected components - remove bridges, flood-fill the rest; the component graph is a tree with bridges as edges (bridge tree).
Edges are inserted online, maintain bridge count - incremental bridge connectivity via DSU over the bridge tree / link-cut trees; classic Codeforces problem.
Randomized alternative without Tarjan - assign random 64-bit values to non-tree edges, xor down the tree; a tree edge is a bridge iff its accumulated xor is 0.
Why doesn't this transfer to directed graphs - 'bridge' becomes strong-connectivity-critical edges; you'd reason on the SCC condensation instead.
canonical.mutation: Instead of critical edges, return all critical NODES — servers whose removal disconnects the network. The low-link machinery survives but the condition and the root both change; re-derive low[child] >= disc[u] and why the DFS root needs the child-count rule.

canonical.pattern: weighted union-find on a ratio graph
canonical.key_idea: Each equation a/b = k is a pair of directed weighted edges (a->b weight k, b->a weight 1/k); a query is the product of edge weights along any path. Weighted DSU stores each variable's ratio to its root so queries become one divide.
canonical.invariant: DSU invariant: w[x] = value(x) / value(root(x)) at all times — path compression multiplies weights along the compressed chain, and union picks w[rootA] = (w[b] * k) / w[a] to preserve it.

progression.idea: Build the adjacency list with reciprocal edges; for each query run DFS/BFS from numerator to denominator multiplying weights, with a visited set. Fine at LC scale but every query pays a full traversal — wasteful when Q is large.
progression.complexity: O(Q * (V + E)) time, O(V + E) space

progression.idea: Weighted DSU: w[x] holds value(x)/value(parent[x]). find(x) path-compresses while multiplying weights down the chain so w[x] becomes value(x)/value(root). union(a,b,k) attaches rootA under rootB with the weight that makes a/b = k hold. Query a/b: same root ? w[a]/w[b] : -1.
progression.complexity: O((N + Q) * alpha(N)) time, O(N) space

traps: A query mentioning any variable that never appeared in equations is -1 — even x/x for unknown x, a very common wrong answer.
Forgetting the reciprocal edge (or the reciprocal weight during union) makes half the queries unreachable or wrong.
In the plain-DFS version, forgetting the per-query visited set loops forever on cycles like a->b->a.
Getting the union weight formula inverted — derive it from the invariant on the spot (value(a) = w[a]*value(rootA), set a/b = k, solve) rather than memorizing.
followups: Equations arrive online interleaved with queries - weighted DSU is already incremental; nothing changes.
Detect contradictions like a/b=2 then a/b=3 - on union of two already-same-root nodes, check w[a]/w[b] against k within epsilon and flag mismatch.
Avoid floating-point drift on long chains - store weights as exact rational pairs (num, den) with gcd reduction, or log-space doubles.
10^5 variables and 10^5 queries - per-query DFS is O(Q*E) and dies; DSU is near-linear total.
Can BFS give a 'best' path when equations are noisy/inconsistent - yes, treat log-weights and average over paths, but that leaves exact-solve territory into least squares.
canonical.mutation: Some equations may be mutually contradictory, and for each query you must answer only if the value is uniquely determined by a consistent subset containing all equations on that component. Re-derive: contradiction detection at union time, and why one inconsistency poisons an entire component rather than a single pair.

canonical.pattern: tree center via layered leaf peeling (topological trim)
canonical.key_idea: The roots minimizing height are exactly the tree's center(s) — the middle 1 or 2 nodes of a diameter path. Repeatedly strip all current leaves in rounds; whatever remains when <= 2 nodes are left is the answer.
canonical.invariant: Stripping a full leaf layer decreases every surviving node's eccentricity by exactly 1 (every node's eccentricity is realized at some leaf), and a center is never a leaf while more than 2 nodes remain — so the loop 'while remaining > 2: strip all leaves' terminates holding precisely the center set (size 1 or 2).

progression.idea: Root the tree at every node, BFS to compute its height, return the argmin set. Simple and correct but n BFS passes is quadratic — 2*10^4 nodes already means ~4*10^8 node visits.

Two-BFS diameter trick: BFS from any node to the farthest node u, BFS from u to farthest v recording parents; the middle one or two nodes of the u-v path are the centers. Linear and elegant, but you must prove 'center = diameter midpoint' and reconstruct the path correctly.

Kahn-style peel: queue all degree-1 nodes, remove them a full layer at a time (decrementing neighbor degrees, enqueueing new degree-1 nodes), stop while remaining > 2. Equally linear, no proof gymnastics, and each peeled layer lowers every survivor's eccentricity by exactly one, homing in on the centers.

traps: n == 1 returns [0] and n == 2 returns both nodes — the 'while remaining > 2' guard handles these only if you seed correctly (a single node has degree 0, never enters the leaf queue).
Peel a full layer per round (snapshot the queue length); interleaving single-node removals corrupts which 'round' a node belongs to.
Enqueue a neighbor exactly when its degree drops to 1, not <= 1, or already-removed nodes re-enter.
If your answer ever has 3+ nodes, your termination condition is wrong — a tree has at most 2 centers, full stop.
followups: Why at most two answers - the center of a tree is the midpoint of any diameter path; a path of length d has 1 middle node (d even) or 2 (d odd).
What height do the MHT roots achieve - ceil(diameter / 2).
Relation to centroid - different concept: centroid minimizes max subtree SIZE, center minimizes eccentricity; they can differ (star with one long tail).
Input isn't guaranteed to be a tree - peeling on a graph with cycles never reduces below the cycle; you'd need to detect that and reason on the block structure instead.
Find the center in a forest - run per component; 'minimum height' is only meaningful per tree.
canonical.mutation: Edges now carry positive integer weights and height = weighted distance. Degree-based peeling is meaningless (it ignores weights entirely) — re-derive via the weighted diameter (two-DFS still works) and note the true optimum point may sit strictly inside an edge, so you must argue which endpoint(s) to return.

canonical.pattern: single-source reachability on a directed graph (DFS/BFS flood)
canonical.key_idea: Rooms are vertices and each key is a directed edge room -> key's room. The question is pure reachability: flood from room 0 and check whether the visited set has size n.
canonical.invariant: At every step, visited = exactly the set of rooms reachable from room 0 via keys already collected; each room is pushed at most once, at the moment its first key is found.

progression.idea: Fixpoint iteration: repeatedly scan all visited rooms; if any holds a key to an unvisited room, mark it and restart the scan until a full pass changes nothing. Correct but each of up to n rounds rescans all keys — quadratic-ish and pointless when a stack does the bookkeeping.
progression.complexity: O(n * K) time (K = total keys), O(n) space

progression.idea: DFS or BFS from room 0 with a visited array: push room 0, pop a room, push every key's room not yet visited (marking at push time). Each room and key touched once; finish by checking visitedCount == n.

traps: Mark visited when PUSHING, not when popping — otherwise a room with many inbound keys enters the queue repeatedly and K blows up the frontier.
Room 0 needs no key: count it as visited before the loop or your final count is off by one.
Keys can be duplicates or point to the room itself — harmless only if the visited check happens before every push.
The answer compares visited count to n at the END; short-circuiting 'true' as soon as the stack empties is fine, but short-circuiting inside the loop on count==n needs the count actually maintained.
followups: List the unreachable rooms instead - same flood, return the complement of visited.
You may choose the starting room: which starts open everything - reachability from every node is O(n*(n+K)) naively; better, condense SCCs and check for a unique source SCC.
Keys are single-use and doors relock behind you - state must include which keys remain; reachability becomes search over (room, key-multiset), exponential in general, bitmask BFS if keys <= ~20.
A room needs ALL of several listed keys to open - BFS with a per-room countdown of missing keys; a room enqueues when its counter hits 0 (Kahn-flavored).
Stream version: keys are granted over time - re-run flood from newly unlocked rooms only; amortized linear with a pending-keys buffer per locked room.
canonical.mutation: Each key now opens its target door exactly once, and the door relocks when you leave the room. Plain reachability collapses because 'can I get there' depends on consumption order — re-derive the state space (room, remaining-keys bitmask), its size, and when the problem stops being tractable.

canonical.pattern: cycle avoidance via reverse-graph topological sort (or 3-color DFS)
canonical.key_idea: A node is safe iff EVERY path from it ends in a terminal node — equivalently, it cannot reach any cycle. Reverse all edges and run Kahn's starting from terminals (out-degree 0 originally); every node ever dequeued is safe. Alternatively 3-color DFS where hitting a gray node condemns the current path.
canonical.invariant: Kahn form: a node is dequeued iff every one of its ORIGINAL out-neighbors has already been proven safe (counter hit 0). DFS form: gray = on current path; gray-hit proves a cycle through the current chain.

progression.idea: From each node, enumerate paths looking for one that revisits a node (cycle) — exponential path count; even bounding by 'DFS per source with fresh visited' is O(V*(V+E)) and re-proves the same subgraphs safe over and over.

Single DFS with 3 colors: white unseen, gray on the current stack, black finished-safe. See gray => cycle => everything on the current chain is unsafe; a node is black only if all out-neighbors ended black. Linear, but the memoization is subtle — one 'visited means safe' shortcut and it's wrong.
progression.complexity: O(V + E) time, O(V + E) space

progression.idea: Mechanical version: reverse the graph, compute each node's original out-degree, queue the terminals, and Kahn-peel: dequeue a safe node, decrement the out-degree counter of each reverse-neighbor, enqueue those hitting 0. Sort the dequeued set. Same complexity as DFS but no recursion and nothing subtle.
progression.complexity: O(V + E) time, O(V + E) space

traps: The classic bug: memoizing a single boolean 'visited' without separating in-progress (gray) from done (black) — nodes still on the stack get read as safe and the answer includes cycle members.
Terminal nodes (out-degree 0) are SAFE by definition, not edge cases to exclude.
Unsafe is bigger than 'on a cycle' — nodes that merely REACH a cycle are unsafe too; a check that only finds cycle members underfills the unsafe set.
The answer must be in ascending index order — free if you iterate 0..n-1 collecting safe flags, not if you dump a hash set.
followups: Relation to SCCs - unsafe nodes are exactly those that can reach a nontrivial SCC (or a self-loop); safe = the rest of the condensation DAG below only trivial SCCs.
Do it iteratively without reversing - explicit-stack 3-color DFS; gray/black transitions happen on the post-visit, which is the part people fumble.
Count distinct paths from each safe node to terminals - after deleting unsafe nodes the rest is a DAG; DP in reverse topological order, watch overflow.
Edges are added dynamically, maintain the safe set - additions can only shrink it; recheck lazily by re-running the peel from affected counters, or accept O(V+E) rebuilds.
Why not just Tarjan SCC first - it works (mark nontrivial SCCs unsafe, back-propagate reachability), it's just more machinery than the problem needs.
canonical.mutation: Redefine safe as: SOME path from the node reaches a terminal (exists instead of for-all). The for-all structure that forced out-degree counting evaporates — re-derive why this is now plain reverse-reachability (one BFS from all terminals on the reversed graph) with no counters at all.
