canonical.pattern: shortest path on an implicit state graph (BFS, bidirectional optional)
canonical.key_idea: The 10^4 wheel combinations are vertices; each state has 8 neighbors (4 wheels x +-1 with wrap). Unweighted shortest path from "0000" to target with deadends as deleted vertices — plain BFS by layers.
canonical.invariant: BFS layer k contains exactly the states whose minimum turn count is k; deadends are inserted into visited before the walk begins, so they behave as removed vertices.

progression.idea: DFS/backtracking over turn sequences: explores exponentially many sequences, revisits states, and even with a visited set gives A path, not the SHORTEST — depth-first order has no distance guarantee on this graph.

BFS from "0000": seed visited with all deadends, expand layer by layer generating 8 neighbors per state, return the depth at which target appears. State space is capped at 10^4 so this is already fast in absolute terms.

Bidirectional BFS: grow frontiers from both "0000" and target, always expanding the smaller one, stop when a generated state is in the opposite frontier (answer = sum of the two depths). Cuts explored states from ~b^d to ~2*b^(d/2) in practice; same worst-case bound.
progression.complexity: O(10^4 * 8) worst-case time, O(10^4) space (far smaller typical frontier)

traps: "0000" itself in deadends means return -1 before anything else; target == "0000" means return 0 — both trivially forgotten.
Wheel wrap 9<->0: in C++ compute (d + 9) % 10 for the down-turn, since (d - 1) % 10 goes negative.
Mark visited at ENQUEUE time; marking at dequeue lets the 8-way branching enqueue the same state many times and stalls the run.
In bidirectional BFS the deadend set must block BOTH directions, and the meeting check happens on generation, not after a full layer, or you overcount by one move.
followups: Why does BFS give optimality here - all moves cost 1; the first time a state is generated is via a shortest sequence, guaranteed by layer order.
Add A* - admissible heuristic = sum over wheels of min(|a-b|, 10-|a-b|); consistent, so first pop of target is optimal.
Generalize to n wheels base b - state space b^n, BFS O(n * b^n); for n=10 you're at 10^10 and need bidirectional/A*/meet-in-the-middle.
Turning wheel i costs c_i - edge weights differ, BFS breaks; switch to Dijkstra over the same implicit graph.
Memory-constrained variant - iterative deepening A* (IDA*) trades re-expansion for O(depth) memory.
canonical.mutation: One move now turns TWO ADJACENT wheels simultaneously (both +1 or both -1). The neighbor function shrinks and the graph may disconnect — re-derive the state graph, find the conserved quantity (an invariant mod 10 over alternating wheel sums) that decides solvability before any search.

canonical.pattern: hash map complement lookup (single pass)
canonical.key_idea: For each element x, ask whether target-x has already been seen; store value-to-index in a hash map as you scan, so every pair is examined exactly once without nesting loops.
canonical.invariant: Before processing index i, the map contains value-to-index for exactly the elements at indices 0..i-1.

progression.idea: Check every pair (i,j) with a double loop; insufficient because n^2 pair checks are hopeless past n ~ 1e4.

Copy with original indices, sort, then two pointers inward on the sum; works but pays a sort and extra bookkeeping to recover indices, and still needs O(n) space, so it strictly loses to hashing here.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: Single left-to-right pass: check map for target-x, then insert x with its index. Check-before-insert guarantees the match is a strictly earlier index.

traps: Insert AFTER the complement check, or x pairs with itself (target 6, x = 3 at a single position)
target - x can overflow 32-bit int for extreme values; compute in long long in C++ if constraints allow negatives near INT_MIN
Duplicates are legal and must work (e.g. [3,3], target 6) - the check-then-insert order handles this for free
Return indices, not values; and the two indices must be distinct positions
followups: What if the array is already sorted? - two pointers, O(n) time and O(1) extra space, no hashing needed
Return ALL pairs, including with duplicates? - frequency map and enumerate distinct values, pairing counts carefully (v with target-v, and the v == target-v case uses C(cnt,2))
Extend to 3Sum / kSum? - fix one element and recurse to (k-1)Sum; sorted two-pointer base gives O(n^2) for 3Sum
Design a class with add(num) and find(target) called many times? - count map: add is O(1), find is O(n) over distinct values, watch the need for count >= 2 when complement equals the value
Hash map worst case in adversarial settings? - O(n) per op under collision attacks; sorted two-pointer is the deterministic fallback
canonical.mutation: Change 'sums exactly to target' to 'sum closest to target': why does the hash map die outright, and what is the new optimal approach and its complexity?

canonical.pattern: canonical-form hashing (bucket by signature)
canonical.key_idea: Map each string to a signature that is identical for all its anagrams - its sorted form or its 26-slot letter-count vector - and bucket strings under that key in a hash map. Grouping becomes a single linear pass.
canonical.invariant: Two strings land in the same bucket if and only if their character-frequency vectors are equal.

progression.idea: Pairwise anagram tests (compare count vectors for every pair) and merge into groups; insufficient because n^2 pair comparisons dominate even though each test is O(k).
progression.complexity: O(n^2 * k) time, O(n * k) space

progression.idea: Use each string sorted as its map key; correct and simple, but pays a per-string sort that the counting signature avoids.
progression.complexity: O(n * k log k) time, O(n * k) space

progression.idea: Build a 26-entry count vector per string and use it (serialized with delimiters) as the key; one O(k) pass per string, no sorting.
progression.complexity: O(n * k) time, O(n * k) space

traps: Serializing the count vector needs delimiters: counts [1,12] and [11,2] concatenate to the same digits without a separator
std::unordered_map<vector<int>, ...> needs a custom hasher in C++; std::map works out of the box but adds a log factor
Empty strings are anagrams of each other and must form a group, not be dropped
Confirm the alphabet with the interviewer - the fixed 26-array silently assumes lowercase ASCII
followups: Sorted key vs count key, when does each win? - count is O(k) vs O(k log k), so it wins on long strings; sorted key is less code for short dictionary words
Unicode or unbounded alphabet? - fixed array dies; use the sorted string, or a sorted list of (char,count) pairs as the key
Prime-product hashing (letter -> prime, key = product)? - cute but overflows 64 bits fast on long words; needs modular hashing plus collision verification
Streaming input, groups maintained online? - same map, each arriving string is O(k) to place; groups grow incrementally
Deterministic output order? - keep a vector of buckets plus key-to-bucket-index map so groups appear in first-seen order
canonical.mutation: Group by shift-equivalence instead: two strings match if one is a uniform Caesar shift of the other (so 'abc' groups with 'bcd' and 'zab'). Re-derive the canonical signature.

canonical.pattern: frequency count + partial selection (bucket sort / size-k heap / quickselect)
canonical.key_idea: Count frequencies in a hash map, then select the k largest counts WITHOUT fully sorting: either a size-k min-heap, quickselect on the (value,count) pairs, or - since every frequency lies in [1,n] - linear-time frequency buckets scanned from the top.
canonical.invariant: bucket[f] holds exactly the distinct values whose frequency equals f, and every frequency is bounded by n, so the bucket index space is O(n).

progression.idea: Count, then sort all distinct elements by frequency descending and take k; insufficient because it fully sorts when only a k-selection is needed.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: Maintain a min-heap of (count,value) capped at size k - evict the smallest whenever it grows past k; still carries a log k factor per distinct element.
progression.complexity: O(n log k) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: bucket[f] = list of values with count f (f <= n bounds the index space); walk buckets from n down, emitting until k collected. Pure linear passes.

traps: Heap direction: keep the k LARGEST by evicting from a MIN-heap; C++ priority_queue is a max-heap by default, so you need greater<> - the classic inversion
Multiple values can share one frequency: stop emitting from a bucket at exactly k, and never assume one value per bucket
Quickselect is O(n^2) worst case without randomized pivots - say it before an interviewer does
Answer may be returned in any order; sorting the output re-introduces the log factor you just removed
followups: Why is bucket sort legal here but not for general sorting? - frequencies live in the bounded integer domain [1,n], sidestepping the comparison lower bound
Heap vs quickselect? - heap is worst-case O(n log k) and works online; quickselect is O(n) expected but offline and needs randomization
Top k frequent WORDS with lexicographic tie-break? - comparator on (count, word); with buckets you must sort within a bucket, paying a log on bucket size
Unbounded stream, bounded memory? - exact top-k is impossible; Misra-Gries or Count-Min Sketch plus a candidate heap gives approximate guarantees
Sharded across machines? - exact needs a full count merge; practical systems send top-k' (k' > k) per shard or merge sketches, and you should name the error tradeoff
canonical.mutation: k disappears and memory drops to O(k) while the input becomes an unbounded stream: what exactness guarantee survives, and which algorithm family do you reach for?

canonical.pattern: prefix-suffix decomposition (two-pass accumulation, no division)
canonical.key_idea: answer[i] = (product of everything left of i) * (product of everything right of i). Fill the output with prefix products left-to-right, then sweep right-to-left multiplying in a single running suffix variable - division never appears.
canonical.invariant: After the left pass, ans[i] holds the product of nums[0..i-1]; during the right pass, suffix holds the product of nums[i+1..n-1] at the moment ans[i] is finalized.

progression.idea: For each i, multiply all j != i; insufficient at O(n^2) and gains nothing structurally.
Total product divided by nums[i]; insufficient because division is typically banned by the prompt and zeros wreck it (one zero and two-plus zeros are separate cases needing a zero count).
Pass 1: ans[i] = prefix product of nums[0..i-1] (ans[0] = 1). Pass 2: keep suffix running from the right, ans[i] *= suffix, then suffix *= nums[i].
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(1) extra space beyond the output array

traps: Empty-product base: prefix and suffix both initialize to 1, and ans[0] / ans[n-1] each see exactly one side
In the right pass, multiply ans[i] by suffix BEFORE updating suffix with nums[i] - swapping those lines includes nums[i] in its own answer
Overflow: products of 1e5 32-bit values exceed 64 bits in general; LeetCode promises the answer fits in 32 bits, but state that assumption aloud
If you do discuss the division variant, enumerate zero counts 0 / 1 / >=2 explicitly - each gives a different output shape
followups: If division were allowed? - total product over nums[i] with an explicit zero-count case analysis, still O(n)
Range product queries with point updates? - Fenwick or segment tree over products, plus a separate zero-count tree because zeros are not invertible
Does the trick generalize? - any operation with prefix-decomposable aggregates works via prefix/suffix arrays; invertible ops (sum, XOR) additionally allow the divide-out shortcut
Products mod prime p? - division becomes multiplication by modular inverse; values divisible by p play the role of zeros
Can you beat O(n) space including the output? - no, the output itself is Omega(n); the achievement is zero auxiliary arrays
canonical.mutation: Replace product with (a) XOR-except-self and (b) max-except-self: for each, decide whether the single-aggregate divide-out shortcut works or the prefix/suffix decomposition is forced, and say exactly why.

canonical.pattern: hash set with sequence-start filtering (amortized single walk per run)
canonical.key_idea: Dump all values into a hash set, then walk upward (x, x+1, x+2, ...) ONLY from values x where x-1 is absent - i.e. only from the minimum of each run. Every run is traversed exactly once, making the whole thing linear despite the nested loop.
canonical.invariant: An element x begins a walk if and only if x-1 is not in the set, so each maximal run is traversed exactly once, from its minimum.

progression.idea: From every element, extend upward by re-scanning the array for value+1 at each step; insufficient - up to n starts times n extension steps times n per membership scan.
progression.complexity: O(n^3) time worst case, O(1) space

progression.idea: Sort, then one scan counting runs while skipping duplicates; clean and deterministic, but the sort breaks the O(n) target the problem explicitly sets.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space (in-place sort)

progression.idea: Hash set of all values; iterate the set, and only when x-1 is missing, walk x, x+1, ... counting length. The start filter is the entire trick.

traps: Omitting the x-1 start check silently degrades to O(n^2): every member of a run re-walks its whole suffix - the single most common way to fail this problem
Duplicates: iterate the SET, not the array, or duplicated starts re-walk runs
x+1 and x-1 at INT_MAX / INT_MIN overflow in C++; widen to long long or guard the boundaries
Empty input returns 0 - easy to miss when the walk loop assumes at least one start
followups: Prove the O(n) bound despite the nested while - every value is consumed by exactly one walk since walks start only at run minima; amortized argument
Hash set worst case? - adversarial collisions make unordered_set O(n) per op; the sort-based O(n log n) is the deterministic fallback, and say so unprompted at an HFT interview
Union-find alternative? - union x with x+1 when present, answer is the largest component; same asymptotics with inverse-Ackermann overhead and more code
Return the actual sequence? - record the best start and length during walks, materialize at the end
Longest consecutive path in a binary tree or matrix? - same 'consecutive values' idea but graph-shaped: DFS with memoization instead of a set walk
canonical.mutation: Elements now arrive one at a time, and after EVERY insert you must report the current longest run in O(1): re-derive the structure (what do you store at run endpoints, and what happens on a merge?).

canonical.pattern: stack matching (LIFO bracket pairing)
canonical.key_idea: Push every opener; on a closer, the stack top must be its matching opener - pop it or fail immediately. The string is valid iff no mismatch occurred and the stack ends empty. LIFO is forced because the most recently opened bracket must close first.
canonical.invariant: At each position, the stack holds exactly the currently unmatched openers, innermost on top.

progression.idea: Repeatedly delete adjacent matched pairs (), [], {} until no change; valid iff the string empties. Insufficient: up to n/2 passes of O(n) each.
One scan with a stack: openers push; closers compare-and-pop against the top. Fail fast on empty-stack close or wrong top; final check is emptiness.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) space worst case (all openers)

traps: Closer arriving on an empty stack - e.g. ')(' - check emptiness BEFORE reading top, or UB in C++
Leftover openers at the end - '((' passes every per-character check and fails only the final emptiness test
Odd-length strings can be rejected upfront; cheap win interviewers like to hear
Map closers to expected openers once (or use a switch); ad-hoc if-chains breed the ']' vs '}' typo
followups: Single bracket type - can the stack go? - yes: a counter; fail if it ever dips negative, valid iff it ends at 0
Longest valid parentheses substring? - stack of indices with a base marker, or DP; O(n)
Minimum additions to make valid? - for one type, track open surplus and needed inserts in one pass; answer is unmatched closes plus leftover opens
Validity with '*' as wildcard (open, close, or empty)? - track the interval [lo, hi] of feasible open counts; valid iff hi never goes negative and lo can end at 0

Validate a terabyte string in parallel? - each chunk reduces to (unmatched closes, unmatched opens) per type-free case and these compose associatively - a monoid, so map-reduce works; with multiple types you reduce each chunk to an irreducible close-prefix + open-suffix and concatenate-cancel
canonical.mutation: You must validate strictly left-to-right holding O(1) state (one machine word): for which bracket alphabets is this possible, and what does the two-plus-type case provably require?

canonical.pattern: augmented stack (per-node suffix minimum)
canonical.key_idea: Store, alongside each pushed value, the minimum of the stack up to and including that node - either as a (value, min) pair or a synchronized second stack. The top node then answers getMin in O(1), and popping automatically restores the previous minimum with zero recomputation.
canonical.invariant: For every node, its stored minimum equals min(node value, stored minimum of the node beneath it) - hence the top always carries the global stack minimum.

progression.idea: Plain stack; getMin scans every element. Insufficient: getMin is O(n), which the problem statement forbids.
progression.complexity: O(1) push/pop/top, O(n) getMin; O(n) space

progression.idea: Second stack pushed on EVERY operation with min(new, current top of min-stack); correct and O(1) everywhere, but always stores 2n entries even when the minimum almost never changes.
progression.complexity: O(1) all ops; O(n) space, always 2 entries per element

progression.idea: Sparse aux stack: push onto it only when v <= current min (note <=, not <); pop from it only when the popped value equals its top. Same O(1) ops, aux space shrinks to the number of running minima.
progression.complexity: O(1) all ops; O(n) space worst case, aux stack often far smaller

traps: Sparse version MUST push on v <= min, not v < min: with duplicate minima, popping one copy would otherwise strand getMin on a value no longer present
Pop must compare against the aux top and pop it on equality - forgetting this desyncs the two stacks permanently
The O(1)-extra-space delta-encoding trick (push 2v - min when v < min) can overflow 64 bits since 2v - min spans about 2.5x the value range - flag it before using it
Empty-stack pop/top/getMin: the problem guarantees valid calls, but say what your API would do anyway
followups: O(1) auxiliary space version? - encode deltas: when v < min, push 2v - min and set min = v; decoding on pop reverses it; mention the overflow caveat
Max Stack with popMax? - lazy deletion with a max-heap plus tombstones, or a doubly linked list with an ordered multimap; popMax O(log n)
Min QUEUE? - two min-stacks simulating a queue gives amortized O(1) - and that is exactly the sliding-window-minimum engine
getMedian instead of getMin? - per-node constant summaries no longer suffice; you need order statistics (two heaps or an indexed BST), ops become O(log n)
Concurrent min stack? - Treiber lock-free stack works unchanged because the min is per-node and immutable once pushed - no shared mutable aggregate to contend on
canonical.mutation: getMin becomes getMin(k) - the minimum among only the top k elements: why does the per-node suffix-min invariant collapse, and what structure restores O(1)-ish queries (hint: what does a range-min query over the stack array need on each push)?

canonical.pattern: monotonic stack (next greater element)
canonical.key_idea: Scan left to right holding a stack of indices whose answer is still unknown, kept non-increasing (weakly decreasing) by temperature - equal temperatures stack up together. Each new strictly warmer reading pops and resolves every colder index above it - so every index is pushed once and popped at most once.
canonical.invariant: The stack holds indices in increasing position order and non-increasing (weakly decreasing) temperature - equal temperatures coexist on the stack - precisely the set of days still awaiting a strictly warmer day.

progression.idea: For each day, scan forward until a strictly warmer day; insufficient because a monotonically decreasing input forces the full O(n^2).
Monotonic decreasing stack of INDICES: while current temp beats the stack top's temp, pop and set ans[top] = i - top; then push i. Leftovers keep their default 0.

traps: Push indices, not temperatures - the answer is a DISTANCE, and the distance needs the position
Strictness: warmer means strictly greater; pop while temp[top] < temp[i], so equal temperatures stay parked on the stack
Do all pops BEFORE the push of i; interleaving corrupts the monotone order
Indices never resolved (suffix maxima) must read 0 - pre-fill the answer array rather than flushing the stack manually
followups: Prove O(n) despite the nested while - each index enters the stack once and leaves at most once, so total stack operations are at most 2n; amortized
O(1) extra space? - sweep right to left using the answer array itself as jump pointers: from i+1, hop ans[] to skip runs of colder days
Circular array (next greater element II)? - iterate i from 0 to 2n-1 with i mod n, pushing only during the first lap
Previous smaller / next smaller variants? - same skeleton with the comparison flipped and/or direction reversed; that quartet is the substrate for histogram-type problems
Does it work online as readings stream in? - yes, the stack is inherently online: each new reading resolves past days immediately and permanently
canonical.mutation: 'Next warmer day' becomes 'next day at least 5 degrees warmer': construct a 3-4 element input where the residual stack stops being monotone and a buried index misses its answer - then re-derive (what do you key a heap on, and what does it cost?).

canonical.pattern: monotonic stack (nearest smaller on both sides / span expansion)
canonical.key_idea: The best rectangle at full height h[i] spans from just past i's previous-smaller bar to just before its next-smaller bar. One increasing stack finds both boundaries in a single pass: the arriving smaller bar is the popped bar's right boundary, and the new stack top is its left boundary.
canonical.invariant: The stack holds indices of bars with increasing heights whose right boundary is still unknown; when h[i] < h[top], top's rectangle is settled with height h[top] and width i - (index below top) - 1.

progression.idea: Try every (l, r) pair keeping a running minimum height, area = min * (r - l + 1); insufficient at O(n^2) for n up to 1e5.

Divide and conquer on the minimum bar: the answer either crosses it at min height * full width, or lies wholly left / wholly right. Needs an RMQ (sparse table) to find minima fast; insufficient in practice - heavier machinery and worse constants than the stack, O(n^2) if you skip the RMQ on sorted input.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time (O(n) recursion after O(n log n) sparse-table build), O(n log n) space

progression.idea: Increasing stack of indices with a height-0 sentinel appended: when h[i] < h[top], pop top and settle its area as h[top] * (i - stack.newTop - 1), or width i if the stack empties. The sentinel flushes everything at the end.

traps: The empty-stack width case: after popping, if nothing remains, the width is i (rectangle reaches index 0) - THE classic bug; a -1 base sentinel or leading 0-height bar removes the branch
Trailing bars never meet a smaller bar - append a 0-height sentinel (or flush the stack after the loop) or you silently drop suffix rectangles
Equal heights: popping on <= leaves earlier equal bars with undersized widths, but the LAST equal bar gets the full span, so the maximum is still correct - know why before an interviewer asks
Area overflow: height 1e4 times width 1e5 is 1e9 - inside int32 but within 2.2x of the edge; compute in long long on principle
followups: Maximal rectangle of 1s in a binary matrix? - per row, maintain heights of consecutive 1s ending at that row and run this algorithm per row: O(rows * cols)
Why do nearest-smaller bars bound the optimum? - widening past either boundary includes a strictly shorter bar, capping the height below h[i]; exchange argument
Two explicit PSE/NSE passes vs settle-on-pop in one pass? - identical asymptotics; two-pass is easier to verify under pressure, one-pass is less code

Bars appended online, running max after each append? - a running best over settled pops alone is WRONG: bars still open on the stack contribute h[j] * (t - left_boundary_j), which grows with every append (append just [5]: settled best is 0, true max is 5). Answer each query with a virtual 0-height sentinel flush over the current stack - O(stack size) per query - or explicitly maximize over the open bars; the per-append answer is not free from the incremental stack
Largest rectangle in a bar chart with negative heights allowed? - full-height framing dies for negative bars; restrict to positive-height spans and treat negatives as hard separators
canonical.mutation: Bars gain arbitrary positive widths w[i]: which half of the stack derivation survives verbatim, and which formula must be re-derived (what replaces i - newTop - 1, and what precomputation makes it O(1) per pop)?

canonical.pattern: two pointers over prefix/suffix maxima
canonical.key_idea: Water above bar i equals min(maxLeft[i], maxRight[i]) - h[i]. Walk two pointers inward, moving the side whose running max is smaller, because that side's bound is already final regardless of unseen bars.
canonical.invariant: When leftMax <= rightMax, the column at l is fully determined by leftMax alone, since some bar of height >= leftMax exists to its right; symmetric on the other side.

progression.idea: For each index scan left for its max and right for its max, add min(L,R)-h[i]. Recomputes the same maxima n times, so it is quadratic and wasteful.
Precompute prefixMax[] and suffixMax[] in two passes, then one pass to sum min(prefixMax[i], suffixMax[i]) - h[i]. Optimal time but pays O(n) extra memory for two arrays.

Strictly decreasing monotonic stack of indices; on each pop, add a horizontal water slab: width = i - stack.top() - 1, height = min(h[i], h[stack.top()]) - h[popped]. Also linear, but the slab geometry is easy to get wrong and it still uses a stack.

Two pointers l, r with running leftMax, rightMax. If leftMax <= rightMax, the water at l is exactly leftMax - h[l] (right bound cannot be lower), settle l and advance; else settle r. One pass, no arrays.

traps: Advancing the pointer before adding that column's water (or vice versa) shifts every contribution by one bar.
Comparing h[l] vs h[r] instead of leftMax vs rightMax works only if maxes are updated first; mixing the two conventions silently drops water.
In the stack variant the slab is horizontal, not a vertical column: width is i - stack.top() - 1 after the pop, not i - popped.
First and last bars trap nothing; code that seeds leftMax/rightMax with h[0]/h[n-1] must not also add water for those positions.
followups: How does this extend to a 2D elevation grid - min-heap seeded with the boundary cells, pop lowest wall inward like a flood, O(mn log(mn)).
Bars arrive as a stream, one end only - two pointers need both ends; the monotonic stack version works online in one direction.
Each bar i has width w[i] - multiply each column contribution by w[i]; the two-pointer argument is unchanged.
Prove the two-pointer step is safe - exchange argument: with leftMax <= rightMax the true right bound for l is >= leftMax, so min of the bounds equals leftMax exactly.
How would you parallelize - prefixMax and suffixMax are scans, so compute both with a parallel prefix in O(n/p + log p) per pass.
canonical.mutation: You may delete exactly one bar before the water settles; maximize the total trapped water. Which precomputed structures let you evaluate each deletion in O(1), and why does the plain two-pointer walk stop being enough?

canonical.pattern: opposing two pointers with exchange argument
canonical.key_idea: Area = min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l). Start at the extremes and always move the shorter wall inward: keeping it can only pair it with smaller widths while its height still caps the area, so no optimum is ever discarded.
canonical.invariant: At every step, some optimal pair (i*, j*) satisfies l <= i* <= j* <= r: a pointer is only moved off a wall after all pairs using that wall as the minimum have been dominated by the current area.

progression.idea: Evaluate all n(n-1)/2 pairs and take the max. Correct but quadratic; ignores that most pairs are dominated by a wider pair with the same short wall.

l = 0, r = n-1; record area; move the pointer at the shorter wall (either on tie). Every skipped pair (short wall, something inside) has width < current and height <= short wall, hence area <= current recorded area.

traps: Moving the taller pointer breaks the proof: the discarded pairs are not dominated and the optimum can be lost.
On h[l] == h[r], moving either is safe (moving both is also safe) - but only after recording the current area.
min(h) * width overflows 32-bit when heights ~1e9 and n ~1e5; compute in 64-bit in C++.
Do not confuse with the trapped-water problem: here only the two chosen walls matter and interior bars are ignored.
followups: Prove no optimal pair is skipped - exchange argument: if h[l] < h[r], every pair (l, j) with j < r has area <= min-height h[l] times a smaller width.
Return the indices, not just the area - track argmax (l, r) at each improvement; nothing else changes.

Find the k largest containers - the single-pass greedy certifies only the single maximum: its dominance argument discards pairs that can still rank in the top k, and area is not monotone under pointer moves (e.g. [1,100,100,1]: the widest pair (0,3) scores 3 while the inner pair (1,2) scores 100), so a best-first heap frontier from the ends pops states out of order and is wrong. Enumerate all pairs while keeping a size-k min-heap of areas, O(n^2 log k).
Heights can be updated between queries - a single query is O(n) anyway; there is no known polylog dynamic structure, so recompute per query.
Why does binary search not apply - area is not monotone or unimodal in either pointer; the structure exploited is dominance, not order.
canonical.mutation: Score becomes min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l)^2. Does moving the shorter wall still never discard the optimum? Exhibit the failure or re-prove, and give the best algorithm you can for the new objective.

canonical.pattern: sort + two-pointer pair sum with duplicate skipping
canonical.key_idea: Sort, fix the smallest element a[i], then find pairs summing to -a[i] in the sorted suffix with converging pointers. Sorting makes duplicate triplets adjacent so they can be skipped by index, not by hashing.
canonical.invariant: With i fixed, every pair strictly outside [l, r] has already been classified: pairs dropped on the left summed too small, pairs dropped on the right summed too large.

progression.idea: Triple nested loop, collect zero-sum triples into a set of sorted triples for dedup. Cubic time and the dedup set costs extra memory; hopeless for n = 3000+.
progression.complexity: O(n^3) time, O(t) space for t triplets

progression.idea: For each pair (i, j) look up -(a[i]+a[j]) in a hash map of values to indices. Quadratic, but deduplication of unordered triplets via hashing is error-prone and the map costs O(n).
Sort once. For each i (skipping repeats of a[i]), two pointers l = i+1, r = n-1 converge on sum -a[i]; on a hit, record and advance both past their duplicate runs. Break early once a[i] > 0.
progression.complexity: O(n^2) time, O(log n) space beyond output (sort recursion)

traps: Skip i only when i > start and a[i] == a[i-1]; comparing with a[i+1] instead skips valid first uses of a value.
After recording a triplet you must advance BOTH pointers past their duplicate runs, else the same triplet re-emits.
a[i] + a[l] + a[r] can overflow 32-bit near INT_MAX bounds; sum in 64-bit.
Early termination a[i] > 0 is valid only after sorting; with target t != 0 the break condition changes to 3*a[i] > t.
followups: Extend to 4Sum or kSum - one more outer loop per k, O(n^(k-1)); recursive kSum reduces to 2Sum base case.
3Sum closest - same scan but track min |sum - t| instead of exact hits; dedup no longer needed.
Count triplets (with element multiplicities) instead of listing distinct ones - run over duplicate runs and count combinatorially (choose 2 within a run, products across runs).
Can you beat O(n^2) - 3SUM is conjectured to have no strongly subquadratic algorithm; best known shaves subpolynomial factors only.
Solve without sorting - hash-based O(n^2) works but dedup needs normalized (sorted) triple keys in a set.
canonical.mutation: Output must be index triples (i < j < k) and the input order cannot be disturbed, with all index triples required, not distinct value triples. Sorting destroys indices - rederive the approach and its true output-sensitive complexity.

canonical.pattern: variable-size sliding window with last-seen index jump
canonical.key_idea: Grow r; the window [l, r] stays all-distinct by moving l just past the previous occurrence of s[r] whenever it duplicates. A last-index map lets l jump there directly instead of shrinking one step at a time.
canonical.invariant: [l, r] contains pairwise-distinct characters and l is the minimal such left end for the current r.

progression.idea: Check every substring for distinctness with a set. O(n^2) substrings times O(n) validation; ignores that extending a bad substring stays bad.
For each start l, extend r with a boolean seen[] until a repeat. Each start is O(sigma)-bounded but restarts discard all window knowledge, so total is quadratic-ish.
progression.complexity: O(n * min(n, sigma)) time, O(sigma) space

progression.idea: One pass with last[c] = latest index of c. At r: l = max(l, last[s[r]] + 1), then ans = max(ans, r - l + 1). l never retreats, so both pointers move at most n times.

traps: l = max(l, last[c] + 1): dropping the max lets a stale occurrence BEFORE the current window drag l backwards, corrupting the invariant.
Update the answer after fixing l for this r, not before; the pre-fix window may contain the duplicate.
Charset assumption: fixed int last[128/256] for ASCII, but unicode input needs a hash map keyed on code points.
Empty string must return 0; initializing ans = 1 unconditionally breaks it.
followups: Longest substring with at most k distinct characters - count map plus shrink-while-(distinct > k) window, O(n).
Return the substring, not the length - record argmax l alongside the max length.
At most one character allowed to repeat (one duplicate pair) - track duplicate count, shrink while it exceeds 1.
Input is a stream too large to store - the O(sigma) last-index map is all the state needed; one pass suffices as long as you only need the length.
Every character must appear at least k times - different problem: divide and conquer on rare characters or 26 windows parameterized by distinct-count.
canonical.mutation: Instead of no repeats, at most ONE character in the window may appear twice (all others distinct). Redefine the shrink condition and the state you maintain, and argue the pass is still O(n).

canonical.pattern: variable-size sliding window with need/have match counting
canonical.key_idea: Expand r accumulating counts of needed characters; once the window covers t's full multiset, shrink l maximally while still feasible and record. A matched counter over distinct characters makes feasibility O(1) to test.
canonical.invariant: matched equals the number of distinct characters c with have[c] >= need[c]; the window covers t iff matched == number of distinct chars in t.

progression.idea: For every start, extend until t's counts are covered, verifying with a 52/128-slot count comparison. Quadratic starts times count checks; recomputes coverage from scratch per start.

Prefix count matrix over needed chars; for each l binary search the smallest r whose prefix delta covers need. Coverage is monotone in r so the search is valid, but it is log-slower and memory-heavier than the window.
progression.complexity: O(n * sigma_t * log n) time, O(n * sigma_t) space

progression.idea: Single window: have[] counts window chars, matched = number of distinct chars with have >= need. Expand r; while matched == distinct(t), record r-l+1 and pop l. Each index enters and leaves once.

traps: t can contain duplicates: coverage is multiset containment, need[c] can be > 1 and a plain set of chars is wrong.
Increment matched only on the transition have[c] == need[c] (and decrement only when dropping below); using >= on every add double-counts.
Record the answer BEFORE popping l breaks feasibility, inside the while-feasible loop.
Initialize best length to a sentinel > n and return empty string if untouched; m > n is an immediate no.
followups: Characters of t must appear in order (as a subsequence) inside the window - min-window subsequence: forward greedy match to find an end, backward match to tighten the start, O(n*m) worst case.
Many query strings t against one fixed s - precompute next-occurrence tables nxt[i][c] on s, answer each t independently.
s is a stream and you only need the best length - the window needs only O(sigma) counters plus the current window contents; with random access denied keep a deque of needed-char positions.
Count all minimal windows or all feasible windows - for each r, after maximal shrink, l choices below the tight l all remain feasible: add l_tight - last_recorded bookkeeping.
Window may miss at most k characters of t - relax feasibility to matched_weight >= |t| - k using total covered count instead of per-char matched.
canonical.mutation: Coverage becomes ordered: the window must contain t as a subsequence, not as a multiset. Show why the matched-counter window collapses, and rederive the two-phase forward/backward pointer solution with its O(n*m) bound.

canonical.pattern: monotonic decreasing deque over a fixed-size window
canonical.key_idea: Keep a deque of indices whose values are strictly decreasing; the front is always the current window's max. New elements pop dominated (smaller-or-equal) tails; the front is evicted when its index leaves the window.
canonical.invariant: The deque holds indices from the current window in increasing index order with strictly decreasing values, so the front is the window maximum.

progression.idea: Scan all k elements of each of the n-k+1 windows. Re-examines each element up to k times; fine for tiny k, useless at k ~ n.
Max-heap (or multiset) of (value, index); pop stale indices lazily when they surface at the top. Correct and simple but every push/pop pays a log factor the structure of the problem does not require.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: Monotonic deque of indices: before pushing i, pop back while a[back] <= a[i] (dominated: smaller and dies sooner); pop front while front <= i - k. Answer at each i >= k-1 is a[front]. Each index is pushed and popped at most once.

traps: Evict the front by INDEX (front <= i - k), never by value equality - duplicate values make value-based eviction remove the wrong occurrence.
Pop-back condition a[back] <= a[i] vs < a[i]: with <=, equal older elements are discarded (safe and keeps the deque strict); with < they linger harmlessly but the invariant weakens to non-increasing.
Start emitting answers only at i == k-1; emitting earlier yields maxima of partial windows.
Store indices, not values - values alone cannot tell you when an element expires.
followups: Sliding window minimum - identical with an increasing deque.
Why is it O(n) despite the inner while - amortization: each index enters the deque once and leaves once, so total pops <= n.
2D version, max of every k x k submatrix - run the deque row-wise then column-wise on the row results, O(n*m) total.
Max over a queue supporting arbitrary push/pop (no fixed k) - monotonic queue built from two monotonic stacks, amortized O(1) per op.
Sum of maxima over ALL subarrays - deque no longer enough; contribution technique with previous/next greater element via monotonic stack, O(n).
canonical.mutation: Queries become arbitrary offline ranges [l, r] instead of a fixed width k. The deque's single-width assumption dies - rederive with a sparse table (O(n log n) build, O(1) query) or an offline monotonic-stack + BIT sweep, and justify the choice.

canonical.pattern: binary search on the answer (monotone feasibility predicate)
canonical.key_idea: hours(k) = sum of ceil(a_i / k) is non-increasing in k, so feasible(k) = hours(k) <= h is monotone. Binary search the smallest feasible k in [1, max(a)].
canonical.invariant: feasible(k) implies feasible(k+1); throughout the search, lo <= answer <= hi, with everything below lo proven infeasible and hi always feasible once first tested.

progression.idea: Try k = 1, 2, 3, ... and return the first feasible speed. Each trial is O(n), and the answer can be as large as max(a) ~ 1e9, so the scan is linear in the value range.
Binary search on k: lo = 1, hi = max(a); if feasible(mid) then hi = mid else lo = mid + 1. Monotonicity guarantees the loop closes on the minimal feasible speed.

traps: ceil as (a + k - 1) / k overflows 32-bit when a is near INT_MAX; use 64-bit or a/k + (a % k != 0).
The hour sum reaches n * 1 up to ~1e4 piles but intermediate ceil sums with small k can exceed 2^31; accumulate in long long.
lo must start at 1: k = 0 divides by zero, and hi = max(a) is tight since hours(max(a)) = n <= h is given.
Classic infinite loop: with hi = mid the midpoint must round down and lo = mid + 1 on infeasible, or the interval never shrinks.
followups: Why is hi = max(piles) sufficient - at that speed each pile takes exactly 1 hour, total n <= h; faster speeds cannot beat 1 hour per pile.
Same template elsewhere - ship capacity in D days, split array minimizing largest sum, aggressive cows (maximize the min gap, predicate flipped).
Answer is real-valued (fractional speed allowed, fractional hours) - then hours(k) = sum(a_i)/k and k* = sum(a)/h in closed form; no search needed.
Maximize instead of minimize - flip the predicate direction and keep the last feasible on the other side: lo = mid on feasible, with ceil midpoint.
Prove correctness of the loop - invariant: [lo, hi] always contains the minimal feasible k; each iteration halves it; terminates when lo == hi.
canonical.mutation: Koko may switch piles mid-hour, so partial hours across piles combine: total time becomes ceil(sum(a_i) / k) hours. Show the predicate is still monotone, derive the closed-form k*, and explain why binary search became unnecessary.

canonical.pattern: modified binary search (sorted-half classification)
canonical.key_idea: A rotation point splits the array into two sorted runs, and any [lo, hi] slice has at least one sorted half around mid. Identify the sorted half via a[lo] <= a[mid], range-test the target against it, and discard the half that cannot contain it.
canonical.invariant: If the target exists it lies in [lo, hi], and a[lo] <= a[mid] certifies [lo, mid] is sorted (else [mid, hi] is), because at most one rotation point exists in any subrange.

progression.idea: Linear scan for the target. Ignores that the array is two sorted runs; O(n) when O(log n) is available.

Two passes: binary search the rotation point (min index), then standard binary search in whichever run can contain the target by comparing with a[0]. Same asymptotics as one-pass but two loops and two boundary proofs to get right.
One pass: if a[lo] <= a[mid] the left half is sorted - recurse into it iff a[lo] <= target < a[mid], else go right; symmetric when the right half is sorted (a[mid] < target <= a[hi]).

traps: Use a[lo] <= a[mid] (with equality) so the single-element half lo == mid classifies as sorted; strict < misroutes it.
The range tests must be half-open and asymmetric: a[lo] <= t < a[mid] on the left, a[mid] < t <= a[hi] on the right; sloppy <= on both ends loops or skips.
Rotation by 0 (fully sorted input) must still work - the classification degenerates gracefully only if the equality cases are right.
With duplicates (a[lo] == a[mid] == a[hi]) neither half can be certified sorted; O(log n) is lost, shrink lo++/hi-- for worst-case O(n).
followups: Duplicates allowed (variant II) - when a[lo] == a[mid] == a[hi], shrink both ends by one and retry; worst case degrades to O(n), provably unavoidable.
Find how many times the array was rotated - the index of the minimum, found by its own binary search against a[hi].
Why can't duplicates keep O(log n) - an all-equal array with one different element hides that element from any comparison-based halving; adversary argument.
One-pass vs pivot-then-search - identical asymptotics; one-pass does fewer comparisons, two-pass isolates the two invariants and is easier to defend under pressure.
What if it was rotated multiple times - composition of rotations is a single rotation; the algorithm is unchanged.
canonical.mutation: The array was originally sorted either ascending or descending (unknown which), then rotated. Rederive the half-classification: what single extra comparison recovers the direction, and which range tests flip?

items.name: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

canonical.pattern: binary search for the rotation point (compare mid against right boundary)
canonical.key_idea: Compare a[mid] with a[hi]: a[mid] > a[hi] proves the drop (and the minimum) lies strictly right of mid; otherwise [mid, hi] is sorted and the minimum is at mid or left of it. The interval collapses onto the rotation point.
canonical.invariant: The minimum's index always stays in [lo, hi]: a[mid] > a[hi] certifies min in (mid, hi]; a[mid] <= a[hi] certifies min in [lo, mid].

progression.idea: Linear scan for the minimum, or scan for the unique index where a[i] > a[i+1]. Ignores the two-sorted-runs structure; O(n) where O(log n) exists.
lo = 0, hi = n-1; while lo < hi: if a[mid] > a[hi] then lo = mid + 1 (min is right of mid) else hi = mid (mid could be the min). Return a[lo]. No target check, no equality exit.

traps: Compare against a[hi], not a[lo]: on an unrotated array a[mid] > a[lo] holds yet the min is at lo - the left comparison cannot distinguish the two cases.
hi = mid, never mid - 1: mid itself may be the minimum, and excluding it can skip the answer.
Loop condition lo < hi with mid rounding down guarantees progress (hi = mid strictly shrinks since mid < hi); lo <= hi variants need an explicit exit.
Duplicates (variant II): a[mid] == a[hi] certifies nothing - decrement hi by one; worst case O(n).
followups: With duplicates - on a[mid] == a[hi] do hi--; the min survives in [lo, hi] and worst case is provably O(n).
Recover the rotation count k - it equals the index of the minimum returned by this search.
Find the maximum instead - it sits at (minIdx - 1 + n) % n, or run the mirrored search comparing against a[lo].
Why not compare a[mid] with a[lo] - the unrotated case and the rotated case give the same comparison result, so the branch cannot decide a side.
Use this to solve full search-in-rotated - find the min index in O(log n), then one standard binary search in the run that can contain the target.
canonical.mutation: The rotated array is then negated element-wise (every value multiplied by -1). Find the MAXIMUM of the result in O(log n): which boundary do you compare against now, and which branch inequalities flip?

canonical.pattern: binary search on partition (kth element of two sorted arrays)
aliases: binary search on the shorter array
canonical.key_idea: Binary-search the cut position i in the shorter array; the cut j in the other array is forced by i + j = (m+n+1)/2. A cut is valid when every left-side element is <= every right-side element, and the median then comes from the four border values only.
canonical.invariant: cut (i,j) with i + j = (m+n+1)/2 and A[i-1] <= B[j] and B[j-1] <= A[i] means the left halves together are exactly the smallest ceil((m+n)/2) elements

progression.idea: Merge both arrays fully, then index the middle element(s). Insufficient because the problem explicitly demands O(log(m+n)) and merging touches every element.
Two-pointer walk that advances only until reaching position (m+n)/2 without materializing the merged array. Still linear: it cannot skip elements, so the log bound is unreachable this way.

Binary search cut i on the shorter array A with j = (m+n+1)/2 - i in B. If A[i-1] > B[j] move hi left; if B[j-1] > A[i] move lo right; else the partition is valid and the answer is max(A[i-1],B[j-1]) for odd total, averaged with min(A[i],B[j]) for even. Use +/-infinity sentinels at i=0, i=m, j=0, j=n.

traps: Binary search must run on the SHORTER array, otherwise j = half - i can go negative or exceed n and index out of bounds
Use (m+n+1)/2 (the +1) so the odd case is handled by max-of-left alone; forgetting it breaks odd totals
Border sentinels: i=0 means left of A is -infinity, i=m means right of A is +infinity; missing these crashes on arrays that fully precede each other
Even/odd split: even total needs (maxLeft + minRight)/2.0 as double, not integer division
followups: Generalize to kth smallest of two sorted arrays - same cut search with i + j = k; answer is max(A[i-1], B[j-1])
Why does non-strict <= handle duplicates - the partition check never needs strict order, equal border values still form a valid cut
Median of k sorted arrays - binary search on the VALUE domain, counting elements <= mid across all arrays in O(k log n) per probe
Arrays are forward-iterator streams with no random access - the log bound dies; best is the O(m+n) two-pointer walk since binary search needs indexing
Can two-pointer beat the partition method in practice for tiny arrays - yes, branch-predictable linear scan wins under ~50 elements; know both
canonical.mutation: You now get THREE sorted arrays and must return the overall median in sub-linear total time. The i + j = half trick no longer pins the cuts - what do you binary-search on instead, and what is the new complexity?

canonical.pattern: sort by start + linear sweep merge
canonical.key_idea: After sorting by start, any interval that overlaps the current merged block must be its immediate successor, so one pass suffices: extend the block while next.start <= cur.end, otherwise flush it and start a new block.
canonical.invariant: after processing the first i sorted intervals, the output list is disjoint and sorted and covers exactly their union, and only the last block can still absorb future intervals

progression.idea: Build the overlap graph (test all pairs), find connected components with DSU/BFS, and merge each component into [min start, max end]. Correct, but quadratic pair testing ignores that sorting makes overlapping intervals adjacent.

Sort by start. Keep one open block; for each interval either absorb it (start <= block.end, so block.end = max(block.end, end)) or flush the block and open a new one. Push the final block after the loop.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space for output (O(log n) auxiliary for the sort)

traps: Touching intervals: with closed intervals [1,4] and [4,5] DO merge, so the test is next.start <= cur.end, not <
Containment: update with end = max(cur.end, next.end); writing end = next.end breaks on [1,10],[2,3]
Forgetting to flush the last open block after the loop drops the final merged interval
Sorting by end (or not sorting at all because input 'looks sorted') silently produces wrong merges
followups: Do it with O(1) auxiliary space - after the in-place sort, compact merged blocks back into the input array with a write pointer
Unsorted stream of inserts with live queries - keep a std::map keyed by start and merge each insert with floor/ceiling neighbors, O(log n + k) per insert
Total covered length instead of the list - same sweep, accumulate (end - start) at each flush
Billions of intervals on disk - external merge sort by start, then the sweep is a single streaming pass with O(1) state
Return how many original intervals each merged block absorbed - carry a counter in the open block, reset at flush
canonical.mutation: Merge only intervals that overlap by at least k units (next.start <= cur.end - k). Does one sorted sweep still work, is the 'mergeable' relation still safe to apply greedily left-to-right, and which line of the invariant changes?

canonical.pattern: three-phase linear scan into sorted disjoint intervals
canonical.key_idea: The input is already sorted and disjoint, so no sort is needed: emit every interval that ends before the new one starts, fold every overlapping interval into the new one with min/max, then emit the untouched tail.
canonical.invariant: during the scan, everything emitted lies strictly before the (possibly grown) new interval, and all overlapping intervals seen so far are folded into [min(starts), max(ends)]

progression.idea: Append newInterval and rerun full Merge Intervals (sort + sweep). Correct but throws away the given sorted-disjoint structure, paying an unnecessary log factor.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: Phase 1: copy intervals with end < newStart. Phase 2: while start <= newEnd, fold via newStart = min(newStart, start), newEnd = max(newEnd, end); then push the grown interval. Phase 3: copy the rest verbatim.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) space for output (O(1) auxiliary)

traps: Overlap test for closed intervals is start <= newEnd AND end >= newStart; [1,2] and [2,3] DO overlap - a strict < here is the classic off-by-one
If the array ends while still in phase 2, the grown interval must still be pushed - easy to fall off the loop without emitting it
Empty input array: the answer is just [newInterval]; phase logic must degrade to that
Fold with min/max as you consume; comparing against the ORIGINAL newInterval bounds after partial folding misses chains
followups: Delete an interval instead of insert - same scan, but each overlapping interval is clipped into up to two survivors (Remove Interval, LC 1272)
Many online inserts - std::map<start,end>: erase the overlapped run, insert one merged node, amortized O(log n + k) per insert
Can binary search improve it - lower_bound finds the first overlap in O(log n), but the merged run can span O(n) intervals, so worst case stays O(n)
Truly in-place in a vector - possible with a write pointer, but element shifting keeps it O(n); a linked list gives O(1) splice after locating the position
canonical.mutation: Intervals become half-open [start, end). Re-derive the overlap and merge conditions - which comparisons flip from <= to <, and give a concrete test case whose answer changes.

canonical.pattern: greedy by earliest end (activity selection)
canonical.key_idea: Minimizing removals equals maximizing the kept disjoint set. Sort by END and greedily keep every interval whose start is >= the last kept end: the earliest-ending choice leaves maximal room, proved by an exchange argument.
canonical.invariant: after scanning the sorted prefix, the kept set is a maximum disjoint subset of that prefix, and its last end is the minimum achievable over all such maximum subsets

progression.idea: Enumerate all 2^n keep/remove subsets and check pairwise disjointness of the kept set. Exponential - dead on arrival past n ~ 20.

Sort by start; dp[i] = length of the longest non-overlapping chain ending at interval i (LIS-style over compatible predecessors). Correct, but the pairwise transition is quadratic and TLEs at n = 10^5.

Sort by end. Keep a counter and lastEnd = -inf; for each interval, if start >= lastEnd, keep it and set lastEnd = end. Answer is n - kept. Exchange argument: any optimal solution's first interval can be swapped for the earliest-ending one without losing count.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(1) auxiliary space (beyond the sort)

traps: Sort by END, not start - sort-by-start greedy fails on [1,100],[2,3],[4,5] (keeps 1 instead of 2)
Touching intervals do NOT overlap here: keep when start >= lastEnd (this problem's convention differs from Merge Intervals)
Return removals (n - kept), not kept - trivially swapped under pressure
If you insist on sorting by start, you must instead keep the smaller end on conflict (drop the larger-end interval), a different but equivalent greedy
followups: Prove the greedy - exchange argument: replacing the first interval of any optimal solution with the earliest-ending compatible one never decreases the count
Weighted version (maximize total kept weight) - greedy breaks; weighted interval scheduling DP with binary search over p(i), O(n log n)
Output the removed intervals themselves - record every interval that fails the start >= lastEnd test during the sweep
Allow up to k overlaps at any point instead of 0 - sweep with a multiset of active ends; on exceeding k, evict the interval with the LARGEST end
Relation to Meeting Rooms I - attending all meetings is feasibility (zero removals needed), the degenerate case of this problem
canonical.mutation: Each interval now has a removal COST and you must minimize total cost removed (not count). Show a 3-interval instance where earliest-end greedy is suboptimal, then name the replacement formulation.

canonical.pattern: boundary-event sweep (min-heap of active end times)
canonical.key_idea: Rooms needed = maximum number of meetings simultaneously active. Sorting starts and ends separately (or holding active ends in a min-heap) turns that maximum into a linear scan over 2n boundary events.
canonical.invariant: the heap (or active counter) holds exactly the end times of meetings still running at the current event time; the peak of its size over the sweep is the answer

progression.idea: For each meeting, count how many other meetings overlap it and take the max clique naively - or evaluate concurrency at every start point against all intervals. Quadratic, and it also subtly under/overcounts unless done at start boundaries only.
Sort by start; min-heap of end times of running meetings. For each meeting, pop while heap.top() <= start (those rooms freed), push its end; track peak heap size. Peak = answer.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

progression.idea: Sort the starts array and ends array independently; two pointers walk them: on start, ++active (advance start ptr) unless the smallest unprocessed end is <= that start, in which case retire it first. Same asymptotics as the heap, smaller constant, and generalizes to a +1/-1 difference-array sweep.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

traps: Tie order: a meeting ending at t frees its room for one starting at t - process ends before starts on equal timestamps (heap: pop while top <= start), else you overcount by 1
The answer is the PEAK concurrency during the sweep, not the heap size at the end
Sweeping raw timestamps with a counter array explodes when times are large/sparse - sort boundaries or compress coordinates
Sorting intervals by end (instead of start) before the heap sweep breaks the freeing logic
followups: Assign an actual room number to each meeting - heap of (endTime, roomId) plus a min-heap of free room ids; reuse the freed id
Meeting Rooms I (can one person attend all) - sort by start, check adjacent pairs for overlap, O(n log n)
Repeated max-overlap queries over updates - difference array / BIT over compressed coordinates with a prefix-max query
Each room needs a cleaning gap g after every meeting - push end + g into the heap instead of end; nothing else changes
Streaming arrivals (no global sort) - ordered multiset of ends handles admission per meeting, but exact peak concurrency still requires a full sweep or an augmented interval structure
canonical.mutation: Rooms are no longer interchangeable: meeting i may only use rooms from a given subset S_i. Why does the counting argument (peak overlap = rooms) collapse, and what combinatorial model answers feasibility for k rooms?

items.name: Kth Largest Element in an Array

canonical.pattern: selection via quickselect / bounded min-heap
canonical.key_idea: You need one order statistic, not a total order. Partitioning around a pivot discards the entire side that cannot contain index n-k, giving expected linear time; a size-k min-heap trades a log k factor for streaming friendliness.
canonical.invariant: after each partition around pivot p, everything left of p is <= p and everything right is >= p, so exactly one side can contain index n-k and the other is discarded forever

progression.idea: Sort the whole array ascending and return a[n-k]. Does far more work than needed - a full sort resolves all n order statistics to get one.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(log n) space (introsort stack)

progression.idea: Min-heap capped at size k: push each element, pop the min when size exceeds k; the root ends as the kth largest. Great when n is a stream or k << n, but the log k per element is avoidable for in-memory arrays.
progression.complexity: O(n log k) time, O(k) space

progression.idea: Quickselect targeting index n-k (ascending order): partition around a RANDOM pivot, recurse/iterate only into the side containing the target index. Randomization makes adversarial inputs harmless; std::nth_element gives introselect with the same interface.
progression.complexity: expected O(n) time (worst O(n^2) without median-of-medians), O(1) space iterative

traps: kth largest = index n-k of the ASCENDING order (0-based) - the single most common off-by-one here
Fixed first/last pivot on sorted or reverse-sorted input degrades to O(n^2) - randomize the pivot or use nth_element
All-equal arrays make Lomuto partition degenerate to O(n^2); three-way (Dutch flag) partition or Hoare fixes it
Heap direction: kth LARGEST needs a MIN-heap of size k (evict the smallest), inverting this silently returns the kth smallest
followups: Guaranteed worst-case O(n) - median-of-medians (groups of 5) pivot selection; correct but the constant is large, which is why introselect is the practical default
Return all top-k elements - the size-k heap already holds them; after quickselect they sit (unordered) in the suffix a[n-k..n-1]
Streaming input, bounded memory - only the size-k min-heap survives, O(log k) per arrival
Data sharded across m machines - each machine sends its local top-k, merge k*m candidates centrally; or binary search on value with distributed rank counts
Values in a small known range - counting buckets give exact selection in O(n + range), beating all comparison approaches
canonical.mutation: The array becomes a sliding window: elements expire in arrival order and you must report the kth largest after every slide. Quickselect and a plain heap both fail on deletion - what structure gives O(log n) per slide?

canonical.pattern: k-way merge (min-heap of heads / divide-and-conquer pairwise)
canonical.key_idea: Only k nodes can possibly be the next global minimum - the current head of each list. A min-heap over those heads (or log k rounds of pairwise list merges) produces the full merge in O(N log k) instead of O(N log N).
canonical.invariant: the heap contains exactly the smallest unconsumed node of every non-exhausted list, so each pop emits the global minimum of everything not yet merged

progression.idea: Dump all N nodes into a vector, sort, and relink. Ignores that every list is already sorted, paying the full comparison sort.
progression.complexity: O(N log N) time, O(N) space

progression.idea: Merge lists one at a time into an accumulator (list1+list2, result+list3, ...). The accumulator grows and gets re-walked on every merge: with k equal lists the total is N + 2N/k*... summing to O(N*k).

Min-heap seeded with each non-empty head; pop the min, append to a dummy-headed result, push the popped node's successor. Alternative with identical asymptotics: divide and conquer - merge lists in pairs, halving k each round for log k rounds of O(N) total work, better cache behavior and no heap constant.
progression.complexity: O(N log k) time, O(k) space (heap) or O(log k) recursion (D&C)

traps: C++ priority_queue over ListNode* with the default comparator compares POINTER ADDRESSES - you must supply a comparator on ->val (and greater-than for a min-heap)
Null/empty lists in the input array must be skipped when seeding the heap or they crash the comparator
Forgetting the dummy head makes the first-node splice a special case riddled with null checks
In the one-by-one 'better' approach, people misreport O(N log k) - the accumulator re-walk makes it O(N*k); know why
followups: k sorted ARRAYS with an iterator API (hasNext/next) - heap of (value, arrayIdx, elemIdx); next() is one pop+push, O(log k)
Where does this appear in systems - it is exactly the merge phase of external merge sort; k is capped by the memory/page budget
Heap vs divide-and-conquer trade-off - same O(N log k); D&C wins on cache locality and constants, the heap wins when lists are streams you cannot re-walk
Millions of tiny lists - a loser tree (tournament) halves comparisons per emitted element versus a binary heap; standard in DB merge joins
Stability - if equal values must preserve list order, break heap ties by list index
canonical.mutation: You now need only the MEDIAN element of the union of the k sorted lists, not the merged output. Popping N/2 times is O(N log k) - can you beat O(N), and what do you binary-search on to get there?

canonical.pattern: two balanced heaps (max-heap lower half / min-heap upper half)
canonical.key_idea: Split the multiset into a max-heap 'lo' holding the smaller half and a min-heap 'hi' holding the larger half, sizes within 1 of each other. The median is always a heap top (or the average of both tops), and each insert costs a constant number of heap ops.
canonical.invariant: max(lo) <= min(hi) and |size(lo) - size(hi)| <= 1, with lo holding the extra element when the count is odd

progression.idea: Append every arrival to a vector and sort inside findMedian. Re-sorts everything per query - unusable once queries interleave with inserts.
progression.complexity: O(n log n) per findMedian, O(1) per addNum, O(n) space

progression.idea: Keep the vector sorted: binary-search the insertion point, then shift. Queries become O(1), but the memmove on every insert is linear and dominates on adversarial (sorted) streams.
progression.complexity: O(n) per addNum (O(log n) search + O(n) shift), O(1) per findMedian, O(n) space

progression.idea: Push each arrival into lo, then move lo's max into hi (this guarantees ordering); if hi outgrows lo, move hi's min back. Both halves stay ordered and balanced with at most 3 heap ops per insert; median reads the top(s).
progression.complexity: O(log n) per addNum, O(1) per findMedian, O(n) space

traps: Routing by 'push into the smaller heap' without comparing against tops breaks the ordering invariant - always push-then-rebalance through a top transfer
Averaging two ints for the even case: use doubles (or a safe sum) - (a+b)/2 in int truncates and can overflow at extremes
C++ heap directions: priority_queue is a MAX-heap by default; hi needs greater<> - flipping one silently corrupts medians
Rebalance must restore the size gap to <= 1 after EVERY insert; letting it hit 2 before fixing gives stale medians on interleaved queries
followups: Sliding-window median - heaps cannot erase middles; use two multisets (or heaps with lazy deletion and real-size counters), LC 480
Values bounded in a small range like 0..100 - counting buckets: O(1) insert, median by maintained pointer or O(range) walk; beats heaps outright
Arbitrary quantile p instead of the median - same two-heap machinery with sizes maintained at ratio p : 1-p
Billions of elements, bounded memory, approximate ok - quantile sketches (t-digest, KLL/GK) with tunable error; exact medians need Omega(n) space
Why not one order-statistics tree - it works at O(log n) insert + select, but two heaps have smaller constants and far less code
canonical.mutation: The stream now also issues delete(x) for arbitrary previously-inserted values. Binary heaps cannot remove interior elements - redesign for O(log n) per operation and state the new balance invariant.

canonical.pattern: hash map + doubly linked list (O(1) recency order)
canonical.key_idea: A doubly linked list maintains recency order with O(1) unlink/relink, and a hash map gives O(1) key-to-node access. Every get/put splices the touched node to the head; eviction unlinks the tail and erases its key from the map.
canonical.invariant: the list is exactly recency order (front = most recently used, back = LRU) and the map holds key->node for every live node - both structures agree on membership at all times

progression.idea: Vector of (key, value, lastUsed timestamp); get scans linearly, eviction scans for the minimum timestamp. Every operation is linear in capacity - fails the required O(1).

Hash map key->entry plus a balanced BST (or std::map) keyed on last-use timestamp for eviction order. O(log n) per op - the log comes only from keeping a SORTED recency order, which is overkill: you only ever need the two ends.
progression.complexity: O(log capacity) per get/put, O(capacity) space

progression.idea: std::list<pair<key,value>> in recency order plus unordered_map<key, list::iterator>. get: map lookup, splice node to front, return value. put: overwrite+splice if present; else if full, erase map entry for back node (the node stores its key precisely for this) and pop it, then push front and record the iterator. Sentinel head/tail (or std::list itself) removes all null checks.
progression.complexity: O(1) average per get/put, O(capacity) space

traps: put on an EXISTING key must update the value AND move the node to front - forgetting the move is the classic bug that passes small tests
Eviction must erase the map entry too; the list node stores its key precisely so the tail can be removed from the map - store only values and you cannot evict correctly
Overwriting an existing key must NOT trigger eviction (size unchanged); evict only when inserting a genuinely new key at full capacity
C++: std::list::splice preserves iterator validity so stored map iterators survive the move-to-front; hand-rolled node swaps that copy payloads invalidate that assumption
followups: Thread safety - single mutex first; then sharded/striped locks, noting the recency list is the contention hotspot (real caches batch or approximate the promotions)
LFU instead of LRU - map key->node plus freq->DLL buckets and a minFreq pointer gives O(1) (LC 460); explain why one list is no longer enough
TTL expiry - store a deadline per node, lazily drop expired entries on access, plus a background sweep or a min-heap of deadlines
Why Java solves this in 10 lines - LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true IS map+DLL; override removeEldestEntry for eviction
How Redis approximates LRU - samples k random keys and evicts the stalest, avoiding a global list and its cache-line contention
canonical.mutation: Capacity is now a BYTE budget and values have wildly different sizes, so one put may evict many entries (and an oversized value may be uncacheable). Rewrite the eviction loop and the invariant - what replaces 'size <= capacity'?

canonical.pattern: in-place linked-list block reversal (k-group pointer surgery)
canonical.key_idea: Advance k nodes to confirm a full group exists, reverse exactly those k nodes with the standard 3-pointer iterative reversal, then splice: previous group's tail points to the new group head, and the old group head (now the tail) becomes the anchor for the next group.
canonical.invariant: After each round, every node up to and including groupPrev is in its final position, and groupPrev is the tail of the last fully reversed block.

progression.idea: Copy node values into a vector, reverse each k-sized chunk of values, write values back into the list in one pass. Insufficient: interviewers require actual pointer rewiring with O(1) extra space, and value-swapping is disallowed when nodes carry identity (e.g. payloads you cannot copy).

Recursive: walk k ahead; if a full group exists, reverse the first k nodes, then set the old head's next to the recursive result on the rest. Clean, but the recursion depth is n/k, so a long list with small k risks stack overflow and it is not O(1) auxiliary space.

Iterative with a dummy pre-head. Keep groupPrev (tail of the last finished group). Each round: probe k steps to find the kth node (bail if fewer remain), save groupNext = kth->next, reverse the k nodes with prev initialized to groupNext so the reversed block is already spliced forward, then wire groupPrev->next to kth and advance groupPrev to the old group head.
progression.complexity: O(n) time (each node visited twice: probe + reverse), O(1) space

traps: A leftover tail of fewer than k nodes must remain in original order - you must count k nodes BEFORE reversing, not discover the shortfall mid-reversal.
After reversal the old group head is the new tail: forgetting to move groupPrev onto it detaches or scrambles the next splice.
Save kth->next before reversing; the reversal destroys it and you lose the rest of the list.
The list head changes (it becomes the kth node), so return dummy.next, never the original head.
followups: Can you do it recursively and what is the cost - yes, same O(n) time but O(n/k) call stack, which the iterative version avoids.
Reverse the final short group too - drop the count-check bail-out and reverse unconditionally; one-branch change.
Reverse only alternate k-groups - after splicing a reversed group, walk k plain steps to skip a group before the next round.
How many times is each node touched - twice (probe pass + reversal pass), so it is still a constant-factor single sweep.
Doubly linked list version - also swap prev pointers during reversal, but splicing gets simpler because tails are addressable.
canonical.mutation: You are given an array g[] of group sizes (summing to at most n) instead of a fixed k - reverse the i-th block of size g[i] in one pass with O(1) extra space. What part of your loop structure survives and what must be re-derived?

canonical.pattern: linked-list composite: fast/slow middle split + in-place reverse + interleaved merge
canonical.key_idea: L0,Ln,L1,Ln-1,... is exactly the first half interleaved with the reversed second half; so find the middle with fast/slow pointers, reverse the back half in place, and zip the two halves alternately.
canonical.invariant: During the merge, every node strictly before the current 'first' cursor is already in its final interleaved position, and both remaining sublists are internally intact.

progression.idea: Load all node pointers into a vector, then two indices i from the front and j from the back rewire next pointers alternately until they meet. Correct and O(n), but uses linear extra memory, which the standard constraint forbids.

Three phases, all in place: (1) slow/fast to locate the middle; (2) cut the list there and reverse the second half iteratively; (3) merge: alternately take one node from the front half and one from the reversed back half, saving each next pointer before overwriting it.

traps: Terminate the first half with nullptr after the split - skipping this creates a cycle that the merge silently preserves.
Odd length: decide which half owns the middle node (fast = head->next start gives first half the extra node) and make the merge loop condition match that choice.
Save first->next AND second->next before any rewiring in the zip step; overwriting first->next first orphans the rest of the front half.
The merge must stop when the second (reversed) half runs out - it is the shorter-or-equal half by construction.
followups: How do you prove no cycle remains - run Floyd's cycle detection after, or argue each node's next is assigned exactly once in the merge.
Could you use a deque of pointers instead - yes, pop front/back alternately, O(n) space for much simpler code; state the tradeoff.
Generalize to L0,Ln,Ln-1,L1,... taking two from the back - the merge ratio changes; middle split point must shift to 1/3.
Recursive solution - possible by recursing to the ends, but O(n) stack defeats the purpose.
How do you test it - odd and even lengths, length 1 and 2, and assert the final node's next is null.
canonical.mutation: Reorder instead as L0,L2,L4,...,L5,L3,L1 (even indices forward, then odd indices backward) in O(1) space - the fast/slow split no longer applies; which decomposition replaces it?

canonical.pattern: tree DP via post-order (local downward gain vs global bent-path answer)
canonical.key_idea: For each node, the best path that bends at it is node->val + max(0, leftGain) + max(0, rightGain); but the value returned upward may include at most ONE child arm, because a path entering from the parent cannot fork. Track the bent-path maximum globally while returning the single-arm gain.
canonical.invariant: dfs(node) returns the maximum sum of a path that STARTS at node and descends through at most one child; the global answer is the max over all nodes of node->val + clampedLeftGain + clampedRightGain.

progression.idea: For every node treated as the path's highest point, run fresh DFS computations of the best downward sum in its left and right subtrees, combine, take the max over all nodes. Insufficient: each subtree's downward gain is recomputed for every ancestor, giving quadratic work on skewed trees.
progression.complexity: O(n^2) time worst case, O(h) space

progression.idea: Single post-order pass. dfs(node) computes leftGain = max(0, dfs(left)) and rightGain = max(0, dfs(right)), updates the global answer with node->val + leftGain + rightGain, and returns node->val + max(leftGain, rightGain). Each node is combined exactly once, when the recursion is at its bend point.

traps: Clamping child gains at 0 is correct for extension, but the global answer must be initialized to -infinity (or the root value), not 0 - an all-negative tree's answer is its largest single node.
Never return the two-arm (bent) sum up the recursion - a parent path cannot traverse both children; this is THE classic bug here.
A path need not pass through the root and need not end at a leaf - any node-to-node downward-bent path counts, including a single node.
With 10^4+ nodes and values up to 10^9-scale in variants, accumulate in long long (int64) to avoid overflow.
followups: Return the actual path, not just the sum - remember the argmax bend node during the pass, then re-walk greedily down the arms whose gains were positive.
What if the path must go leaf-to-leaf - stop clamping at 0; require both arms to exist and return leaf-anchored gains only.
Iterative version - explicit-stack post-order with a gain map; needed if h can blow the call stack.
Complexity and why one pass suffices - O(n)/O(h); every path has a unique highest bend node, and each node is evaluated as bend exactly once.
Max path sum limited to at most k nodes - per-node DP arrays over arm lengths; cost grows to O(n*k).
canonical.mutation: Change sum to PRODUCT with negative values allowed - maximizing greedily breaks because two negatives multiply positive; what pair of quantities must each recursive call now return?
