canonical.pattern: self-delimiting tree encoding via preorder DFS with null sentinels
canonical.key_idea: Preorder traversal that writes an explicit sentinel (e.g. '#') for every null child produces a token stream a recursive reader can decode uniquely with no lengths or indices: read one token, if sentinel return null, otherwise create the node and recursively decode left then right.
canonical.invariant: Decode invariant: the token cursor always points to the start of the serialization of the next subtree to build, and every recursive call consumes exactly its subtree's tokens before returning.

progression.idea: Store two traversals (preorder + inorder) and rebuild with the classic reconstruction. Insufficient: reconstruction breaks with duplicate values, costs extra passes and hash maps, and doubles the encoding size.
progression.complexity: O(n) time with an O(n) index map, O(n) space; incorrect under duplicates

progression.idea: BFS level-order with null markers (the LeetCode display format): encode via queue, decode by pairing each dequeued parent with the next two tokens. Works and is iterative, but carries a queue plus many trailing null tokens, and the pairing logic is fiddlier than the recursive consumer.

Preorder with null sentinels and a streaming tokenizer (istringstream or an index cursor). Encode: dfs writes 'val,' or '#,'. Decode: a recursive function consumes exactly the tokens of its own subtree, so no global bookkeeping is needed - the cursor position IS the state.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) output string, O(h) recursion space

traps: Values need a delimiter - multi-digit and negative numbers make '12,3' vs '1,23' ambiguous without one; single-char assumptions fail instantly.
Building the string with repeated operator+ in a loop is O(n^2); use ostringstream / a growing buffer.
Two-traversal reconstruction silently corrupts trees with duplicate values - state this if you propose it.
Deep skewed trees can overflow the decode recursion; mention an explicit stack fallback.
followups: The tree is a BST - drop the null sentinels entirely; preorder plus (min,max) bounds during decode reconstructs it, roughly halving the encoding.
N-ary tree - write each node's child count (or a closing sentinel per node) since 'left/right' no longer delimits.
Minimize bytes - separate structure from data: 2 bits per node for child presence, then values as fixed-width binary.
Can decode be iterative - yes, a stack of pending parents with a 'which child is next' flag.
Stream too large for memory - preorder-with-sentinels decodes incrementally from a file cursor; no lookahead needed.
canonical.mutation: Serialize a BST (values fit in 4 bytes) into the provably minimum-size encoding you can decode in O(n) - which redundancy that the general-tree codec pays for can you now delete, and what replaces it at decode time?

items.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree

canonical.pattern: single-pass post-order LCA (deepest split point of two targets)
canonical.key_idea: DFS that returns a target node the moment it sees p or q, and otherwise returns whatever its children found: if BOTH children return non-null, the current node is where the targets split - the LCA. Since a node counts as its own ancestor, returning early at p or q is safe when both are guaranteed present.
canonical.invariant: dfs(x) returns: the LCA if subtree(x) contains both targets, the target itself if it contains exactly one, and null if it contains neither.

progression.idea: Compute the root-to-p path and root-to-q path with two separate DFS searches storing the path, then walk both paths in lockstep and report the last common node. Insufficient: two/three traversals, O(n) path storage, and clumsy to extend.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) space for the stored paths

progression.idea: One DFS to record parent pointers into a hash map, then climb from p inserting ancestors into a set, then climb from q until the first ancestor already in the set. Insufficient only in elegance and memory: the hash structures cost O(n) and it is two phases.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(n) space (parent map + ancestor set)

progression.idea: Single post-order pass: dfs(x) returns x if x is null, p, or q; otherwise recurse both sides; if both sides are non-null return x (the split), else return whichever side is non-null. The first node where the answers from both subtrees are non-null is the deepest common ancestor.

traps: Early-returning at p or q without exploring below it is only correct because both nodes are guaranteed to exist - one target may be in the other's subtree and that is exactly the ancestor-of-itself case.
Do not port the BST version's value comparisons here - a general binary tree has no ordering to exploit.
If both children return non-null, the CURRENT node is the answer even if a target was found at the node itself higher up - trust the propagation, do not add special cases.
Comparing by node pointer, not by value - duplicate values are legal in a general tree.
followups: p or q might not exist - one pass returning (node, countFound); only report an LCA when countFound == 2.
It is a BST - walk from the root: both values smaller go left, both larger go right, else current node is the LCA, O(h) iterative.
Nodes have parent pointers - measure both depths, lift the deeper one, then advance in lockstep; O(h) time O(1) space, same as linked-list intersection.
LCA of k given nodes - same recursion returning any found target; the first node whose subtrees cover all k (track a count) is the answer.
Millions of queries on a static tree - preprocess with Euler tour + sparse-table RMQ (O(n log n) build, O(1) query) or binary lifting (O(log n) query).
canonical.mutation: p and q are given as VALUES that may be absent from the tree, and you must return null unless both exist - still one pass. Why does the early-return-at-target trick now give wrong answers, and what does the recursion have to return instead?

canonical.pattern: BST validation via propagated open-interval bounds (equivalently strict inorder monotonicity)
canonical.key_idea: A tree is a BST iff every node lies strictly inside the open interval inherited from ALL its ancestors: the root gets (-inf, +inf), going left replaces the upper bound with the parent's value, going right replaces the lower bound. Equivalently, its inorder sequence must be strictly increasing.
canonical.invariant: At every visited node, low < node->val < high where (low, high) is the intersection of all ancestor constraints; in the inorder variant, prev < curr at every step.

progression.idea: For each node, compute the maximum of its left subtree and the minimum of its right subtree and check max < node < min, recursing everywhere. Insufficient: subtree extrema are recomputed for every ancestor, degrading to quadratic on skewed trees.
progression.complexity: O(n^2) time worst case, O(h) space

progression.idea: Inorder-traverse into a vector, then scan once checking a[i-1] < a[i]. Correct because inorder of a BST is strictly sorted, but stores the whole traversal when only the previous element is ever needed.

Either bounds DFS passing (low, high) down and checking low < val < high at each node, or inorder with a single 'prev' value/pointer carried across the traversal. Morris threading makes the inorder variant O(1) auxiliary space if pressed.
progression.complexity: O(n) time, O(h) space (O(1) with Morris)

traps: Checking each node only against its immediate parent accepts invalid trees - a right-grandchild can violate the grandparent's bound; ancestors' constraints must propagate.
Equal values must FAIL: the inequalities are strict on both sides.
Node values can be INT_MIN/INT_MAX, so int bounds break - use long long sentinels or pointer/optional bounds.
The inorder 'prev' must persist across recursive calls (member/reference), not be a by-value parameter.
followups: Exactly two nodes were swapped in a BST; fix it - inorder pass finds one or two inversions; swap the first inversion's left element with the last inversion's right element.
Validate in O(1) auxiliary space - Morris inorder threading, restoring links as you go.
Find the largest BST subtree in a general tree - post-order returning (min, max, size, isBST) tuples per subtree.
Count how many subtrees are valid BSTs - same tuple recursion, increment on each isBST.
Why is 'strictly increasing inorder' equivalent to the bounds definition - both are equivalent to every-ancestor constraints; prove by induction on the traversal order.
canonical.mutation: You are given only the PREORDER value sequence of a claimed BST and must validate it in O(n) without building the tree - what single running lower bound plus a stack reproduces the interval logic?

canonical.pattern: trie / prefix tree (character-indexed child pointers + terminal flags)
canonical.key_idea: Each node holds an array (or map) of child pointers keyed by character plus an isEnd flag; every operation walks one edge per character of the argument, so insert/search/startsWith cost O(L) regardless of how many words are stored - the structure shares prefixes instead of comparing them.
canonical.invariant: The character path from the root to any node spells exactly one prefix, and isEnd is true precisely on nodes whose root-path was inserted as a complete word.

progression.idea: Keep all words in a hash set: insert and exact search are O(L) expected, but startsWith must scan every stored word and compare prefixes. Insufficient: prefix queries cost O(N*L) and hashing gives no prefix sharing.
progression.complexity: O(L) insert/search expected, O(N*L) startsWith; O(total chars) space

progression.idea: Keep words in a sorted container; startsWith becomes lower_bound + prefix compare, search is binary search. Insufficient: insert is O(N) for a sorted vector (or O(L log N) comparisons in a tree with long common prefixes re-compared every level).
progression.complexity: O(L log N) query, O(N) worst insert, O(total chars) space

progression.idea: Trie: from the root, follow/create one child per character. insert allocates missing children and sets isEnd on the last node; search walks and requires isEnd true at the end; startsWith walks and only requires the path to exist. All three are single loops over the argument.
progression.complexity: O(L) per operation, O(ALPHABET * nodeCount) space (array children)

traps: search and startsWith differ only in the final isEnd check - omit it and searching 'app' succeeds after inserting only 'apple'.
Array-of-26 children waste memory on sparse tries; know when to switch to a hash-map child table or a compressed (radix) trie.
On insert, allocate the child BEFORE descending - dereferencing a null slot is the classic segfault here.
In C++ with raw pointers, the trie leaks or double-frees without a recursive destructor or unique_ptr children - say it out loud.
followups: Support delete(word) - unset isEnd, then recursively free/unlink childless nodes with isEnd false on the way back up.
Support '.' wildcard search - DFS that branches over all children on a dot (Word Dictionary problem), worst case O(26^dots * L).
Cut memory dramatically - radix/Patricia compression (merge single-child chains) or a DAWG to share suffixes.
countWordsWithPrefix(p) in O(|p|) - maintain a passCount per node, incremented on insert along the path.
Unicode input - fixed arrays die; use hash-map children keyed by code point, or serialize to bytes and key on bytes.
canonical.mutation: Add getKth(k): return the k-th lexicographically smallest word currently stored, in O(L * ALPHABET) - what per-node counter do you have to maintain on insert/delete, and how does the descent use it?

canonical.pattern: grid DFS backtracking (in-place mark/undo path search)
canonical.key_idea: From every cell equal to word[0], DFS that matches one character per step in the 4 directions, overwriting the current cell with a sentinel ('#') so the path cannot reuse it, and restoring the letter on return - the board itself is the visited set.
canonical.invariant: On entry to dfs(r, c, i), the cells consumed for word[0..i-1] form a valid adjacent path and are all sentinel-marked; on every return path the board is restored to exactly its original state.

progression.idea: DFS over all simple paths carrying a separate visited set, copied or rebuilt per branch. Correct but the per-branch set copies add an O(L) (or hashing) factor and memory churn on top of the exponential search - it TLEs where in-place marking passes.
progression.complexity: O(m*n * 3^(L-1) * L) time, O(L) space per active path plus copies

progression.idea: In-place marking: overwrite board[r][c] before recursing, restore after. Removes the visited-set cost entirely. Still insufficient on adversarial inputs like an all-'a' board with word 'aaaa...b', where the search wastes time on doomed starts.

Same DFS plus cheap pruning: (1) count letter frequencies - if the word needs more of some letter than the board has, return false immediately; (2) if the word's last letter is rarer than its first, search the reversed word so DFS starts from the rarer end and dies faster. Same worst-case bound, orders-of-magnitude fewer explored nodes in practice.
progression.complexity: O(m*n * 3^(L-1)) worst-case time, O(L + alphabet) space

traps: Forgetting to restore the cell on backtrack silently poisons sibling branches - the single most common bug.
Check i == L (success) BEFORE the bounds/mismatch checks, or a word ending at a border cell is missed.
Mark the starting cell too - the path may otherwise curl back and reuse cell 0.
The branching factor is 3, not 4, after the first step (you never step back onto your parent) - quote 3^(L-1) and know why.
followups: Search 10^5 words at once (Word Search II) - build a trie of the words and run ONE board DFS descending the trie, pruning dead branches and removing found words.
Cells may be reused - existence becomes reachability in the (cell, index) state graph: BFS/DFS over m*n*L states, polynomial not exponential.
Count all occurrences instead of exists - remove the early-exit true propagation; define whether overlapping paths count separately.
Make it iterative - explicit stack of (r, c, i, nextDirection) frames so you can undo marks when a frame is popped exhausted.
Parallelize - starting cells are independent; shard them across threads with per-thread board copies or immutable visited bitmasks.
canonical.mutation: The path may now revisit cells (no visited constraint at all) - the backtracking argument for termination evaporates. What state space makes the problem finite again, and what is the new complexity?

canonical.pattern: backtracking with reuse (start-index enumeration of unbounded multisets)
canonical.key_idea: DFS carrying a start index i and the remaining target: at each step either take candidates[i] again (recurse with the SAME i, remaining - candidates[i]) or move past it (i+1). Fixing the index order means each multiset is generated exactly once - no used-set, no dedup pass.
canonical.invariant: Every recursion path is a multiset whose candidate indices are non-decreasing, and remaining = target - sum(path) is >= 0 on entry to every call.

progression.idea: Enumerate all sequences of candidates whose sum is target (choosing any candidate at every step), then canonicalize each solution (sort it) and dedup through a hash set. Insufficient: every k-element combination is generated up to k! times and the dedup set costs hashing and memory.
progression.complexity: O(N^(T/M) * k! * k) time-ish with duplication blowup, O(#solutions * k) space

progression.idea: Start-index backtracking without sorting: recurse (i, remaining - c[i]) to reuse, (i+1, remaining) to skip; emit when remaining == 0, abandon when remaining < 0. Correct and duplicate-free by construction, but explores branches that are already hopeless because larger candidates are not detected early.
progression.complexity: O(N^(T/M)) time (T = target, M = min candidate), O(T/M) recursion depth

progression.idea: Sort candidates ascending first, iterate choices at each level, and BREAK out of the level loop the moment candidates[j] > remaining - every later candidate is even larger. Same asymptotic bound but the sorted-break prune kills entire sibling subtrees at once.
progression.complexity: O(N log N + N^(T/M)) time, O(T/M) recursion depth + output

traps: Reuse means recursing with i, not i+1 - passing i+1 silently turns it into the each-used-once variant (Combination Sum II).
The break-on-too-big prune is only valid AFTER sorting; unsorted, it must be a continue or you drop valid answers.
push_back before recursing must pair with pop_back after - a missed pop corrupts every sibling path.
Enumerating is inherently exponential; if the interviewer shifts to COUNTING solutions, stop backtracking and switch to DP - do not count by enumerating.
followups: Candidates contain duplicates, each usable once (Combination Sum II) - sort, recurse with i+1, and skip equal values at the same depth (j > i && c[j] == c[j-1]).
Count combinations only - dp[t] += dp[t - c] with the candidate loop OUTER over the target loop, O(N*T) time, so orderings are not double-counted.
Count ordered sequences (Combination Sum IV) - swap the loops: target outer, candidates inner; explain why loop order alone flips the semantics.
Exactly k numbers from 1-9, each once (Combination Sum III) - depth is fixed at k, search space is tiny; same skeleton with both a count and a sum constraint.
What bounds the recursion depth - T/M, the target divided by the smallest candidate; that is also the longest possible solution.
canonical.mutation: Candidates may now be NEGATIVE - the recursion no longer terminates and 'remaining < 0' stops pruning. What extra constraint must the interviewer grant to make the answer set finite, and how does the state you search over change?
